structure.rationale: The episode meanders from John Cena's hair transplant into Supreme Court commentary without a clear transition, and the discussion drifts loosely between transgender sports, gay marriage history, and bathroom policy with no discernible segment structure.

relevance.rationale: The episode directly engages a specific Supreme Court ruling on transgender issues and related state-vs-federal jurisdiction questions, making it substantively on-topic for News/Politics, though the opening segment is off-topic celebrity chatter.

clipability.rationale: The slippery-slope argument and the 'we have to differentiate' passage have some standalone potential, but most moments require surrounding context or are embedded in rambling tangents that undercut clean extraction.

card.tagline: Supreme Court's transgender ruling: what it means for every state's laws
card.description: Chuck Douglas breaks down the Supreme Court's transgender decision and argues that erasing gender distinctions threatens the legal foundation of civil rights legislation nationwide.
card.reason: Because this episode covers a major Supreme Court ruling on gender and identity

scored.feedTitle: The 405 Coffee Break with O.K. Solberg
scored.description:
Greetings and Welcome to The 405 Coffee Break with O.K. Solberg

New episodes tend to air over the local KMMR radio station @ 5 minutes past 4PM each M-F. And have been doing so, nearly every week since Sept 2018.

I'm D.J. Rasmussen, O.K.s friend since junior high, possibly your neighbor & this websites maintainer, whom strives to get each episode's show notes written, the transcript proofed and the audio posted to the internet within a few hours of that days KMMR air time. NOTE: recently been publishing most new releases by 4:30PM.

Thanks for visiting and I hope you enjoy the time we can spend together.

latestEpisode.title: The 405 Aired July 10th, 2026

transcript.scoringWindow: WEBVTT 00:00:00.000 --> 00:00:09.200 I wanna again welcome you to The 405 Coffee Break. Guys, get a tall cool glass of lemonade and find a shady spot. Let's see what's happening out there. 00:00:09.840 --> 00:00:28.955 Spring wheat $5.63 a bushel. 550lb steer calf $5.35 on the top end if they're shiny, and a 100lb fat lamb in Billings $3.35 a pound. But, guys, there's more, much more. 00:00:28.955 --> 00:00:55.705 Okay. Okay. You know, I like to read online, and I found a story online like no other. No other that you or I have ever heard. Now it must have been a creative writing class. I wished I was in it. And the exercise was to write a story about nothing. Well, as soon as I read that, of course, I thought of the TV show Seinfeld. Yeah. It's a show about nothing. 00:00:57.120 --> 00:01:15.255 I had to read the creative story and I'd like to share it with you. Now before you hear it, I have to say I did not write it. I wish I had. I did not write it. But here it is, and believe it or not, it has a bible verse tucked neatly inside. 00:01:15.255 --> 00:01:35.800 It was like a Orvin, it's a win win! The story is titled The rock, the gum, and the dominion. Some mornings begin with great purpose. Others begin because the alarm clock simply refuses to negotiate. 00:01:36.365 --> 00:01:57.900 This was one of those mornings. I was walking down an old gravel road when I noticed a perfectly ordinary rock. Not a famous rock, not a valuable rock, just the kind of rock that has spent its entire career being stepped on without receiving so much as a thank you. 00:01:59.100 --> 00:02:23.850 Next to it lay a stick of juicy fruit gum. Now I don't know who leaves perfectly good chewing gum beside a perfectly ordinary rock, but I immediately became suspicious. You should never trust a rock that's keeping company with chewing gum. The rock looked dependable. The gum looked optimistic. 00:02:24.330 --> 00:02:47.855 Together, they looked unemployed. Just then a man came along carrying what he proudly announced was a dominion. What? I had no idea what he meant. A a dominion. Was it a kingdom, a board game, a title deed, perhaps just a confident chicken. 00:02:47.935 --> 00:03:07.145 I asked him, what is it? He smiled and said, that's exactly the right question. Then he walked away before answering it. I've met politicians who were less mysterious. The gum meanwhile had somehow disappeared. The rock hadn't moved an inch. 00:03:07.145 --> 00:03:33.105 I suppose that's why we trust rocks more than chewing gum. One stays put. The other loses its flavor on the bedpost overnight. As I stood there pondering these deep philosophical matters, a squirrel ran by carrying what appeared to be a half a peanut and all the confidence in the world. He glanced at me as if to say, you don't have a schedule, do you? 00:03:33.905 --> 00:03:48.490 I admitted he was probably right. Then a breeze came through the trees. The leaves applauded. The grass bowed politely. The rock continued its lifelong commitment to remaining being a rock. 00:03:48.810 --> 00:04:05.715 I picked it up. It wasn't any smarter in my hand than it had been on the ground. That was comforting somehow. Not everything has to be something spectacular. Some things simply succeed by being exactly what they are. 00:04:06.435 --> 00:04:30.995 About then I remembered a verse tucked away near the beginning of the bible. This is the day that the lord has made. Let us rejoice and be glad in it. Psalm 118:24 It doesn't mention rocks. It never said a thing about juicy fruit gum or mysterious dominions, but perhaps that's the point. 00:04:31.795 --> 00:04:53.730 Not every day needs to solve the world's problems. Some days are simply invitations to smile at ordinary things, to laugh at a conversation that never really happened, to wonder why squirrels always seem to know where they're going, and to appreciate that even a meaningless story can brighten a meaningful day. 00:04:54.130 --> 00:05:16.480 As for the rock, I put it back where I found it. Someone else may need it for a story about absolutely nothing someday. Thy end, I read that story and I laughed. I didn't write it, but I sure wish I had. The weekend's near. Doc is at the controls. Let's rock and roll. 00:05:16.960 --> 00:05:23.440 So until next time, as you go out there, remember now, don't be bitter.

structure.rationale: The episode has a loose open-and-close framing but transitions abruptly from commodity prices to an extended creative story with no clear connective tissue or segmentation.

relevance.rationale: This episode is a commodity price report and lighthearted storytelling segment with no substantive connection to news or political events.

clipability.rationale: The philosophical rock-and-gum story has a few quotable lines but the humor is cumulative and depends on the full narrative arc to land, limiting standalone clip utility.

card.tagline: Wheat prices, a rock, some gum, and a squirrel with a plan
card.description: O.K. Solberg opens with commodity prices then shares a whimsical creative-writing story about a rock, chewing gum, and a mysterious dominion — with a Psalm tucked inside.
card.reason: Because this episode offers a lighthearted rural break from the news cycle

structure.rationale: Conversational but follows a clear throughline moving from convention energy to Black voter outreach to Crockett's absence to party chair dynamics, though transitions are loose and pacing is uneven.

relevance.rationale: Directly covers the Texas Democratic Party convention, a live intraparty unity fight between Talerico and Crockett, and a contested party chair re-election — all timely, specific Texas political developments.

clipability.rationale: The Crockett-as-afterthought exchange and the Cornyn/Crockett 'sour grapes' parallel are quotable standalone moments, but most insights require the surrounding conversation to land fully.

card.tagline: Texas Dems claim unity — but Jasmine Crockett wasn't even invited
card.description: TribCast breaks down the Texas Democratic convention: Talerico's Black voter problem, Crockett's conspicuous absence, and a party chair who won 75% despite internal revolt.
card.reason: Because this episode covers the Texas Democratic Party's biggest internal fault lines right now

scored.description:
The Diddy Diaries: The Downfall of Sean Combs is a raw, unflinching look at the dramatic collapse of one of hip-hop’s biggest moguls. For decades, Sean 'Diddy' Combs seemed untouchable—a figure who rose from the streets to become a titan of the music, fashion, and entertainment industries. His Bad Boy Entertainment label defined a generation of hip-hop, and his entrepreneurial spirit made him a household name. But beneath the surface of this glittering success lay darker currents—currents that have now surged into the spotlight as the empire he built threatens to crumble beneath the weight of scandal and serious criminal allegations.

This podcast dives deep into the allegations that have sent shockwaves through the entertainment world. At the center of it all are the accusers—those who have come forward with shocking claims of misconduct, manipulation, and abuse. These aren’t just sensational headlines—they are stories that expose a side of Sean Combs the public never saw. The Diddy Diaries explores how these accusations began to surface, what drove his accusers to finally speak out, and how the legal system has responded.

But this isn’t just about Diddy’s public image taking a hit—The Diddy Diaries zeroes in on the high-stakes battle that Diddy now faces to stay out of jail. Each episode unpacks the legal drama as it unfolds in real-time: the charges, the court proceedings, and the fight for his freedom. Once a man who had it all—fame, fortune, and influence—Diddy now stands at the center of a legal storm that threatens to strip him of everything, including his freedom.


We trace the events that led up to this moment, looking at how Diddy’s larger-than-life persona masked a pattern of behavior that would ultimately bring him to this point. Listeners will get a front-row seat to the high-stakes legal battles, where the possibility of incarceration looms large. The tension is palpable as Diddy’s legal team works to combat the mounting evidence against him, while his accusers push for justice.

Through riveting storytelling, The Diddy Diaries paints a full picture of Diddy’s downfall, from the heights of his stardom to the depths of his legal fight for survival. This is not a story about maintaining power or influence—it’s a fight to avoid the ultimate consequence: prison. As the allegations mount and the legal drama intensifies, The Diddy Diaries delivers a compelling narrative of a man grappling with the very real possibility of losing everything he once held dear.

With every episode, listeners are drawn deeper into this unfolding saga of celebrity, power, and justice. The Diddy Diaries not only explores the high-profile case but also delves into the human cost behind the headlines. As Diddy’s world unravels, so too does the carefully constructed image he spent decades building. This podcast is a must-listen for anyone who wants to understand the full scope of Sean Combs’ downfall, where the stakes couldn’t be higher and the outcome remains uncertain.

Prepare for a rollercoaster ride through the most shocking and consequential scandal in recent entertainment history. Tune in to The Diddy Diaries: The Downfall of Sean Combs to witness the dramatic fall of a man who once ruled the music world but now fights for his freedom in the face of life-altering allegations.

structure.rationale: The episode meanders between foreign policy items, audience retention tangents, subscription pitches, and food discussion with no clear segmentation or deliberate pacing — topics are introduced and dropped without resolution.

relevance.rationale: The episode touches on real political topics — Iran assassination plot, Israeli elections, Ukraine drone strikes, oil profits — but the coverage is casual, bar-chat commentary loosely woven between French fry talk, drink-of-the-day segments, and radio promotion, preventing it from scoring as substantive news coverage.

clipability.rationale: The few politically pointed moments (oil profits/Trump quid pro quo, Ukraine reaching Siberia) are too embedded in conversational filler and bar-show banter to stand alone as a 30-45 second preview without significant context.

card.tagline: Iran plots, Israeli elections, and Ukraine drones over Friday night fries
card.description: A casual bar-format political roundup covers the alleged Iranian assassination plot, Netanyahu's October election, and Ukraine's Siberian drone strikes with commentary and cocktails.
card.reason: Because this episode covers several active international political flashpoints

scored.description:
A daily briefing on the AI systems, products, companies, and policy shifts that are just becoming possible.

Want a podcast for your own topics? Join early access: https://www.barelypossible.to/waitlist/?source_path=public_feed&feed_source=rss

latestEpisode.title: Barely Possible - 2026-07-13

clip.text: own website. Now, my first reaction was, didn't we already invent this? It's called RSS, and it's older than some of you listening. But I want to be fair to it, because there's a real thesis underneath. The open web — the blogs, the independent sites, the newsletters — lost the attention war to the algorithmic feeds. Not because the open web had worse content, but because it had worse ergonomics. Scrolling a feed is frictionless. Checking twelve bookmarked blogs is a chore. So HyperTexting's bet is that if you give open-web content the same slick, scrollable wrapper that the social platforms use, people will actually consume it, and creators

transcript.scoringWindow: directly. And the AI crawlers — the bots that scrape the web to feed training data — are, from a creator's point of view, showing up to eat that work for free and give nothing back. So Patreon teaming with Cloudflare, which sits in front of a huge chunk of the internet's traffic and can identify and block those bots, is the platform effectively drawing a fence around its creators' stuff and saying: you want this, you negotiate, you don't just take it. And here's why this matters to you as a builder even if you never touch Patreon. The free-scraping era of the open web is closing. Slowly, unevenly, but closing. Cloudflare has been building tooling to let sites detect and block or charge AI crawlers, and platforms are lining up to use it. If your product's business model quietly assumes you'll be able to hoover up open web content for free forever, that assumption is decaying under you right now. The web is being partitioned into stuff you can take and stuff you have to pay for, and the paywall is getting smarter about telling the difference. Plan for a world where data has a toll booth. Because the toll booths are going up. Now, that theme — who gets to take what, and who gets squeezed — actually runs straight through a couple of the harder stories in today's pile. Let me get into one that has nothing to do with AI on its surface and everything to do with the pattern. Ars Technica ran a piece, reported by Mark Olalde of ProPublica and Jimmy Tobias for High Country News, on the Bureau of Land Management rewriting its grazing regulations. And the headline is the story: the overhaul seeks to cut public involvement. This is the first time since 1995 — three decades — that the BLM is rewriting the rules for how livestock grazing works on public lands. That's federal land, land that belongs to all of us in the technical and legal sense, being used by ranchers to graze cattle under a permit system. And the through-line the reporting flags is that the rewrite is aimed at reducing the public's ability to weigh in on how that land gets used. Now why am I putting a cattle-grazing story in an AI show? Because it's the same move, in a completely different arena, that I keep pointing at. When you're rewriting the rules that govern a shared resource — whether it's public grassland or the open web — one of the quietest, most consequential levers is who gets a seat at the table when the decision gets made. Cut public involvement, and you've changed the outcome before anybody votes on it. It's a process story dressed up as a paperwork story, and the process is where the power lives. I'd keep an eye on that one, because "we streamlined the public comment period" is one of those phrases that sounds like efficiency and functions like a locked door. Let me stay in the public-health-and-policy lane for one more, because it's a recent report worth flagging. Ars Technica's Beth Mole, writing on the tenth, covered a study on the changes RFK Jr. has made to the measles vaccine schedule. The framing from the piece: the study confirms the change will hurt vulnerable toddlers. The specific mechanism, as Ars lays it out, is about the combination shot — the kids who get a combination shot are, in the words of the piece, some of the most vulnerable. Now I'm going to stay in my lane here. I'm a radio guy, not a pediatrician, and I'm not going to freelance on the medical particulars beyond what the reporting says. But the reason it belongs in a briefing for builders and decision-makers is this: it's another instance of a policy change at the top rippling down to the people with the least ability to absorb it. That's a theme with legs whether you're talking vaccine schedules, land access, or web data. The decision gets made way upstream, and the cost lands on whoever's standing at the bottom of the hill. If you're building products that touch health, families, or anything regulated, the regulatory ground is moving, and it's not moving in a straight line. Watch it. Alright, let me lighten the load, because it's summer and not everything's a policy fight. TechCrunch's Lauren Forristal wrote up a review — during last weekend's brutal heat wave, she put the new Ninja Slushi Twist to the test instead of trudging out for a Slurpee. Ninja's latest slushie machine, building on the popularity of the original. Now look, this is a countertop appliance review, and I'm not going to pretend it's a signal about the future of intelligence. But I'll give you the honest builder-brain read on why a machine like this sells: it takes a small, real, recurring annoyance — it's a hundred degrees out and I want a frozen drink and I don't want to leave the house — and it kills it, reliably, for a price people will pay. That's it. That's the whole business. No AI, no agents, no moat made of magic. Just a clear job that a customer feels in their bones every hot afternoon. And I mention it right after all this frontier-model talk on purpose, because sometimes the most durable product in the room is the one that solves the dumbest, most human problem completely. Keep that humility handy when you're tempted to bolt a large language model onto something that just needed to make a good slushie. Now here's a product story that's more in our wheelhouse. TechCrunch's Sarah Perez, in a recent report, wrote up a new app called HyperTexting, which aims to turn the open web into a scrollable, social-media-like feed. The idea is that it takes websites, blogs, newsletters, and podcasts and stacks them into a feed you scroll like Instagram, while also making it easier to post to your own website. Now, my first reaction was, didn't we already invent this? It's called RSS, and it's older than some of you listening. But I want to be fair to it, because there's a real thesis underneath. The open web — the blogs, the independent sites, the newsletters — lost the attention war to the algorithmic feeds. Not because the open web had worse content, but because it had worse ergonomics. Scrolling a feed is frictionless. Checking twelve bookmarked blogs is a chore. So HyperTexting's bet is that if you give open-web content the same slick, scrollable wrapper that the social platforms use, people will actually consume it, and creators will get an audience without renting it from a platform that can change the rules on them Tuesday. And that connects straight back to the Patreon and Cloudflare story, right? Both are, at bottom, about the open web trying to reclaim ground from the walled gardens. One's about keeping the scrapers out. One's about making independent publishing feel as good to consume as the feeds do. Whether HyperTexting works, who knows — the graveyard of "RSS but nicer" apps is deep and full. But the direction of travel is real: a bunch of smart people are trying to rebuild a version of the web where the creator, not the platform, holds the relationship with the reader. As a builder, if you've been assuming the platforms own distribution forever, notice that a lot of money and effort is now betting against that. Might be worth thinking about where you'd stand if the feed weren't the only game in town. Now let me get to a cluster of OpenAI-flavored items, and I'm going to be careful here because a couple of these are thin on detail and I'm not going to build castles on sand. There's a Verge item pointing at GPT-5.6, Codex, and ChatGPT Work. Now, we already covered the GPT-5.6 and ChatGPT Work launch back on the tenth — Altman did the livestream, the desktop app, the whole enterprise push against the incumbents. So per my own rule, I'm not going to re-run that story or re-quote anybody. If there's a genuinely new wrinkle in this particular piece about Codex and how it plugs into the Work product, I don't have enough in front of me to responsibly tell you what it is, so I'll just note it's a continuation of that same enterprise-knowledge-layer play we talked about a few days back and leave it there. And a quick callback while we're here — remember Altman's line we flagged, that the benchmarks suggest 5.6 is the best model in the world "right now"? The operative words there being "right now," which in this business means "until roughly Thursday." Nothing new to add on that today. There's a second Verge item in the pile with a headline suggesting OpenAI's Fidji Simo is stepping down from a CEO role into an advisor role. And here's where I'm going to be a hard-nose, because I've got standards on this show and one of them is: I do not report executive departures off a headline I can't verify with a body. I've got a URL slug and nothing underneath it — no article text, no date I trust, no confirmation of what actually happened or when. Personnel moves at a company like OpenAI are exactly the kind of thing that gets garbled in the retelling, and "steps down as CEO to advisor" could mean a dozen different things depending on which CEO role and what the actual transition is. So I'm going to do the responsible thing and not narrate it as fact. If it firms up with real reporting, we'll cover it properly with the details. Until then, file it under "heard something, waiting on the real story." That's the honest move, and you deserve the honest move. Same discipline on a GitHub item floating in the pile referencing a Claude Code issue involving Anthropic and Microsoft. It's an issue tracker link with no substance I can responsibly read to you — could be a bug report, could be a feature request, could be nothing. I'm not going to spin a corporate-intrigue narrative out of an issue number. If the developer-tooling relationship between those two shifts in a way that matters, it'll show up somewhere I can actually stand on. Not going to guess. I want to flag one more that I'm treating with real caution, and then I'll tell you the deeper point. There's a New York Times piece, from the tenth, on AI-enabled terrorism — the reporting looks at Boko Haram in Nigeria and how AI tools factor in. And there's a related report from an outfit called CASP on AI-enabled terrorism as well. Now, I only have the pointers to these, not the full bodies, and the CASP report in particular I can't reliably date, so I'm not going to imply any of this happened today or read you specifics I can't back up. But I'll name the category, because it's important and it's coming: as these tools get cheaper and more capable, the same capabilities that let a small startup punch above its weight also let bad actors punch above theirs. That's not a reason to panic, and it's definitely not a reason to trust a scary headline I can't read the guts of. It's a reason to understand that the misuse conversation is going to get louder and more concrete, and it's going to shape regulation that lands on everybody building in this space — including the people building nothing but boring, useful, legitimate tools. When the misuse stories get specific, the rulemaking gets specific right behind them. Watch that space, and demand the actual reporting before you believe the framing. Now, since I brought up regulation, let me note one more marker on the map. There's an item pointing to the AI Now Institute — that's a research and policy shop focused on the social implications of AI.

structure.rationale: The host uses a clear throughline (who controls shared resources) to connect disparate stories, with deliberate transitions and explicit signposting, though the pacing loosens in the OpenAI cluster where multiple items are deferred or declined.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers substantive policy-adjacent topics — BLM grazing regulation rewrites, RFK Jr.'s vaccine schedule changes, and AI regulation — but is primarily a tech/builder briefing that treats political developments as secondary signals rather than its core subject.

clipability.rationale: Several self-contained, quotable moments exist — the toll booth metaphor, the slushie machine builder-brain riff, and the 'process is where the power lives' line — each of which could stand alone as a 30-45 second preview.

card.tagline: The open web is being fenced off — here's what builders must know now
card.description: From AI scraper toll booths to BLM rule rewrites, this episode maps how control over shared resources is quietly shifting upstream, away from the public.
card.reason: Because this episode connects AI data policy to real regulatory moves happening now

scored.description:
"The Neutral Corner" boxing podcast covers all the latest news in the fight game. Every Monday, host Michael Montero reviews/previews all the fights from around the world and takes listener calls. He is never afraid to ruffle feathers when he gives hot takes or goes on heated rants.

Montero has contributed to numerous boxing publications in multiple countries, performed ringside commentary around the USA and is an International Boxing Hall of Fame voter.

latestEpisode.title: Episode 389: Jaron "Boots" Ennis stops Xander Zayas; Oleksandr Usyk vacates Titles

"Tavis Smiley" is the flagship program for KBLA, America's newest progressive radio network and LA's first Black-owned talk station. Broadcast live across southern California on 1580AM and around the world on KBLA1580.com and TuneIn, this podcast presents the best of Tavis Smiley's conversations with activists, influencers, thought leaders, and cultural vanguards.

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/tavis-smiley--6286410/support.

structure.rationale: There is a discernible throughline moving from intra-Democratic ideological conflict to third-party prospects to DSA victories to defund-the-police, but transitions are conversational and loose, with a mid-segment Anita Baker digression that disrupts the political flow.

relevance.rationale: The episode engages substantively with live political fault lines — DSA electoral wins, Democratic Party ideological fractures, defund-the-police debates, and third-party viability — but the published date of 2026-06-30 makes some references (Bernie 2020, early DSA wins) feel temporally ambiguous rather than sharply current.

clipability.rationale: Several passages — West's 'neoliberal plantation' line, 'if all we have is the Democratic Party you're gonna lead folk into the crackhouse,' and the Bernie's babies framing — are punchy and self-contained enough to work as standalone previews without heavy setup.

card.tagline: Cornel West calls the Democratic Party a neoliberal plantation — live on air
card.description: Tavis and Dr. West debate whether the Democratic Party can survive its left-right civil war, third-party viability, and what DSA wins mean for Black political power.
card.reason: Because this episode cuts to the heart of the Democratic Party's ideological crisis

scored.description:
Live from West Virginia’s Capital City, with stories from every corner of the Mountain State. From Noon to 3 p.m., MetroNews Midday connects West Virginians with the news that matters most — hosted by 13 News and Tonight Live anchor Amanda Barren and Dave Allen of MetroNews. Amanda and Dave deliver up-to-the-minute headlines and breaking news while amplifying voices from around the state through insightful interviews and real-time conversation.

📺 Watch Hour 3 live on any device with MetroNews TV
📱 Join the conversation by texting 304-TALK-304 or call 800-765-TALK

 With featured segments like:
  • Mayor Monday – spotlighting towns and cities across the state
  • Wonderful West Virginian – honoring unsung heroes for their generosity and community spirit
MetroNews Midday is your live, local, and lively midday connection to West Virginia.

latestEpisode.title: MetroNews Midday Friday July 10, 2026 Hour 3

clip.text: Il cancro è considerato uno dei segni zodiacali più romantici e sensibili. Chi ha cancro o ha una crash di questo segno tende a vivere le emozioni in modo profondo e cerca sempre amore e dolcezza nelle relazioni. Secondo le stelle, la compatibilità migliore in amore per il cancro si trova con lo scorpione, i pesci e il toro. Con lo scorpione nasce una connessione intensa e profonda. Vi sentite protetti e riuscite a tirar fuori il meglio l'uno dall'altro. nonostante qualche piccolo momento di gerosia. Con i pesci c'è una sintonia emotiva unica, fatta di coccole e sostegno reciproco, che vi permette di vivere una storia dolce e sognante.

transcript.scoringWindow: Il cancro è considerato uno dei segni zodiacali più romantici e sensibili. Chi ha cancro o ha una crash di questo segno tende a vivere le emozioni in modo profondo e cerca sempre amore e dolcezza nelle relazioni. Secondo le stelle, la compatibilità migliore in amore per il cancro si trova con lo scorpione, i pesci e il toro. Con lo scorpione nasce una connessione intensa e profonda. Vi sentite protetti e riuscite a tirar fuori il meglio l'uno dall'altro. nonostante qualche piccolo momento di gerosia. Con i pesci c'è una sintonia emotiva unica, fatta di coccole e sostegno reciproco, che vi permette di vivere una storia dolce e sognante. Il toro, invece, garantisce stabilità e sicurezza. Insieme vi sentite davvero a casa e riuscite a costruire una relazione solida e duratura. Le combinazioni meno fortunate includono i gemelli, l'ariete e l'acquario. Con i gemelli è difficile trovarsi, perché voi cercate sicurezza mentre loro amano la libertà, portando spesso a incomprensioni. Con l'ariete c'è attrazione, ma le differenze nel modo di affrontare i problemi possono creare tensioni e richiedere molta pazienza. Con l'acquario vedete l'amore in modo troppo diverso. Voi volete vicinanza, loro indipendenza, e rischiate di sentirvi trascurati. Ricordate però che questi sono solo suggerimenti legati alle caratteristiche dei segni zodiacali. Ognuno è unico.

structure.rationale: The episode follows a loose but recognizable pattern — best matches, then worst matches, then a disclaimer — but it reads as a flat list with minimal transitions or deliberate pacing.

relevance.rationale: This episode covers zodiac sign romantic compatibility for Cancer, which has no connection to news or political events whatsoever.

clipability.rationale: Individual sign pairings are briefly self-contained, but they are formulaic and interchangeable, offering little that would function as a compelling standalone hook.

scored.description:
Beyond the Horizon is a project that aims to dig a bit deeper than just the surface level that we are so used to with the legacy media while at the same time attempting to side step the gaslighting and rhetoric in search of the truth. From the day to day news that dominates the headlines to more complex geopolitical issues that effect all of our lives, we will be exploring them all.

It's time to stop settling for what is force fed to us and it's time to look beyond the horizon.

structure.rationale: The episode moves between distinct interview segments with identifiable guests and clear topic transitions, but pacing is uneven and commercial breaks interrupt the flow without strong signposting between segments.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers two timely, specific local and regional news events — a deadly marina fire in Cabo San Lucas and earthquake relief efforts in Venezuela — giving it clear current-events grounding, though the scope is hyperlocal rather than broadly political.

clipability.rationale: The fire chief's account of the marina blaze and the Venezuela corruption commentary both contain self-contained, quotable moments, though most require at least minimal setup to land fully.

card.tagline: A yacht fire, a sunken fireboat, and a death at Cabo's marina
card.description: Cabo San Lucas's fire chief details a four-hour marina blaze that killed one person and nearly sank Mexico's first purpose-built marine firefighting vessel, while guests discuss Venezuela's earthquake response and government obstruction of aid.
card.reason: Because this episode breaks down two urgent disasters unfolding right now

scored.description: Each week comedian Ross Patterson shares his wildly unique views on pop culture, entertainment, and sports as he's joined by the world's worst producer Jessie Wiseman. She's easy on the eyes, but terrible on Google.

scored.feedTitle: Good News York by Growth Mode Content
scored.description:
Your go-to podcast for inspiring stories, uplifting news, and positive vibes from across New York State!

Whether it's groundbreaking innovations, community heroes, or businesses making a difference, we bring you the stories that remind us all of the good happening around us.

Join us as we highlight the changemakers, dreamers, and everyday folks bringing positivity to the Empire State.

From local businesses thriving in Syracuse to heartwarming tales from Buffalo, Albany, and beyond—this is the news you’ll actually want to hear. Because good news deserves to be heard.

🔊 Listen in, get inspired, and spread the good! Good News York is a project of Growth Mode Content in Syracuse, NY.

Visit: GoodNewsYork.com for full video episodes, clips, links and more!

Want to create podcasts and content for your brand? That's what Growth Mode Content is all about! Visit: GrowthModeContent.com to learn more!

This podcast delivers sharp political commentary, cultural analysis, and headline breakdowns, with a tone that resonates with viewers of Fox News, Newsmax, and other conservative-leaning outlets. Whether you're tuning in for takes on breaking news, media bias, or global affairs, America Today: Daily News Briefing offers clarity, conviction, and unapologetic truth-telling. We dissect stories covered by Fox News, Tucker Carlson, Sean Hannity, Laura Ingraham, and The Five, not to echo them, but to challenge, contextualize, and expand the conversation. transcript.scoringWindow: WEBVTT 1 00:00:00.200 --> 00:00:03.520 The civil and emergency infrastructure of Kiev suffered its most 2 00:00:03.520 --> 00:00:06.519 devastating blow of the year on Thursday, as Russian forces 3 00:00:06.639 --> 00:00:10.800 unleashed an eleven hour overnight bombardment, killing at least twenty 4 00:00:10.839 --> 00:00:15.240 seven civilians and injuring more than ninety others. The complex 5 00:00:15.519 --> 00:00:18.879 waves of attack assaults saw Russia deploy a staggering five 6 00:00:19.000 --> 00:00:23.239 hundred seventy aerial weapons consisting of seventy four missiles and 7 00:00:23.480 --> 00:00:27.399 four hundred ninety six attack drones, forcing thousands of residents 8 00:00:27.480 --> 00:00:32.439 into underground metro stations and bomb shelters. Ukrainian officials described 9 00:00:32.479 --> 00:00:36.159 the onslaught as a night of horror that intentionally targeted 10 00:00:36.159 --> 00:00:40.479 civilian quarters, while rescue crews scrambled through the smoldering ruins 11 00:00:40.520 --> 00:00:44.520 of collapsed apartment blocks to locate survivors trapped beneath the rubble. 12 00:00:45.000 --> 00:00:48.280 The highly coordinated air campaign caught the capital at a 13 00:00:48.320 --> 00:00:54.479 moment of heightened vulnerability. Despite advanced intelligence warnings, Ukrainian President 14 00:00:54.560 --> 00:00:59.000 Volodimir Zelenski had urgently cut short a diplomatic visit to Dublin, 15 00:00:59.280 --> 00:01:03.799 Ireland less than twenty four hours prior after receiving fresh 16 00:01:03.840 --> 00:01:08.200 intelligence reports that Moscow was staging a massive strategic strike. 17 00:01:08.719 --> 00:01:12.599 According to the Ukrainian Air Force, Russia's barrage included an 18 00:01:12.680 --> 00:01:18.120 unusually high concentration of advanced ballistic and hypersonic weapons, alongside 19 00:01:18.200 --> 00:01:22.599 jet powered Shahid drones designed to exhaust local defenses. While 20 00:01:22.640 --> 00:01:27.159 air defense systems successfully intercepted the majority of the incoming targets, 21 00:01:27.560 --> 00:01:31.719 a critical shortage of Patriot interceptor missiles allowed twenty six 22 00:01:31.799 --> 00:01:36.280 missiles and twenty attack drones to bypass the shield, impacting 23 00:01:36.319 --> 00:01:40.000 more than thirty three distinct locations across the city. The 24 00:01:40.040 --> 00:01:44.879 structural and humanitarian damage across Kiev's residential areas is extensive, 25 00:01:45.319 --> 00:01:48.959 prompting Kiev Mayor Vitali Klichko to declare a formal day 26 00:01:49.000 --> 00:01:52.560 of mourning. In the eastern Darnitsky District, on the left 27 00:01:52.599 --> 00:01:56.120 bank of the Denipro River, a direct missile strike caused 28 00:01:56.159 --> 00:01:59.680 six structural levels of a nine story residential building to 29 00:01:59.719 --> 00:02:05.079 com depletely collapse, destroying dozens of apartments and trapping families inside. 30 00:02:05.400 --> 00:02:08.919 A hotel in the city center hosting European expats. An 31 00:02:08.960 --> 00:02:12.919 ambulance station and a building housing EU diplomats were also 32 00:02:13.039 --> 00:02:16.960 heavily damaged or affected. The geographical scope of the Russian 33 00:02:16.960 --> 00:02:21.400 assault extended beyond the capital, with parallel strikes recorded in 34 00:02:21.439 --> 00:02:26.960 the regional hubs of Sumi, de Neipro, Zaparajia, cherkisy Odessa, 35 00:02:27.120 --> 00:02:31.360 and Kharkiv. The Kremlin openly framed the massive bombardment as 36 00:02:31.360 --> 00:02:35.919 a direct retaliatory response to Ukraine's highly effective long range 37 00:02:36.000 --> 00:02:41.360 drone campaign against Russian domestic infrastructure. Russian Defense Ministry officials 38 00:02:41.400 --> 00:02:47.360 stated that the massive, coordinated strikes successfully neutralized military airfield components, 39 00:02:47.759 --> 00:02:53.879 homegrown drone assembly plants, and supporting energy facilities. However, international 40 00:02:53.879 --> 00:02:58.520 military analysts note that the escalation materializes just as Ukraine's 41 00:02:58.560 --> 00:03:03.240 intensified drone campaign against Russian oil refineries, including a recent 42 00:03:03.319 --> 00:03:07.599 successful strike on the lukoil refinery in Kstovo, has triggered 43 00:03:07.639 --> 00:03:12.360 severe domestic fuel strains inside Russia. With Russian President Vladimir 44 00:03:12.400 --> 00:03:16.280 Putin declaring that the mass aerial strikes must continue and 45 00:03:16.439 --> 00:03:21.280 President Zelensky fiercely vowing that Ukraine will definitely retaliate. The 46 00:03:21.319 --> 00:03:25.199 brutal exchange underscores a significant hardening of the conflict.

clipability.rationale: Several self-contained, quotable passages — particularly the Patriot missile shortage detail and the Kremlin's retaliation framing — work as standalone previews without requiring much setup.

scored.description:
The Vault: The Epstein Files Unsealed is a deep-dive investigative podcast that pulls back the curtain on one of the most protected criminal networks in modern history. This series is built from the ground up on the actual paper trail—unsealed court records, depositions, exhibits, emails, and filings that were never meant to be read by the public. No pundit panels. No spin. Just the documents themselves, examined line by line, name by name, connection by connection—paired with precise, document-driven analysis that explains what the record truly shows.

Each episode opens the vault on newly unsealed or long-buried Epstein files and walks listeners through what they actually reveal about power, money, influence, and the systems that failed survivors at every turn. Alongside the filings themselves, informed commentary breaks down the legal strategy, the institutional behavior, the contradictions, and the implications hiding between the lines. From judges’ orders and sealed exhibits to sworn testimony and back-channel communications, the show connects the dots the media often won’t—or can’t. Patterns emerge. Timelines collapse. Excuses fall apart.

The Vault is a working archive in audio form, a living record of the Epstein case as told by the courts themselves—supplemented by rigorous analysis that provides context, challenges official narratives, and exposes where the record has been distorted, sanitized, or deliberately ignored. Every claim is grounded in filings. Every episode is anchored to the record. Listeners aren’t told what to think—they are shown what exists, what was said under oath, and what the commentary reveals about how those facts were buried, softened, or misrepresented.

If you want to understand how Jeffrey Epstein was protected, who circled him, how institutions closed ranks, and why accountability keeps slipping through the cracks, The Vault: The Epstein Files Unsealed is where the record finally speaks for itself—and where the commentary ensures the documents do what no press release ever will.

Stay informed, no matter how busy you are. Reporter Noa Levi of Israel Today: Ongoing War Report brings you the most crucial updates from Israel every hour. Sourced directly from leading news channels and top-rated RSS feeds, our team distills extensive news coverage from 'Telegram Channel Israel Today', 'Haaretz', 'JPost.com', 'The Times of Israel', 'Ynet' and more, into concise 2-3 minute summaries. From breaking headlines to political shifts and cultural happenings, get a comprehensive snapshot of the nation's heartbeat without the fluff. Subscribe now and never miss a beat from the heart of Israel.

  • Telegram Channel Israel Today

  • Front - Globes
  • Haaretz latest headlines
  • JPost.com - Homepage & Breaking

DISCLAIMER DISCLAIMER DISCLAIMER
This podcast's content is generated using advanced artificial intelligence techniques and aims to summarize information from various sources. It's important to note that while the AI strives for accuracy, it might not always provide an exact representation of the news. There's potential for unintended inaccuracies or omissions. The content provides an overview of discussions over the last hour and serves as a digest for those seeking a concise update. For comprehensive and direct information, please refer to primary news sources.

scored.feedTitle: True Crime Podcast 2026 - Police Interrogations, 911 Calls and True Police Stories Podcast
scored.description:
True Crime Podcasts 2026 — 911 Calls, Police Interrogations & Crime Stories

This is your nonstop true crime feed — built for fans who binge for hours.

Hear authentic 911 calls, full-length police interrogations, unsolved mysteries, and shocking cases from across the country.

Episodes drop in shifts — mornings, afternoons, and nights — so there’s always fresh crime waiting, whether you’re commuting, on the job, or streaming through the night.

Perfect for shift workers, commuters, night owls, and binge listeners who want hours of true crime content — not once a week, but every single day.

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/true-crime-podcast-2026-police-interrogations-911-calls-and-true-police-stories-podcast--5693470/support.

relevance.rationale: This is a true crime episode featuring police interrogation audio with no connection to current news or political events.

clipability.rationale: A few visceral moments — 'all I hear is pop' or the $20,000 ultimatum scene — could work as standalone hooks, but most of the excerpt requires significant prior context to follow the cast of characters and sequence of events.

card.tagline: The getaway driver who didn't know it was a murder until the gunshot
card.description: A suspect's interrogation unravels a home invasion gone fatal, as Jonathan leads detectives to a body in the woods while claiming he never knew it would end in murder.
card.reason: Because you follow real interrogations and the cases behind them

scored.description:
Melissa Unfiltered - a podcast where nothing is off limits. As a Mom with three adopted kids, I dive into foster care system failures, realities of Mom life, homeschooling, sexual abuse cover ups, domestic violence and any issues that get me fired up each week.

My perspective is unfiltered, fearless, and usually different from the mainstream narrative. 

Join me as I question and expose our cultures “accepted” truths, shine a light on the uncomfortable, and explore topics through a lens that’s without boundaries and uniquely mine.

latestEpisode.title: The Quiet War

00:16:30.480 --> 00:16:31.279 Why are you following? 00:16:31.440 --> 00:16:32.399 Why are you posting? 00:16:32.480 --> 00:16:33.200 Like, same. 00:16:33.360 --> 00:16:37.759 I'm I did it, I posted it, I felt good about myself. 00:16:37.919 --> 00:16:40.159 I liked, you know, putting it out there. 00:16:40.240 --> 00:16:44.240 I I'm not trying to judge other people again, self-reflection. 00:16:45.200 --> 00:16:51.759 But I just, I don't know, living living your lives and trying to have your brain process all this. 00:16:51.919 --> 00:16:55.519 I I really think that we were never designed for this. 00:16:55.759 --> 00:17:00.399 I really think our brains are not meant to process all of this information. 00:17:01.200 --> 00:17:03.919 I think we're supposed to be in real communities. 00:17:04.079 --> 00:17:05.440 We're supposed to help people. 00:17:05.599 --> 00:17:07.759 And here's another part that I thought about. 00:17:07.920 --> 00:17:19.119 Some people's accounts on here, like mine, um, right now, this podcast, are meant to help people bring awareness, um, help inspire change. 00:17:19.440 --> 00:17:21.039 But what if some of that help? 00:17:21.279 --> 00:17:29.599 Like, what if some of the accounts, instead of being like, let me show you how to cook this meal in under 10 minutes so you can be a better parent? 00:17:29.839 --> 00:17:36.720 Like, would you ever go up to the mom who you see struggling at swim practice with her kids and be like, hey, do you need some help? 00:17:36.960 --> 00:17:37.599 Can I help you? 00:17:37.680 --> 00:17:39.200 Like, where can I be a good friend to you? 00:17:39.359 --> 00:17:40.559 Where can I be a good neighbor? 00:17:40.720 --> 00:17:42.079 Like, I think we've lost that. 00:17:42.240 --> 00:17:45.599 I don't think anyone's actually helping anyone in person anymore. 00:17:45.920 --> 00:17:55.200 If you're willing to post this for strangers, why would you not be willing to help someone in real life within your little community? 00:17:55.359 --> 00:18:02.720 I think that there would be so much value in that rather than just putting yourself on the internet and say, this is to help someone. 00:18:03.039 --> 00:18:11.680 And in all of this, you know, knowing that we were never meant to see any of this, we were never meant to see what people are doing 24-7. 00:18:12.000 --> 00:18:13.440 That's not how our brains work. 00:18:13.599 --> 00:18:16.160 And we wonder why we're all exhausted. 00:18:16.480 --> 00:18:21.839 We wonder why we're anxious and we constantly compare ourselves to everyone. 00:18:22.000 --> 00:18:38.160 I mean, the last time that you said, oh, I want this outfit, or I want this thing in my kitchen, or I need this gadget, was it because you actually thought in your head all on your own that you needed it, or because you saw someone on social media or something on social media that said that you needed it? 00:18:38.400 --> 00:18:45.519 I mean, we are being programmed without even knowing this, and we're allowing it and we're giving access. 00:18:45.680 --> 00:18:47.839 I'm not talking about an ad that pops up. 00:18:48.079 --> 00:18:50.079 It's everything's an ad. 00:18:50.319 --> 00:18:56.799 Everything everyone's doing is essentially an ad because they saw it somewhere and they're trying to keep up and they wanted to do this. 00:18:57.039 --> 00:19:00.559 I think we're just not meant to consume this much information. 00:19:00.880 --> 00:19:10.799 And, you know, we should try living in a much simpler life than in this digital world because we are designed by God to be more simple than this. 00:19:11.279 --> 00:19:16.960 Um, you know, I think knowing all this, the real war isn't left versus right. 00:19:17.119 --> 00:19:20.720 It isn't rich versus poor, it isn't old versus young. 00:19:21.039 --> 00:19:24.559 It's, you know, the your data. 00:19:24.880 --> 00:19:25.839 They're collecting your data. 00:19:25.920 --> 00:19:26.880 I know we hear it all the time. 00:19:26.960 --> 00:19:34.400 They're collecting your data, and yes, they are, but it's not just your data that's valuable, it's your attention. 00:19:34.640 --> 00:19:36.799 This is a war for your attention. 00:19:36.960 --> 00:19:43.680 I mean, the biggest companies in the world are spending billions of dollars trying to keep you scrolling just one more minute. 00:19:43.839 --> 00:19:45.039 And they do, they succeed. 00:19:45.119 --> 00:19:46.240 I catch myself all the time. 00:19:46.319 --> 00:19:48.480 I'm like, oh my gosh, stop, don't scroll. 00:19:48.640 --> 00:19:53.119 Like another great thing I heard it was like, don't bring your phone in the bathroom and don't say, ew, that's gross. 00:19:53.200 --> 00:19:55.119 I know everyone brings their phone in the bathroom. 00:19:55.279 --> 00:19:57.440 Can you go to the bathroom without bringing your phone in? 00:19:57.599 --> 00:19:57.839 Really? 00:19:57.920 --> 00:19:58.400 Can you? 00:19:58.559 --> 00:19:59.440 It's a question. 00:19:59.680 --> 00:20:02.799 Like, they just want your attention. 00:20:03.039 --> 00:20:07.200 Every app that you download on your phone wants to send you notifications. 00:20:07.599 --> 00:20:10.400 You know, headlines have become dramatic and repetitive. 00:20:10.480 --> 00:20:16.319 You'll hear the same thing over and over and over, the same headline on every news station. 00:20:16.640 --> 00:20:21.440 The outrage is spreading like wildfire, like we talked about in last week's episode. 00:20:21.680 --> 00:20:24.000 Everyone's outraged over the silliest things. 00:20:24.160 --> 00:20:25.519 No one cares about good news. 00:20:25.680 --> 00:20:27.200 Everyone wants to be outraged. 00:20:27.279 --> 00:20:29.759 And these videos, like they keep autoplaying. 00:20:29.839 --> 00:20:30.240 Why? 00:20:30.480 --> 00:20:31.759 For your attention. 00:20:32.000 --> 00:20:35.279 Every little second of your attention has so much value. 00:20:35.440 --> 00:20:40.720 It is the most valuable thing you have, not your money, your attention, your time. 00:20:40.960 --> 00:20:42.640 And you're just giving it away. 00:20:42.799 --> 00:20:45.039 Free will, free will, just giving it away. 00:20:45.200 --> 00:20:48.000 Here's my attention, my human attention. 00:20:48.160 --> 00:20:56.160 You know, every company wants more of it, every politician wants more of it, every media outlet wants more of it, every influencer wants your attention. 00:20:56.400 --> 00:20:58.960 Advertisers, they're not really out to sell products. 00:20:59.039 --> 00:21:04.319 They want your attention first, and then your attention allows them to get those products sold. 00:21:04.559 --> 00:21:08.160 And wherever your attention goes, your behavior follows. 00:21:08.400 --> 00:21:11.440 And your behavior, money follows. 00:21:11.680 --> 00:21:13.759 Your attention is so valuable. 00:21:14.000 --> 00:21:15.440 They can't live without it. 00:21:15.680 --> 00:21:21.279 And, you know, I makes me question like what kind of person is the hardest person to manipulate? 00:21:21.440 --> 00:21:23.759 Because I feel like we're all just so easy to manipulate. 00:21:23.920 --> 00:21:44.400 I would say the hardest person to manipulate is someone who can sit quietly with their thoughts, someone who isn't desperate for approval, someone who isn't distracted, someone who reads something and then doesn't react, and who actually goes and does their own research on something, someone who's not emotionally hijacked by every headline. 00:21:44.559 --> 00:21:47.519 I know I have been, I've, you know, we've all been there. 00:21:47.759 --> 00:21:54.160 Um, someone who's not validated by strangers, like, why are we seeking validation from strangers on the internet? 00:21:54.480 --> 00:21:55.759 It's mind boggling. 00:21:55.920 --> 00:21:59.440 You know, those are the kind of people who are hard to manipulate. 00:21:59.519 --> 00:22:07.759 And And on the flip side, the easiest person to influence, someone who's distracted, someone who's reactive, someone who's scrolling all day long. 00:22:08.079 --> 00:22:11.599 I, everywhere I go, I see people just scrolling on their phone. 00:22:11.680 --> 00:22:18.400 You know, I was standing in line getting ice cream with my son the other day, and this grandma in front of us was just scrolling videos on her phone. 00:22:18.640 --> 00:22:25.359 The kid was running off somewhere playing, so it's not like she was neglecting the child, but like, can we sit anywhere without scrolling on our phone? 00:22:25.519 --> 00:22:26.960 They're getting our attention. 00:22:27.039 --> 00:22:28.960 You know, it makes you feel behind. 00:22:29.200 --> 00:22:30.960 It makes you feel, oh, I need approval. 00:22:31.039 --> 00:22:31.920 I'm behind. 00:22:32.079 --> 00:22:33.839 I can't live in this silence. 00:22:34.000 --> 00:22:37.440 You know, I need the constant simulation or I need the validation. 00:22:37.599 --> 00:22:43.200 Like anytime there's a problem, you're posting in a Reddit feed, like, is this, am I the problem? 00:22:43.440 --> 00:22:45.759 You're in mom's groups on Facebook. 00:22:45.839 --> 00:22:53.279 I always say Facebook groups are amazing and they are, they can be a great tool for information, but I feel like we're just giving our attention away, needing validation. 00:22:53.440 --> 00:22:54.640 Am I doing the right thing? 00:22:54.799 --> 00:22:59.359 Like, we are not capable of figuring things out on our own. 00:22:59.519 --> 00:23:05.279 And I think this is my conspiracy head, you know, like the way I think sometimes, usually not wrong. 00:23:05.359 --> 00:23:06.559 I'd love to be wrong. 00:23:06.720 --> 00:23:08.400 But I really think this was planned. 00:23:08.480 --> 00:23:13.039 And it feels to me now like stepping out of this like it was intentional. 00:23:13.200 --> 00:23:15.440 And the worst part is we fell for the trap. 00:23:15.680 --> 00:23:20.640 We all fell for the trap, you know, convenience it makes things easier. 00:23:20.799 --> 00:23:22.319 Here's everything online for you. 00:23:22.480 --> 00:23:26.880 You know, open your AI and ask it every question, it'll be faster. 00:23:27.119 --> 00:23:29.920 But we really need to start paying attention. 00:23:30.240 --> 00:23:35.519 And, you know, we're we we need to start protecting our minds. 00:23:36.400 --> 00:23:41.279 You think about like every night you go to bed and you lock your door, right? 00:23:41.440 --> 00:23:42.880 I would hope that you lock your door. 00:23:43.039 --> 00:23:46.160 We don't just leave it open for anyone. 00:23:46.240 --> 00:23:55.279 Um, you know, you put a password on every account that you have, you protect your kids, you don't just hire a random stranger to babysit your kids, I would hope. 00:23:55.440 --> 00:24:02.319 You know, you protect your identity, um, you

structure.rationale: The episode has a loose but followable throughline — from social media oversharing to the attention economy to manipulation resistance — though it meanders conversationally without tight segmentation or clear signposting.

relevance.rationale: This is a lifestyle/self-help episode about social media habits, attention economy, and digital wellness — not a news or politics episode, despite brief incidental mentions of COVID opinions and political posting.

clipability.rationale: Several genuinely self-contained, quotable moments exist — particularly the 'the real war isn't left vs. right, it's a war for your attention' passage and the 'hardest person to manipulate' list — that could stand alone as preview clips.

card.tagline: The real war isn't political — it's a battle for your attention
card.description: Melissa argues that social media platforms, advertisers, and media outlets have engineered a quiet war to capture your attention, and most of us are losing it without realizing.
card.reason: Because this episode challenges how you spend your most valuable resource

scored.feedTitle: All Caught Up: A Newsworthy podcast
scored.description:
Each week the All Caught Up team examine a newsworthy topic, talk to those in the know and consider what it means for young Australians and beyond. 


Hosts: Anoushka Israni @anoushkaisrani  and Ben Tresno-Turner @bentresnoturner 

Sound Editor and Engineer: Michelle Zeng  @michelle.zeng.creative

Executive producer: Alexandra Spring  @newsworthy_aus

latestEpisode.title: AI-assisted dating is here. Will it change Gen Z’s love lives?

clip.text: QA, but also through an optional camera roll scan. 00:05:43.680 --> 00:05:50.000 So they'll scan your photos, decide what kind of person you are, and then match you with someone else who has a similar lifestyle. 00:05:50.240 --> 00:05:50.399 Okay. 00:05:50.480 --> 00:05:53.040 Do you think your camera roll is like indicative of your personality? 00:05:53.279 --> 00:05:53.759 You know what? 00:05:53.839 --> 00:05:59.519 I don't think it's it might be indicative of who I am, but I don't think it would define what I want in a future partner. 00:05:59.759 --> 00:06:00.160 I agree. 00:06:00.319 --> 00:06:09.839 And I guess the stats also support what you're saying because the

transcript.scoringWindow: about catfishing and scams. 00:02:49.439 --> 00:02:50.319 It's all about trust. 00:02:50.479 --> 00:02:52.800 Dating itself is a very vulnerable thing, you know. 00:02:52.960 --> 00:02:56.000 And if you're getting scammed, it makes it quite icky and quite difficult. 00:02:56.240 --> 00:03:00.000 How do you know that there is somebody real behind the screen, you know, that's really telling you the truth? 00:03:00.159 --> 00:03:02.560 Technology has made deception so much easier. 00:03:02.800 --> 00:03:03.120 That's true. 00:03:03.199 --> 00:03:13.280 And I think a really deceptive part about dating apps is how you're putting your whole, you know, personality, that's 21 years of life experience, on six photos and three like cringy little props. 00:03:13.360 --> 00:03:17.039 It's just like such a weird way to package yourself up and put yourself out there into the world. 00:03:17.280 --> 00:03:17.599 Exactly. 00:03:17.680 --> 00:03:25.599 And these six pictures you're choosing, they have to be the perfect angle, the best lighting, your favorite outfit, everything to perfectly curate who you are. 00:03:25.680 --> 00:03:27.280 It's almost like a personal brand. 00:03:27.360 --> 00:03:31.520 And then by that point, it's hard to know who you're actually swiping on and what they actually look like. 00:03:31.840 --> 00:03:32.000 Right. 00:03:32.080 --> 00:03:39.439 So it feels a bit like a scam in a way, like you're seeing this perfect portrayal of a person, but then in person themselves, it might be a little bit different. 00:03:39.599 --> 00:03:41.439 And the stats sort of back that up. 00:03:41.520 --> 00:03:48.000 So Cyber Daily revealed that 40% of Australians have reported being targeted by some form of dating scam. 00:03:48.319 --> 00:03:48.639 Exactly. 00:03:48.719 --> 00:03:51.199 And romance scams are becoming more and more of an issue. 00:03:51.360 --> 00:04:01.680 According to the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission, romance scams accounted for$28.6 million of financial losses between January and December of 2026. 00:04:01.840 --> 00:04:03.599 And that was reported to ScamWatch. 00:04:03.840 --> 00:04:07.840 AI has added a new level of speed and scale to these romance scams. 00:04:07.919 --> 00:04:14.719 So it allows the scammers to code automated messages and create scarily convincing online personas to trick people, you know? 00:04:14.800 --> 00:04:17.519 They can even do photos and videos and deepfakes now. 00:04:17.600 --> 00:04:18.720 It's all quite scary. 00:04:18.959 --> 00:04:21.279 Yeah, you might be talking to this one girl and you think she's really cool. 00:04:21.439 --> 00:04:23.120 Turns out she's an AI scam. 00:04:23.279 --> 00:04:27.199 But let's bring this back to why people are being icked out on dating apps. 00:04:27.360 --> 00:04:33.680 Well, I reckon it's because you're growing up, watching the notebook, you're hearing about how your parents met in such a romantic way. 00:04:33.839 --> 00:04:35.040 But where are you meeting people? 00:04:35.199 --> 00:04:41.360 Well, it's on this app that's riddled with scammers and catfishing and you know falsified, curated versions of people. 00:04:41.519 --> 00:04:45.360 So I guess to put it simply, it's just less romantic, right? 00:04:48.480 --> 00:04:52.720 AI is not only being utilized by these scam artists, but also by the other side of it all. 00:04:52.879 --> 00:04:57.920 So the dating apps themselves are integrating AI into the platforms to get better results. 00:04:58.160 --> 00:04:58.319 Right. 00:04:58.399 --> 00:05:05.360 Some of the most popular apps have it, and they're trying to help people meet in person through AI compatibility because that's where the sparks fly. 00:05:05.519 --> 00:05:13.519 So Hinge, for example, uses generative AI prompts, you know, to provide feedback on your profile and suggest some random sort of conversation starters. 00:05:13.839 --> 00:05:16.480 And on Tinder, they have an AI tool called Chemistry. 00:05:16.639 --> 00:05:18.480 So this is an AI-powered matching system. 00:05:18.560 --> 00:05:23.920 It's very similar to what Hinge does, and it provides a daily curated batch of highly compatible matches. 00:05:24.079 --> 00:05:26.879 It's kind of instead of your random swiping to give you a bit more direction. 00:05:27.199 --> 00:05:27.839 Yeah, that makes sense. 00:05:28.000 --> 00:05:34.959 Like on paper, they're showing the people that would work with you, who you should match with, so you're not just like randomly swiping at the most random people of all time, right? 00:05:35.199 --> 00:05:36.480 Yeah, and especially with Tinder. 00:05:36.560 --> 00:05:38.079 So what they do, it's really interesting. 00:05:38.160 --> 00:05:43.600 They learn your preferences either through an interactive QA, but also through an optional camera roll scan. 00:05:43.680 --> 00:05:50.000 So they'll scan your photos, decide what kind of person you are, and then match you with someone else who has a similar lifestyle. 00:05:50.240 --> 00:05:50.399 Okay. 00:05:50.480 --> 00:05:53.040 Do you think your camera roll is like indicative of your personality? 00:05:53.279 --> 00:05:53.759 You know what? 00:05:53.839 --> 00:05:59.519 I don't think it's it might be indicative of who I am, but I don't think it would define what I want in a future partner. 00:05:59.759 --> 00:06:00.160 I agree. 00:06:00.319 --> 00:06:09.839 And I guess the stats also support what you're saying because the match.com survey revealed that 47% of people view AI in romantic contexts negatively. 00:06:10.160 --> 00:06:15.120 And that makes sense to me because I know I view human emotion and dating in a very separate spot to AI. 00:06:15.360 --> 00:06:17.120 Yeah, other sides of the coin, in my opinion. 00:06:17.360 --> 00:06:18.639 But maybe that's not the case. 00:06:18.800 --> 00:06:23.040 I came across an app made by a clinical psychologist that utilizes AI. 00:06:23.439 --> 00:06:24.800 Hi, I'm Rachel Harker. 00:06:24.959 --> 00:06:28.319 I'm a clinical psychologist and founder of Tribal Group. 00:06:28.399 --> 00:06:30.160 So we have two apps. 00:06:30.319 --> 00:06:36.480 One is Tribal, our dating platform, and the other one is Connect by Tribal, which is our friendship and community platform. 00:06:36.800 --> 00:06:44.240 To give you a little bit more background context, Tribal, the app, it uses psychology journals to create an algorithm to then match people up. 00:06:44.639 --> 00:06:51.600 So I think AI is such a good tool to assist, but it definitely shouldn't be replacing humans. 00:06:52.000 --> 00:06:52.160 Okay. 00:06:52.399 --> 00:06:54.480 So almost like an assistant, if you will. 00:06:54.639 --> 00:07:01.279 So we have put in the psychology, we've put in the evidence, and we use AI to help us with that and refine it. 00:07:01.439 --> 00:07:04.160 So I mentioned before the compatibility quiz. 00:07:04.240 --> 00:07:07.279 So that has scientific journals backing it up. 00:07:07.360 --> 00:07:22.240 We have the scoring system that I developed, and we've input that into the algorithm, but then we use AI to refine that over time and almost provide kind of more informative recommendations based on the psychology that was input. 00:07:22.319 --> 00:07:23.759 So they work hand in hand. 00:07:24.319 --> 00:07:24.560 Right. 00:07:24.639 --> 00:07:31.040 And I downloaded the app just for scientific purposes here, and they gave me a compatibility score ranked on a bunch of different questions. 00:07:31.120 --> 00:07:37.759 So a 44 question quiz, some were on family value, some were how career-centric you are or how creative you are. 00:07:38.000 --> 00:07:42.399 But interestingly, in my opinion, you can't see who you match with for 72 hours. 00:07:42.480 --> 00:07:45.680 You can only see their sort of AI compatibility score. 00:07:45.920 --> 00:07:47.199 That's very interesting. 00:07:47.360 --> 00:07:55.519 I also think with the compatibility score, one thing that might be an issue, the algorithm doesn't really account for the whole opposite attract idea, does it? 00:07:55.600 --> 00:07:58.240 It just sort of pigeonholes you into one concept. 00:07:58.560 --> 00:08:00.800 That's something that we are refining over time. 00:08:01.279 --> 00:08:04.959 The more data that you put into an AI model, the more it will learn. 00:08:05.199 --> 00:08:08.480 So we have had almost 12,000 downloads since we launched. 00:08:08.560 --> 00:08:14.000 So we now have 12,000 data points of people's quizzes, and you can see how they then match. 00:08:14.079 --> 00:08:20.959 And that's where AI actually is an amazing tool because we might say, well, these are the answers to the quiz, and this is what we think is a good recommendation. 00:08:21.120 --> 00:08:33.279 But then as AI learns from those 12,000 people, they'll be like, actually, you know, it's okay to, it might be really good to have the same family values, but a compatibil compatible couple might have quite different career goals. 00:08:33.360 --> 00:08:40.159 One might be okay to maybe take, you know, a step back and support their partner going forward, depending on, you know, family dynamic. 00:08:40.399 --> 00:08:43.679 So you don't always have to have the exactly same values. 00:08:43.840 --> 00:08:45.279 They just need to be aligned. 00:08:45.600 --> 00:08:50.080 Okay, so they're trying to see AI as more of an assistant, not like the star of the show. 00:08:50.159 --> 00:08:51.759 It's not taking any of the credit. 00:08:51.919 --> 00:08:56.320 And we do also agree that there are some positive aspects of using AI in dating. 00:08:56.480 --> 00:08:58.480 For example, how about like religion matching? 00:08:58.639 --> 00:09:04.480 If you're maybe Christian and you only want to date other Christians, this is a good way to sort of filter out those people who just don't suit your type. 00:09:04.720 --> 00:09:08.399 Or even for age range, you've got AI age verification for safety. 00:09:08.559 --> 00:09:10.399 Date within your age range. 00:09:10.559 --> 00:09:11.840 No one too young, no one too old. 00:09:12.000 --> 00:09:12.240 I agree. 00:09:12.320 --> 00:09:16.799 And how about like another form of AI safety is like face scanning, so you can verify your profile. 00:09:16.879 --> 00:09:20.000 So maybe that's going to reduce the amount of catfishes available online. 00:09:20.320 --> 00:09:20.639 Definitely. 00:09:20.720 --> 00:09:23.200 And then you've also got stating your goals, you know? 00:09:23.360 --> 00:09:26.320 You know when somebody wants a long-term or just a short-term hookup. 00:09:26.720 --> 00:09:30.320 So maybe it's not that bad after all, integrating dating with AI. 00:09:33.759 --> 00:09:38.480 Look, I'm all for AI making things a bit more efficient and a little bit easier for you. 00:09:38.720 --> 00:09:42.879 But when AI and dating get mixed up, I'm really not sure how I feel about it. 00:09:43.120 --> 00:09:44.399 But that's just my opinion. 00:09:44.559 --> 00:09:51.679 So we asked around the UNSW campus to see how other students felt about AI's integration into dating apps and if it worked out for them. 00:09:52.080 --> 00:09:53.200 My name is Eva. 00:09:53.360 --> 00:09:55.360 I'm a student at UNSW. 00:09:55.759 --> 00:10:00.240 There are a couple people that I matched with based on the AI feature. 00:10:00.559 --> 00:10:07.279 And I'm gonna be so honest, I did not see any sort of compatibility between me and them. 00:10:07.440 --> 00:10:18.240 Like, for example, um on a very shallow level, I'm very into sports, but for some reason I was getting matched with people that are more musical and so on. 00:10:18.559 --> 00:10:25.840 And in fact, I thought I would give it a good crack and go on a date based on this AI compatibility level, and I did. 00:10:26.399 --> 00:10:37.200 And what I've learned is that there's just such a huge difference, and what I kind of gathered is that AI doesn't account for all the niche parts of your personality. 00:10:37.679 --> 00:11:07.919 For example, I I'm a bit more of a serious person, and I like someone that's a bit more like you know, bubbly and easygoing, but you can't really catch that on the apps, and AI definitely can't catch that because it does not understand the niche parts of personality, and yeah, so that actually ultimately made me stop going on hinge altogether because I did some research, and AI is really an underlying tone of hinge these days. 00:11:08.159 --> 00:11:08.879 Definitely, yeah. 00:11:09.120 --> 00:11:14.000 Um, and so yeah, now I don't

structure.rationale: The episode follows a clear throughline from scam risks to app features to an expert interview to student vox pops, with identifiable segments and a loose but functional narrative arc.

relevance.rationale: This episode is a lifestyle/technology feature about AI in dating apps, with no substantive connection to news events, legislation, policy, or political developments.

clipability.rationale: The student vox pop from Eva and the clinical psychologist's explanation of AI-assisted matching both work as reasonably self-contained moments with minimal setup required.

card.tagline: Swiping on AI: Is your dating app matchmaker actually a machine?
card.description: Two UNSW students investigate how AI is reshaping Gen Z dating — from romance scams to psychology-backed compatibility algorithms — with a clinical psychologist and real user experiences.

scored.description: Plugged In Australia is your essential podcast for the latest electric vehicle news tailored to Aussie drivers. We break down fresh updates on sales trends, policy changes like road-user charges and tax exemptions, and infrastructure developments—from charging networks in Sydney to regional rollouts. Get quick insights on new models hitting the market, like affordable BYD imports and Tesla’s latest, plus analysis on how global shifts affect Oz. Whether you’re tracking EV adoption rates or debunking myths, tune in weekly for concise, no-fluff coverage to keep you informed on the road to a greener future. Subscribe now and plug into the conversation

latestEpisode.title: Episode 69; Deep Dive: Record EV Sales, Charging Failures, New Affordable EVs and Electric Trucks
