the Ranger plugin hybrid. 01:08:06.800 --> 01:08:10.079 MG is obviously looking at the U9 hybrid we mentioned earlier. 01:08:10.320 --> 01:08:16.960 Nissan has the Frontier Pro plugin hybrid on its way, and GWM already has the larger Canon Alpha plugin hybrid. 01:08:17.119 --> 01:08:23.279 The Canon HI4T gives GWM a chance to compete more directly in the mainstream Ute space. 01:08:23.439 --> 01:08:39.279 For a little bit of context, the larger Canon Alpha HI4T uses a 2-litre turbocharged four-cylinder engine and a 37.1 kilowatt hour battery with a claimed electric only range of 115 kilometers on the wonderful NEDC cycle. 01:08:39.359 --> 01:08:42.399 I don't know what's with all the NEDC cycles with these cars lately. 01:08:42.640 --> 01:08:50.079 As yet we don't have full pricing and specifications for the smaller Canon HI4T, but the market it is aimed at is pretty obvious. 01:08:50.239 --> 01:08:54.479 Plug-in hybrid dutes are becoming one of the biggest battlegrounds in Australia. 01:08:54.800 --> 01:09:07.760 GWM is also expanding the Canon Alpha with an XSR grade, which should be the more sort of off-road focused flagship, and it's adding a new 3-litre turbo diesel to the Canon Alpha and the Tank 500. 01:09:08.000 --> 01:09:13.199 And that diesel part's not really our focus here, but it's still relevant to GWM's broader strategy. 01:09:13.279 --> 01:09:22.319 They are not betting on one powertrain, trying to uh offer petrol diesel, hybrid, plug-in hybrid, and battery electric options across different segments. 01:09:22.560 --> 01:09:28.720 But as we can see, the plug-in hybrids and the electrics are definitely gaining more ground. 01:09:28.960 --> 01:09:33.279 And finally, GWM is preparing to launch Whey in Australia. 01:09:33.520 --> 01:09:40.239 Whey will become GWM's fifth local sub-brand, joining Haval, Tank, Canon, and Aura. 01:09:40.479 --> 01:09:46.239 It's being positioned as the more premium arm focused on large SUVs and people movers. 01:09:46.399 --> 01:10:02.720 The GWM has not confirmed exactly which Whey models Australia will get, but the Chinese lineup includes plug-in hybrid and electric SUVs and MPVs, including models like the V9X large SUV and the G9 people mover. 01:10:03.119 --> 01:10:04.800 That could be very interesting for families. 01:10:04.960 --> 01:10:12.319 Australia is badly lacking proper electrified people movers and large family haulers at sensible pricing. 01:10:12.560 --> 01:10:15.600 Looking at you, Kia, looking at you, Hyundai. 01:10:16.000 --> 01:10:27.279 If Way comes in with a large plug-in hybrid or EV SUV or an MPV with some decent electric range, it could give GWM a completely different kind of buyer. 01:10:27.760 --> 01:10:30.399 This is where the bigger picture sort of comes into play. 01:10:30.560 --> 01:10:43.920 GWM, it's not just trying to be an EV brand like Tesla, it's not trying to be only value like the old Great Wall, even though GWM, Great Wall Motors, and it's not trying to copy BYD exactly. 01:10:44.079 --> 01:10:56.800 It's building on a broad portfolio, affordable EV SUVs, plug-in hybrid youths, hybrid and plug-in family SUVs, off-roaders, premium large SUVs, and people movers. 01:10:56.960 --> 01:11:04.000 That's the right strategy for Australia because Australian buyers are not all moving at the same speed. 01:11:04.479 --> 01:11:13.279 Some are ready for the full EV, some want a plug-in hybrid, some still want diesel for towing and touring, some want a family SUV, some want a U. 01:11:13.600 --> 01:11:18.479 Some want or need seven seats, some want just a cheap runabout commuter EV. 01:11:18.720 --> 01:11:21.520 GWM is trying to have something for all of them. 01:11:21.840 --> 01:11:23.520 Challenge is going to be the execution. 01:11:23.680 --> 01:11:25.840 Nine new models, all variants in six months. 01:11:26.000 --> 01:11:35.279 It's a fair shake of the sauce bottle that uh it's going to put pressure on dealers, servicing training, parts supply, marketing, and customer education. 01:11:35.439 --> 01:11:42.000 It also creates a risk of confusing buyers if the sub brands and model names are not explained clearly. 01:11:42.239 --> 01:11:51.439 Aura, Haval, Canon, Tank, and Way all need to make sense to regular buyers, not just to car nerds like myself and probably some of you out there. 01:11:51.600 --> 01:11:54.479 And that's where BYD still has an advantage. 01:11:54.720 --> 01:11:59.680 BYD's naming can be confusing as well, but the brand itself is very clear now in Australia. 01:11:59.920 --> 01:12:06.000 GWM has a stronger variety of sub-brands, but needs to make the whole family feel coherent. 01:12:06.159 --> 01:12:08.319 Still, the ambition is real and it's there. 01:12:08.560 --> 01:12:19.359 GWM hit a record sales month in June, it has a large production pipeline, it has access to more of its global catalogue, and it is clearly treating Australia as a priority market. 01:12:19.520 --> 01:12:28.319 So while BYD and Tesla are out getting most of the EV headlines right now, GWM is quietly building one of the broadest electrified lineups in the country. 01:12:28.479 --> 01:12:34.800 And if it gets pricing, support, and product quality right, top five might not be as far away as it sounds. 01:12:36.000 --> 01:12:41.600 And as we've reported before, Leap Motor is bringing the B05 Electric Hash to Australia. 01:12:41.760 --> 01:12:50.720 The B05 is due in Australian dealerships from late August of 2026, and it will launch here in two rear-wheel drive variants. 01:12:50.880 --> 01:12:56.079 The entry version is the B05 style, priced from 3590 drive away. 01:12:56.239 --> 01:13:02.560 Above that is the B05 Design LR with regular pricing around 3990 drive away. 01:13:02.640 --> 01:13:09.600 Although a 38990 driveway launch offer is being advertised for eligible vehicles in lightning yellow. 01:13:09.840 --> 01:13:17.600 So this is not a$60,000 EV trying to call itself affordable, so genuinely in the small car price conversation. 01:13:17.840 --> 01:13:26.720 Well that matters because if EVs are going to keep us growing in Australia, we cannot just rely on expensive family SUVs and luxury models, which still have their place. 01:13:26.880 --> 01:13:43.680 We need normal cars, hatchbacks, city cars, small SUVs, stuff that people can actually cross shop against things like a Corolla, a Mazda 3, an I30, Serrado replacement, BYD Dolphin, MG4, GWM Oura 5, or even a secondhand petrol or secondhand EV. 01:13:43.840 --> 01:13:51.039 The B-05 is interesting because it's not just cheap, it also has a layout that should appeal to people who actually enjoy driving. 01:13:51.279 --> 01:13:59.439 Both Australian variants are rear-wheel drive using rear-mounted electric motors producing 160 kW and 240 Nm. 01:13:59.840 --> 01:14:02.000 It's a pretty decent number for this sort of class. 01:14:02.159 --> 01:14:11.680 The entry style uses a 56.2 kWh lithium-ion phosphate battery and has a claimed WLTP range of up to 401km. 01:14:12.079 --> 01:14:21.039 The design LR steps up to 67.1 kWh LFP battery and increases claimed WLTP range to 482km. 01:14:21.520 --> 01:14:28.640 Leap motor claims a 0 to 100km an hour time of 6.7 seconds for the design LR. 01:14:28.800 --> 01:14:36.880 So the top spec B05 has more range than the Dolphin Premium and more than the current MG SN64. 01:14:36.960 --> 01:14:44.720 That gives it a very strong spec sheet argument, especially when you remember the design LR is sitting around 40 grand drive away. 01:14:44.880 --> 01:14:48.079 And charging is better than you might expect in this sort of price range. 01:14:48.239 --> 01:14:56.159 Both variants support an 11 kilowatt AC charging, which is good for home or destination charging if you have three phase available. 01:14:56.319 --> 01:15:06.720 DC charging is listed at up to 174 kilowatts with a claimed 30 to 80% taking around 16 minutes in the style and 18 minutes in the design LR. 01:15:06.880 --> 01:15:08.079 It's a useful figure. 01:15:08.159 --> 01:15:16.319 It's not XPing G6 level charging, but for small, affordable hatch, 174 kW DC capability is pretty strong. 01:15:16.399 --> 01:15:25.439 And if the real-world charging curve is decent, this could be a surprisingly practical road trip EV for people who do not want or need a large SUV. 01:15:25.680 --> 01:15:32.079 Dimensionally, the B05 is larger than a city runabout, but still compact enough to make sense in urban use. 01:15:32.239 --> 01:15:40.239 It measures 4,430mm long, 1,880mm wide with a 2,735mm wheelbase. 01:15:40.319 --> 01:15:48.239 So it's roughly in that sort of Volkswagen Golf, Cooper Born, MG4 sort of space rather than the tiny BYD dolphin territory. 01:15:48.479 --> 01:15:53.439 Cargo space is listed at up to 1,205 litres with the rear seats folded. 01:15:53.520 --> 01:16:03.199 Now that's not SUV space, but it is useful enough for hatchback, especially if you're using it as a daily driver, a commuter, runabout, or a second family car. 01:16:03.520 --> 01:16:07.520 The design is also more premium looking than the price might suggest. 01:16:07.680 --> 01:16:14.079 The B-05 has frameless doors, flush door handles, LED lighting, and a full-width rear light bar. 01:16:14.239 --> 01:16:20.479 Now those things are not essential, but they help the car feel a bit less basic and budget EV rather. 01:16:20.560 --> 01:16:24.319 And this is where Leap Motor seems to be positioning the B05. 01:16:24.640 --> 01:16:28.239 It's not trying to be cute like the old GWM or a hatch. 01:16:28.319 --> 01:16:32.079 It's not as aggressively styled as some performance EVs. 01:16:32.159 --> 01:16:38.800 It's more of a sleek coupe style electric hatch aimed at buyers who want something modern but not too ridiculous. 01:16:39.039 --> 01:16:41.279 Standard equipment list is also very strong. 01:16:41.359 --> 01:17:08.159 Even the style gets 19-inch alloy wheels, LED headlights, automatic climate control, over-the-air updates, an 8.8 inch digital instrument display, a 14.6 inch central touchscreen, wireless Apple CarPlay and Android Auto, satellite navigation, wireless phone charging, NFC and Bluetooth key access, connected devices, a 360-degree camera, and even an integrated dash cam recorder. 01:17:08.720 --> 01:17:10.720 Pretty decent list for the money, I think. 01:17:10.880 --> 01:17:36.960 The design LR adds the bigger battery and longer range, but it also adds a fixed panoramic glass roof with an electric sunshape, techno leather upholstery, heated and power adjustable front seats, a heated steering wheel, power folding mirrors, range sensing frameless wipers, rear center armrests and cup holders, a 12 speaker sound system, rear privacy glass, and multicolor ambient lighting. 01:17:37.199 --> 01:17:43.039 So the design LR is not just a long-range version, it's also the comfort and equipment pick. 01:17:43.279 --> 01:17:52.560 Safety equipment includes seven airbags, including a center airbag plus 21 advanced driver assistant features supported by 14 sensors and cameras. 01:17:52.800 --> 01:18:03.760 The driver assistance package includes level 2 assistance, automatic lane change, front vehicle departure warning, front cross traffic alert assistance, and front cross traffic braking. 01:18:04.000 --> 01:18:06.159 As always, the real world calibration matters. 01:18:06.239 --> 01:18:09.279 We have seen plenty of new cars with long safety features. 01:18:09.520 --> 01:18:17.039 They're let down by the annoying alerts, the poor lane keeping tuning, or over-sensitive driver alert monitoring. 01:18:17.199 --> 01:18:25.119 So the B-05 will need to be judged on how those systems behave on Australian roads, not just how many features are listed in the brochure. 01:18:25.520 --> 01:18:28.239 One of the more interesting points is chassis tuning. 01:18:28.399 --> 01:18:49.359 Leap Motor's been making a fair bit of noise about the B-05's Alpha Romeo influenced chassis development, and reports from early overseas drives suggest the car is not just a soft commuter appliance, the rear drive layout should also help it feel bit more balanced than front drive EV hatches, especially under hard acceleration and through corners. 01:18:49.760 --> 01:18:52.159 That can become a good selling point. 01:18:52.239 --> 01:18:57.520 The MG4 has built part of its

structure.rationale: The episode moves methodically through individual vehicle segments with clear transitions and consistent analytical framing, though the excerpt reads as a long sequential rundown without strong signposting between topics.

relevance.rationale: This episode is automotive consumer content focused on EV models, specs, and pricing in Australia — not news or politics.

clipability.rationale: Some standalone moments exist — particularly the GWM brand strategy summary and the B05 price-point argument — but most clips require prior context about the specific models being discussed.

card.tagline: Nine new GWM models in six months — can Australia's dealers keep up?
card.description: Deep dive into record Australian EV sales, GWM's sprawling multi-brand push, and whether the sub-$40K Leap Motor B05 hatch can crack the mainstream market.
card.reason: Because this episode benchmarks every major affordable EV entering Australia right now

structure.rationale: The conversation meanders through repeated clarifying loops as the host catches up on basics, with no clear segmentation beyond a mid-episode ad break, making the throughline hard to follow for a new listener.

relevance.rationale: The episode substantively covers an active geopolitical conflict with direct U.S. foreign policy implications, referencing specific actors (TPLF, RSF, Abiy Ahmad), the 2022 peace agreement, and U.S. government response — clearly news/politics territory, though the framing is introductory rather than breaking-news sharp.

clipability.rationale: Most exchanges require significant setup to be intelligible — the host's repeated 'do I have that correct?' moments and the guest's fragmented answers rarely produce a self-contained, quotable standalone moment.

card.tagline: Ethiopia is on the brink — and Washington may be forced to act
card.description: Africa analyst Scott Morgan breaks down the TPLF's resurgence, Eritrea's proxy role, and why Ethiopia's internal conflict is now a U.S. foreign policy problem.
card.reason: Because Ethiopia's escalating conflict is drawing in regional powers and U.S. attention

scored.description:
Welcome to the official podcast series from USA Cabinet Store – your premier destination for exceptional kitchen and bathroom remodeling since 2011. Based at usacabinetstore.com, we're passionate about bringing your dream home to life through functional, spectacularly designed spaces that enhance your well-being and family lifestyle.

Our Philosophy in Action "We believe that everyone deserves a beautiful home with functional, spectacularly designed kitchens and bathrooms. Our aim is to convert that dream into reality". This core philosophy drives every episode as we share expert insights, client success stories, and industry trends that matter to homeowners.

What to Expect from Our Podcast Join our team of kitchen and bathroom design professionals and installation experts as we explore:

  • Real Client Transformations: Hear directly from satisfied customers like Mrs. Naffa, who praised our "attention to detail and overall project overview"
  • Design Inspiration: From custom cabinet installations to premium countertop selections and space optimization solutions
  • Industry Expertise: Insights from our 16 showrooms across Virginia, Maryland, Texas, North Carolina, and Florida
  • Remodeling Tips: Professional guidance on everything from 3D rendering to complete home remodeling projects

Why Trust USA Cabinet Store With our recent achievement of a 4.8-star Trust Index rating, we've proven our commitment to excellence in both design and construction. Our comprehensive service portfolio includes complete kitchen and bathroom remodeling projects, from initial design consultation through final installation.

Locations & Community Focus Broadcasting from our showrooms in Fairfax and Chantilly, Virginia, we serve communities throughout Northern Virginia including Aldie, Alexandria, Ashburn, Burke, Centreville, Herndon, McLean, Reston, and beyond. Our expansion across multiple states reflects our national reach while maintaining local values and personalized service.

Connect with Us Visit usacabinetstore.com to explore our full range of services, browse our project galleries, and schedule your free design consultation. Follow our podcast journey as we continue to make homes better and improve lives through exceptional kitchen and bathroom transformations.

The Mike O'Meara Show has been delivering daily doses of laughter and insight since 2009. Hosted by the dynamic duo Mike O'Meara and Robb Spewak, this talk show dives deep into everything from personal stories to the latest in news and pop culture, all with a uniquely irreverent twist.
Transitioning from traditional radio to podcasting, The Mike O'Meara Show has cultivated a devoted fanbase that spans the country. Their success is undeniable.
What truly sets The Mike O'Meara Show apart is the strong connection with its listeners. The show thrives on the support and loyalty of a passionate community that tunes in daily for laughs, banter, and a sense of belonging.

scored.feedTitle: The Last Days Of Jeffrey Epstein
scored.description:
The Last Days of Jeffrey Epstein is a long-form investigative podcast that serves as both an archive and a real-time chronicle of the events surrounding the life, imprisonment, and death of Jeffrey Epstein. From the moment of his arrest in 2019 through the unanswered questions that continue to emerge years later, the podcast follows the timeline piece by piece — examining court filings, jail records, witness statements, surveillance failures, media reports, government investigations, and newly released documents as they become public. Rather than treating the story as a closed chapter, the series approaches it as an evolving historical event whose consequences continue to ripple through politics, finance, intelligence circles, law enforcement, and the media. Every episode is designed to preserve the record while also analyzing how the narrative around Epstein has shifted over time, what information has surfaced, what remains hidden, and why the case continues to generate worldwide scrutiny.

The podcast blends archival storytelling with ongoing investigative coverage, creating a living timeline of one of the most controversial deaths in modern American history. Using court documents, deposition transcripts, inspector general reports, leaked communications, news coverage, and firsthand accounts, The Last Days of Jeffrey Epstein reconstructs the chain of events that led to Epstein’s death inside the Metropolitan Correctional Center in New York while continuing to track the fallout that still unfolds in real time. The series does not simply revisit headlines; it documents how the story evolved, how institutions responded, how narratives changed, and how new revelations continue to reshape public understanding years later. Whether examining the failures inside MCC, the powerful figures connected to Epstein, or the unanswered questions surrounding the final days of his life, the podcast functions as both a historical archive and an ongoing examination of a case that refuses to disappear.

structure.rationale: The transcript-reading format provides a clear throughline and the Q&A structure is inherently organized, but the raw deposition format with timestamp artifacts and mid-sentence interruptions makes navigation uneven.

relevance.rationale: Directly covers a sitting Cabinet official's (Howard Lutnick, Commerce Secretary) congressional testimony about his ties to Jeffrey Epstein, a live political accountability story with immediate relevance to current government.

clipability.rationale: Several self-contained exchanges — particularly Lutnick's 'I must have' admission about the island invitation and the Adfinn co-signatory denial — work as standalone moments without heavy setup.

card.tagline: Howard Lutnick under oath: what did he know about Epstein's island?
card.description: Part 10 walks through Lutnick's congressional deposition, revealing his denials about the Epstein island visit, a shared business deal, and how Epstein got on his contact list.
card.reason: Because a sitting Cabinet secretary's Epstein ties are under active congressional scrutiny

scored.description: Mark Blazor Show podcasts – each afternoon, the best of the best of the show lives here.

scored.description:
Since July 2017, Pete Quiñones has been the host of The Pete Quiñones Show (previously known as Free Man Beyond the Wall). His program has focused on the opposition to the globalist project that has undermined historical endeavors. His current interests include philosophy and historical revisionism.

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-pete-quinones-show--6071361/support.

Darwin, who was mostly motivated by economic 533 00:36:21.519 --> 00:36:24.920 ideas and las He says so in his biography we 534 00:36:25.000 --> 00:36:29.599 read malthusts and and and an anthem were his big 535 00:36:30.360 --> 00:36:34.760 motivat you know, right, I mean, what what was what 536 00:36:34.760 --> 00:36:37.679 could be more music to their ears than to hear 537 00:36:37.760 --> 00:36:40.840 that the natural order is based on competition and then 538 00:36:40.880 --> 00:36:43.679 once you defeat your opponent because you're you think you 539 00:36:43.679 --> 00:36:44.639 know you adapt better. 540 00:36:45.000 --> 00:36:51.440 You then absorb their their their their resources, let alone colonialism. 541 00:36:51.519 --> 00:36:53.559 It was, it was music to their ears. 542 00:36:54.360 --> 00:36:57.519 Colonialism in the British sense, was based on bringing bringing 543 00:36:57.559 --> 00:37:03.840 these people, to bring these savages to civilization. We we 544 00:37:03.920 --> 00:37:08.400 have a right to these things. We simply won. Uh uh, 545 00:37:08.599 --> 00:37:12.559 you know Darwinian struggle, you know, Richard. Of course Spencer 546 00:37:12.599 --> 00:37:20.400 made it more explicit. But you know, I I didn't. 547 00:37:20.440 --> 00:37:23.880 I mean, play Plato was my first real sledgehammer to 548 00:37:23.920 --> 00:37:25.719 the idea of the individual and all the stuff that 549 00:37:25.760 --> 00:37:29.559 you know Locke talks about. But once I grasped, I mean, 550 00:37:32.079 --> 00:37:35.000 it took a while before I was able to really 551 00:37:35.000 --> 00:37:38.199 get something out of out of out of hand. Every 552 00:37:38.239 --> 00:37:39.599 once in a while you come across a professor who 553 00:37:39.639 --> 00:37:40.760 really knows how to lay it out. 554 00:37:41.079 --> 00:37:43.280 I thought it was one of them, but you know, 555 00:37:43.280 --> 00:37:44.400 it's very, very difficult. 556 00:37:46.679 --> 00:37:49.079 But when he breaks down what an individual is not 557 00:37:49.119 --> 00:37:52.960 just a human being, but any object, and how we 558 00:37:53.079 --> 00:37:55.480 just assume that it's a single thing, but in reality 559 00:37:55.519 --> 00:37:59.360 it's a whole bunch of things, and it falls apart 560 00:37:59.480 --> 00:38:01.760 even at the tiniest analysis. 561 00:38:02.039 --> 00:38:07.960 Well, yeah, and that's why something that lament I, these 562 00:38:07.960 --> 00:38:12.280 old philologists, these guys like Domseel and like Michael Prodden. 563 00:38:12.480 --> 00:38:14.519 You know, I cite that book on Praden on the 564 00:38:14.679 --> 00:38:17.320 Mongols a lot. And you know, in the Third Reich 565 00:38:17.440 --> 00:38:21.320 that was that that was a book that was taken 566 00:38:21.360 --> 00:38:30.360 really seriously, especially by the SS Officer Corps at the 567 00:38:30.480 --> 00:38:36.800 at the Yunker School. But uh, to your point about 568 00:38:37.280 --> 00:38:40.480 I didn't really come to understand Kano philosophy, I mean, 569 00:38:40.559 --> 00:38:46.440 Hegel and Any and Ficta and Schopenhauer and Nietzsche and 570 00:38:46.519 --> 00:38:53.880 Heidegger until I developed a truly you know, something approaching 571 00:38:53.920 --> 00:38:58.559 a complete historical perspective on the modern era, you know. 572 00:38:58.639 --> 00:39:02.480 And they're I mean, Corny's might sound. There's an intrinsicy 573 00:39:02.599 --> 00:39:06.920 holistic aspect to studying anything relating to the human condition 574 00:39:07.079 --> 00:39:11.800 at scale, you know, So you can't just study philosophy 575 00:39:11.840 --> 00:39:18.440 and isolation or study history in isolation or linguistics or anthropology. 576 00:39:18.559 --> 00:39:21.199 You've got to treat it as this sort of total 577 00:39:21.360 --> 00:39:26.960 enterprise and and then patterns that emerge and the sort 578 00:39:26.960 --> 00:39:30.840 of key factors emerge and kind of come into relief 579 00:39:30.960 --> 00:39:33.559 real like I'm a better way to describe it, at least, 580 00:39:34.039 --> 00:39:37.639 I mean that's that's the way I learn or perceive things. 581 00:39:37.679 --> 00:39:41.239 And I I don't think I'm some radical outlier, you know. 582 00:39:41.679 --> 00:39:44.599 And uh, that was the reasoning, yeah, you know, that 583 00:39:44.679 --> 00:39:49.480 was the reasoning behind disciplines such as philology, which were 584 00:39:50.679 --> 00:39:55.440 well by our contemporary metric. We do this as radically interdiscipline, ay, 585 00:39:55.480 --> 00:39:58.360 when in reality it's it's just a way, it's just 586 00:39:58.400 --> 00:40:02.760 it's a you know, a holistic paradigm of research. But I. 587 00:40:04.559 --> 00:40:07.840 In my early twenties, you know, in as I finished college, 588 00:40:07.920 --> 00:40:11.719 grad school, I was mostly a political theorist, a political philosopher. 589 00:40:12.800 --> 00:40:15.719 But I eventually my dissertation on Michael Oakeshott. 590 00:40:15.719 --> 00:40:17.719 One of the things that Michael Oakshott says is that 591 00:40:18.239 --> 00:40:19.360 these ideas are. 592 00:40:19.239 --> 00:40:20.119 Just hanging in the air. 593 00:40:21.039 --> 00:40:25.119 They have to apply to a group of people, a 594 00:40:25.119 --> 00:40:27.480 living group of people, have to have to manifest these 595 00:40:28.760 --> 00:40:31.599 And so I realized that then comparative politics is going 596 00:40:31.639 --> 00:40:33.599 to be my other And that's where the Russia stuff 597 00:40:33.639 --> 00:40:33.960 came in. 598 00:40:34.239 --> 00:40:38.840 Gus maybe starting in maybe in ninety five. 599 00:40:39.960 --> 00:40:42.760 Because you know, it can't just be political theory, you know, 600 00:40:42.800 --> 00:40:45.480 what the will is, what the intellect is. No, it 601 00:40:45.480 --> 00:40:48.000 has to be political theory relative to a specific group 602 00:40:48.000 --> 00:40:53.159 of people, their historical development, everything about them. Otherwise it's useless. 603 00:40:53.159 --> 00:40:56.280 It's just it's just words in the air. And Michael 604 00:40:56.840 --> 00:40:57.960 made that at the Central Court. 605 00:40:58.519

structure.rationale: The conversation meanders through Hegel, Marx, Darwin, Oakeshott, and Russian history without clear segmentation or signposting, following the loose logic of a multi-part roundtable rather than a structured episode.

relevance.rationale: This is a philosophical discussion about Hegelian thought, libertarianism vs. conservatism, Marxist ontology, and intellectual history — not tied to any current news event or political development.

clipability.rationale: A few quotable lines exist — notably the claim that 'the free market is a revolutionary ideology' — but most moments require the surrounding philosophical context to land with any force.

card.tagline: Is free-market capitalism actually a revolutionary, not conservative, ideology?
card.description: Dr. Matthew Raphael Johnson and Thomas777 argue that libertarianism and conservatism are philosophically incompatible, tracing the idea through Hegel, Marx, and Darwin.
card.reason: Because this episode digs into the ideological roots of conservatism and capitalism

scored.feedTitle: America’s Criminologist with Dr. Currie Myers
scored.description:
Dr. Currie Myers brings 35 years of hard‑earned expertise to every discussion—from his early days as a Kansas State Trooper and 
KBI Special Agent serving as sheriff of one of the largest counties in the Midwest.
 
As an applied criminologist, educator, ethicist and public‑policy expert, he has worked deep undercover on major drug operations, 
cracked public‑corruption rings and led high‑profile homicide investigations.
 
Tune in to America’s Criminologist with Dr. Currie Myers—where Sheriff (Ret.) Currie Myers, PhD, MBA, cuts through the noise on crime, policy and politics.
 
As a nationally renowned applied criminologist, ethicist and “go‑to” media expert, he brings hard‑won insight, fearless opinions and a dash 
of wit to every candid conversation. Ready for a real talk on the issues that shape justice in America? Dr. C’s got you covered.  https://drcurriemyers.substack.com/

latestEpisode.title: Americas_Criminologist_07_07_2026_Host_DR_Currie_Myers_History_of_American_Law_Enforcement

clip.text: was fascinated by competent government. Reading Hamilton today, one 228 00:20:34.240 --> 00:20:37.400 cannot escape the impression that he would have appreciated the 229 00:20:37.480 --> 00:20:43.039 frustrations familiar to every modern day police, chief, sheriff, or 230 00:20:43.079 --> 00:20:50.799 public administrator. Rules without enforcement accomplished. Little authority without capability 231 00:20:51.319 --> 00:20:59.480 invites failure. Good intentions cannot substitute for institutional effectiveness. Hamilton 232 00:20:59.559 --> 00:21:05.240 understood good that government carries a practical responsibility. It must 233 00:21:05.319 --> 00:21:10.240 defend the nation, It must administer justice. It must protect commerce, 234 00:21:10.960 --> 00:21:18.519 it must preserve domestic tranquility. Those responsibilities require authority. In 235 00:21:18.680

transcript.scoringWindow: not arguing that government should possess power unlimited 242 00:21:56.599 --> 00:22:00.720 power over its citizens. Rather, he argued that when the 243 00:22:00.839 --> 00:22:05.519 Constitution assigns a legitimate responsibility to government, it must also 244 00:22:05.640 --> 00:22:13.240 provide sufficient authority to fulfill that responsibility. Effectively, a government 245 00:22:13.359 --> 00:22:16.720 charged with protecting the nation, but denied the tools necessary 246 00:22:16.839 --> 00:22:21.359 to go do it is not a safeguard for liberty. 247 00:22:21.480 --> 00:22:27.359 It's merely an illusion, and Hamilton returned repeatedly to this 248 00:22:27.640 --> 00:22:32.640 theme throughout the Federalist papers. Federalists Number fifteen, he warned 249 00:22:33.599 --> 00:22:39.160 against governments that rely solely upon voluntary compliance. Laws, he argued, 250 00:22:40.000 --> 00:22:45.319 cannot remain mere recommendations. A republic worthy of the name 251 00:22:45.480 --> 00:22:52.079 must possess the practical ability to execute its laws. For 252 00:22:52.200 --> 00:22:55.000 those of us who have spent our careers in law enforcement, 253 00:22:55.359 --> 00:23:03.440 Hamilton's observation possess a timely quality. Legislature may pass criminal statutes, 254 00:23:04.119 --> 00:23:08.960 Judges may interpret them, Prosecutors may seek justice. But unless 255 00:23:09.400 --> 00:23:14.759 there exists a lawful mechanism to investigate crime, execute warrants, 256 00:23:15.279 --> 00:23:21.319 arrest offenders, protect witnesses, and maintain public order, the rule 257 00:23:21.400 --> 00:23:27.160 of law becomes little more than an aspiration. And Hamilton 258 00:23:27.319 --> 00:23:32.519 recognized a principle that every experienced police officer today eventually 259 00:23:32.640 --> 00:23:39.359 comes to appreciate. Justice requires institutions capable of carrying it 260 00:23:39.559 --> 00:23:45.000 into effect. At the same time, Hamilton understood that power 261 00:23:45.079 --> 00:23:51.759 without accountability threatens liberty. This is where many modern portrayals 262 00:23:51.799 --> 00:23:59.480 of Hamilton become incomplete. He has sometimes been caricatured, characterized 263 00:23:59.519 --> 00:24:03.839 as faith favoring unlimited central authority, and that's not the case. 264 00:24:05.119 --> 00:24:08.640 In fact, the Federal's papers reveal something far more nuanced. 265 00:24:09.720 --> 00:24:15.119 Hamilton sought an energetic government, not an arbitrary one. Government 266 00:24:15.200 --> 00:24:19.960 power enough to preserve order, a government constrained enough to 267 00:24:20.079 --> 00:24:24.759 preserve freedom. That balance remains one of the defining characters 268 00:24:24.839 --> 00:24:31.079 of American constitutional policing today. Every lawful arrest reflects governmental authority, 269 00:24:32.000 --> 00:24:37.279 every search warrant reflects constitutional restraint, every use of force 270 00:24:37.400 --> 00:24:44.960 review reflects accountability. Every criminal trial reflects due process. Hamilton 271 00:24:45.000 --> 00:24:50.079 would have recognized all of these not as contradictions, but 272 00:24:50.200 --> 00:24:55.880 as evidence that a constitutional government is functioning exactly as 273 00:24:56.000 --> 00:25:03.079 it should now. If Hamilton one supplied the Constitution with energy, 274 00:25:04.240 --> 00:25:09.440 James Madison supplied it with restraint. Together, they formed one 275 00:25:09.440 --> 00:25:14.119 of the greatest intellectual partnerships in American history. Madison was 276 00:25:14.200 --> 00:25:19.720 not physically imposing. Standing barely over five feet tall, often 277 00:25:19.759 --> 00:25:23.440 plagued by illness, and soft spoken by nature, he possessed 278 00:25:23.559 --> 00:25:29.400 none of Hamilton's theatrics personality. Yet what Madison lacked in 279 00:25:29.480 --> 00:25:35.559 physical presence he more than compensated for with extraordinary intellectual depth. 280 00:25:36.759 --> 00:25:41.559 Years of studying history, philosophy, classical government, and political theory 281 00:25:42.079 --> 00:25:46.920 had convinced him that free societies rarely collapse because of 282 00:25:47.480 --> 00:25:54.359 a foreign evasion alone. More often, they destroyed themselves from within. 283 00:25:55.799 --> 00:25:59.880 He understood something that every student of history eventually discovers. 284 00:26:01.559 --> 00:26:08.200 Human nature never changes. Technology changes, weapons changes, governments change, 285 00:26:08.880 --> 00:26:16.559 uniforms change, but human ambition, pride, fear, greed, jealousy, vengeance, 286 00:26:16.640 --> 00:26:22.559 and the desire for power remained remarkably constant throughout history, 287 00:26:24.119 --> 00:26:28.359 and that observation became the starting point for Madison's political philosophy. 288 00:26:29.640 --> 00:26:33.599 Unlike some Enlightenment thinkers who believe that education alone would 289 00:26:33.680 --> 00:26:40.000 perfect mankind, Madison viewed humanity through a far more realistic lens. 290 00:26:40.880 --> 00:26:45.480 He believed men were capable of extraordinary virtue, but he 291 00:26:45.559 --> 00:26:52.160 also knew they were capable of extraordinary corruption. Consequently, government 292 00:26:52.240 --> 00:26:56.839 had to be designed with both realities and mind. His 293 00:26:57.000 --> 00:27:00.599 most famous explanation appears in Federalist Number fifty one, perhaps 294 00:27:00.680 --> 00:27:07.680 the single most quoted essay in American constitutional history. Madison wrote, 295 00:27:08.359 --> 00:27:13.400 if men were angels, no government would be necessary. If 296 00:27:13.440 --> 00:27:18.240 angels were to govern men, neither external or internal controls 297 00:27:18.279 --> 00:27:24.119 on government would be necessary. How true, and only two sentences, 298 00:27:24.759 --> 00:27:29.559 Manison summarized one of the deepest truths ever written about government. 299 00:27:31.359 --> 00:27:35.839 Government exists because human beings are imperfect. Crime exists because 300 00:27:35.920 --> 00:27:41.559 human beings are imperfect, Conflict exists because human beings are imperfect, 301 00:27:42.440 --> 00:27:47.240 and law enforcement exists because human beings are imperfect. If 302 00:27:47.319 --> 00:27:52.720 every person respected life, property and contracts, and the dignity 303 00:27:52.759 --> 00:27:58.640 of others, there would be no need for police officers, prosecutors, judges, 304 00:28:00.640 --> 00:28:07.119 or criminal courts. Civilization itself would function without coercive authority. 305 00:28:08.599 --> 00:28:12.160 If Madison knew, such a world has never existed and 306 00:28:12.359 --> 00:28:16.119 never will Why because God has given us free will. 307 00:28:17.519 --> 00:28:24.200 Yet he immediately added another equally important truth. Those entrusted 308 00:28:24.680 --> 00:28:32.319 with governmental authority are also imperfect. Police officers are human. 309 00:28:33.359 --> 00:28:39.160 Sheriffs are human, Governors are human, Presidents are human. Judges 310 00:28:39.200 --> 00:28:43.640 are human. Every public servant carries the same human strength 311 00:28:44.039 --> 00:28:51.960 and weaknesses found throughout society. Therefore, Madison concluded government itself 312 00:28:52.599 --> 00:28:59.960 must be restrained. Now, this insight fundamentally distinguishes American constance 313 00:29:00.000 --> 00:29:04.279 titutional government from nearly every form of government that preceded it. 314 00:29:05.680 --> 00:29:09.920 Throughout much of history, rulers assumed that the problem always 315 00:29:10.079 --> 00:29:16.359 lay with the people. Madison argued that government itself could 316 00:29:16.400 --> 00:29:22.559 become the problem. That single insight forever changed the relationship 317 00:29:22.640 --> 00:29:29.480 between citizens and those entrusted to enforce the law. As 318 00:29:29.519 --> 00:29:32.480 someone who spent nearly a quarter century in law enforcement 319 00:29:32.839 --> 00:29:36.279 as a state trooper, special agent, and sheriff, I often 320 00:29:36.440 --> 00:29:43.319 reflect on Madison's wisdom. Most officers enter this profession for 321 00:29:43.480 --> 00:29:48.160 honorable reasons. They generally want to help people. They willingly 322 00:29:48.279 --> 00:29:55.359 place themselves in danger for complete strangers. They routinely miss holidays, birthdays, 323 00:29:55.480 --> 00:30:00.400 family gatherings because someone else's emergency requires immediate ada attention. 324 00:30:01.839 --> 00:30:04.640 I have also witnessed acts of courage that the public 325 00:30:05.240 --> 00:30:10.519 will never know. I've also learned another lesson. No profession 326 00:30:10.799 --> 00:30:15.680 is immune from human weakness. The overwhelming majority of officers 327 00:30:15.799 --> 00:30:20.359 serve honorably, but a very small number abuse the authority 328 00:30:20.559 --> 00:30:26.599 entrusted to them, and the founding fathers anticipated that very reality. 329 00:30:27.880 --> 00:30:32.720 That is precisely why they created constitutional limitations. Rather than 330 00:30:32.880 --> 00:30:39.279 relying solely upon the good intentions of public officials. The 331 00:30:39.400 --> 00:30:46.119 Constitution assumes that character matters. It also assumes that institutions matter. 332 00:30:47.279 --> 00:30:54.680 Character without accountability invites abuse. Accountability without character becomes just bureaucracy. 333 00:30:56.039 --> 00:31:01.759 Healthy constitutional governments require both, and that principle remains as 334 00:31:01.839 --> 00:31:07.519 relevant today as it did in seventeen eighty eight. Madison 335 00:31:07.559 --> 00:31:10.759 expanded his reasoning in Federalist Number ten, where he addressed 336 00:31:10.799 --> 00:31:18.079 another enduring challenge facing every free society faction Today. We 337 00:31:18.200 --> 00:31:22.359 often think of faction simply as political parties. Madison meant 338 00:31:22.440 --> 00:31:26.880 something very much broader. A faction is an organized group 339 00:31:26.960 --> 00:31:30.720 of people united by common interests or passions, that become 340 00:31:30.880 --> 00:31:35.799 willing to sacrifice the rights of others in pursuit of 341 00:31:35.920 --> 00:31:42.960 their own objectives. History is filled with such factions. There 342 00:31:43.000 --> 00:31:49.240 could be religious factions, economic factions, political factions, regional factions, 343 00:31:49.400 --> 00:31:55.640 ideological factions. Sometimes they seek noble goals, sometimes destructive ones. 344 00:31:56.680 --> 00:32:00.880 But Madison warned that no society could eliminate factors entirely 345 00:32:01.440 --> 00:32:10.119 without destroying liberty itself. Freedom allows people to disagree, Freedom 346 00:32:10.240 --> 00:32:17.720 allows competing interests, freedom allows passionate debate. The challenge, therefore, 347 00:32:18.480 --> 00:32:23.519 was not eliminating disagreement. The challenge was preventing any single 348 00:32:23.720 --> 00:32:29.039 faction from capturing the machinery of government. And why does 349 00:32:29.079 --> 00:32:33.960 this matter for law enforcement? Because constitutional policing cannot become 350 00:32:34.519 --> 00:32:39.759 an instrument of political factions. The law must remain above politics. 351 00:32:41.079 --> 00:32:47.000 An officer cannot selectively enforce criminal statutes based on political preference. 352 00:32:48.079 --> 00:32:53.759 Sheriff cannot suspend constitutional rights because public opinion temporarily demands it. 353 00:32:54.799 --> 00:32:58.400 Federal agents cannot investigate individuals because they belong to an 354 00:32:58.480 --> 00:33:05.720 unpopular group. Likewise, prosecutors can either nor criminal conduct simply 355 00:33:05.799 --> 00:33:12.640 because offenders happen to support preferred political causes. The Constitution, 356 00:33:13.240 --> 00:33:17.880 ladies and gentlemen, requires equal justice under the law, not 357 00:33:18.039 --> 00:33:24.319 equal justice under politics. That principle traces directly back to 358 00:33:24.480 --> 00:33:30.920 Madison's understanding of faction. When officers swear to uphold the Constitution, 359 00:33:31.799 --> 00:33:37.240 they implicitly swear to resisting. Because instruments of temporary political 360 00:33:37.359 --> 00:33:44.000 passions exist, the obligations have never been more important than today. 361 00:33:45.759 --> 00:33:50.880 Madison also provided another concept that remains central to American policing, 362 00:33:51.079 --> 00:33:55.480 and that's the separation of powers, which nobody else has 363 00:33:55.559 --> 00:33:59.079 in the world like we do. This idea seems almost 364 00:34:00.680 --> 00:34:05.759 to us as Americans. Legislatures write the laws, executives enforce 365 00:34:05.880 --> 00:34:09.559 the laws, and courts interpret the laws. Yet this division 366 00:34:09.679 --> 00:34:17.159 represented a revolutionary departure from many governments throughout history. Under 367 00:34:17.239 --> 00:34:22.800 absolute monarchies, the king frequently exercised all three powers simultaneously. 368 00:34:23.679 --> 00:34:26.199 He could create laws, he could interpret them, he could 369 00:34:26.320 --> 00:34:32.199 enforce them, he could punish violators. No independent institution stood 370 00:34:32.239 --> 00:34:38.000 between government authority and the individual citizen. The Constitution rejected 371 00:34:38.079 --> 00:34:44.320 that model entirely, and Congress enacts criminal statutes. The executive 372 00:34:44.360 --> 00:34:50.880 branch investigates violations. Independent courts determined guilt or innocence, juries 373 00:34:50.960 --> 00:34:55.679 drawn from ordinary citizens participate in the process. Each institution 374 00:34:56.039 --> 00:35:01.239 limits the other. Each serves as a safeguard against concentrated power. 375 00:35:02.480 --> 00:35:08.960 Law enforcement officers therefore, occupy a unique constitutional position. They're 376 00:35:09.039 --> 00:35:14.679 not legislatures. They're not judges. The responsibility is neither to 377 00:35:14.800 --> 00:35:20.159 create law or determine guilt. Their responsibility is to investigate 378 00:35:20.320 --> 00:35:26.639 facts objectively, protect constitutional rights, and preserve public order, and 379 00:35:26.760 --> 00:35:32.199 present evidence fairly within the judicial process. Now, this distinction 380 00:35:33.239 --> 00:35:39.119 is one of the most defining characteristics of American professional policing. 381 00:35:41.199 --> 00:35:46.239 Officers enforce the law, they do not become the law. 382 00:35:48.599

structure.rationale: The episode follows a clear throughline moving from Hamilton to Madison with identifiable thematic segments, though the lecture-style delivery and dense historical narration create uneven pacing without sharp signposting.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers constitutional history and law enforcement philosophy through the Federalist Papers, offering general political commentary with loose contemporary relevance but no specific timely news hook or current political event driving the discussion.

clipability.rationale: Several quotable standalone moments exist — particularly the Madison 'if men were angels' passage and 'officers enforce the law, they do not become the law' — that could function as self-contained previews without heavy setup.

card.tagline: What Hamilton and Madison actually built into American policing — and why it matters
card.description: Dr. Currie Myers traces constitutional law enforcement philosophy from the Federalist Papers, arguing the Founders anticipated both police authority and police abuse.
card.reason: Because this episode connects Founding-era political philosophy to modern policing debates

scored.feedTitle: Watchdog on Wall Street with Chris Markowski
scored.description:
Welcome to The Watchdog on Wall Street Podcast. Author, investment banker, consumer advocate, analyst, and trader Chris Markowski exposes the lies and myths of the big brokerage firms, the mainstream press, and the government. Chris explains the news coming out of the complex worlds of finance, economics and politics and the impact it will have on everyday Americans.

For more information, visit radioamerica.com/watchdog-on-wall-street/

structure.rationale: The episode meanders through America's 250th anniversary reflections, political tribalism, Plessy v. Ferguson, and then pivots to NYC school failure statistics, with loose connective tissue and frequent ad breaks disrupting any clear throughline.

relevance.rationale: The episode touches on education policy failures and government accountability, but the content is primarily opinion commentary and financial show branding rather than substantive coverage of specific legislation, policy decisions, or timely political developments.

clipability.rationale: The hospital/fire department/water utility analogy for failing schools is a genuinely self-contained, quotable moment, and 'if you see a fraud and you don't say fraud, you're a fraud' lands cleanly, but most segments require surrounding context to land.

card.tagline: 900 NYC schools failing — why isn't anyone shutting them down?
card.description: Chris Markowski argues that 43% of NYC students in failing schools would be a scandal in any other industry, demanding accountability for $40K-per-student spending.
card.reason: Because this episode challenges the status quo on public education spending and results

structure.rationale: The conversation follows a loose thematic thread around China's development model but meanders through tangents on East Asian culture, EU education, and personal anecdotes without clear segmentation or signposting.

relevance.rationale: The episode engages substantively with China's geopolitical and economic rise, Belt and Road strategy, Western tariff policy, and global development competition — timely and politically substantive subject matter.

clipability.rationale: A few punchy standalone lines exist — particularly on China's development model versus Western aid — but most moments require surrounding context and the transcript is riddled with transcription errors that undercut clean extraction.

card.tagline: Why China's development model is beating Western aid — and what the West won't admit
card.description: A former US aid worker argues China's Belt and Road is correcting a decades-long capital flow problem that Western institutions deliberately ignored.
card.reason: Because this episode challenges Western narratives on China's economic rise

scored.description:
Jeffrey Epstein: The Coverup Chronicles is a podcast dedicated to examining not just who Epstein was and what he did, but how so many people and institutions worked—then and now—to keep it all hidden. This series cuts past the headlines and digs into the documentation: court filings, deposition transcripts, plea deals, sealed exhibits, and the bureaucratic paper trail that still tells the real story. Our focus isn’t on speculation or recycled outrage. It’s on facts—and the deliberate efforts to keep those facts out of public view.

Each episode will feature in-depth analysis of newly surfaced records and underreported legal developments, alongside expert commentary that connects them to the broader machinery of power that shielded Epstein for decades. We’ll revisit the timeline from his first arrests through his 2008 plea deal, and into the re-investigations that followed his 2019 death in federal custody. And we won’t stop there—we’ll look closely at the current state of affairs: the closed probes, the lingering co-conspirators, the civil suits, and the glaring gaps in accountability.

What makes The Coverup Chronicles different is that we’re not here to sensationalize the story—we’re here to document the ongoing concealment of it. This isn’t just about reliving Epstein’s crimes. It’s about following the networks that enabled them, protected him, and continue to obscure the truth. If you want an honest look at what’s still being hidden—by whom, and why—this is the podcast that pulls those threads.


And I should know—I’ve spent over six years uncovering every dark corner of this case. My name is Bobby Capucci, and I’ve dedicated those same six years  exposing the truth about Epstein and the powerful figures who enabled him. From on-the-ground investigations at Epstein’s Zorro Ranch, where I spoke with insiders, to national appearances on Tucker Carlson, I’ve followed this story farther than most are willing to go.


Who helped Epstein build his empire? Who protected him? And who is still pulling the strings? The answers lie in the shadows of Jeffrey Epstein's criminal empire.  .

This is the truth they don’t want you to hear. And I’m here to make sure you do.

latestEpisode.title: Mega Edition:   A Look Back At The Ghislaine Maxwell Trial (Part 1) (7/13/26)

structure.rationale: The host moves between editorial commentary, trial logistics, and resource recommendations without tight segmentation, resulting in a loose, conversational flow that lacks clear signposting or deliberate pacing.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers the Ghislaine Maxwell criminal trial with specific references to witnesses, testimony, and courtroom proceedings, making it substantively tied to a real legal/political accountability story, though it is a retrospective 'mega edition' look-back rather than breaking news coverage.

clipability.rationale: The passage about children not being able to consent and the victims' courage on the stand contains several emotionally self-contained, quotable moments that could stand alone as a preview without much surrounding context.

card.tagline: Maxwell in court: did the evidence finally hold up against the powerful?
card.description: A deep-dive retrospective on the Ghislaine Maxwell trial, breaking down witness testimony, survivor courage, and whether justice went far enough.
card.reason: Because this episode revisits one of the most consequential sex trafficking prosecutions in recent history

structure.rationale: The episode loosely strings together call-in segments, a recurring 'Donkey of the Day' feature, and celebrity gossip without a clear throughline or deliberate pacing.

relevance.rationale: This is an urban radio morning show rebroadcast covering entertainment gossip, a car show, and a sports announcer cheating scandal — no substantive news or political content.

clipability.rationale: The maritime law caller moment has mild standalone potential, but most exchanges require surrounding context from the show's ongoing bits and relationships to land.

scored.description: Introducing where it all starts, hosted by two VERY normal teenage girls who talk about dream lives, family experiences, expressing feelings and differences in a more of a comedic way. Like any other teenagers, they talk about how tiktok has influenced their lives ESPECIALLY during corona and how they are trying to keep their mind off the pandemic. They end it off with a very memorable quote, one that'll surely have an impact on your day to day life.

structure.rationale: The excerpt follows a clear interview throughline with identifiable topic pivots (norms/filibuster → candidate strategy), though the mid-episode ad break and abrupt cut suggest pacing is functional rather than tightly engineered.

relevance.rationale: The episode directly engages a timely, specific political debate — whether Democrats should abandon procedural norms like the filibuster to counter Republican consolidation of power — anchored to current events including Trump's legislative agenda and recent Democratic electoral losses.

clipability.rationale: The filibuster asymmetry argument — 'there is no filibuster for tax cuts, there is no filibuster for judges' — is a genuinely self-contained, quotable moment that would work as a standalone preview without much setup.

card.tagline: Should Democrats break the rules to save democracy from Republicans?
card.description: Brian Tyler Cohen argues Democrats must abandon deference to norms like the filibuster, using Trump's own playbook to deliver healthcare, voting rights, and climate action.
card.reason: Because this episode debates the defining tactical question facing Democrats right now

scored.description: Charlie is America's hardest working grassroots activist who has your inside scoop on the biggest news of the day and what's really going on behind the headlines. The founder of Turning Point USA and one of social media's most engaged personalities, Charlie is on the front lines of America’s culture war, mobilizing hundreds of thousands of students on over 3,500 college and high school campuses across the country, bringing you your daily dose of clarity in a sea of chaos all from his signature no-holds-barred, unapologetically conservative, freedom-loving point of view.

latestEpisode.title: From the Archive: Charlie’s 2025 CLS Speech

structure.rationale: The speech follows a loose but identifiable arc — advice to men, advice to women, then a Q&A segment — though transitions are informal and pacing is uneven.

relevance.rationale: This is a motivational speech to young conservatives at a Turning Point USA chapter leadership summit focused on dating, marriage, and gender roles — not a news or political policy episode.

clipability.rationale: A few punchy standalone lines exist (the mirror line, the Sabbath advice) but most moments require the surrounding speech context to land with full meaning.

card.tagline: Charlie Kirk tells young women to silence popular culture and prioritize marriage
card.description: A 2025 Turning Point USA leadership summit speech where Kirk delivers blunt advice on dating, gender roles, and faith to college-age conservatives.
card.reason: Because this episode covers conservative cultural messaging aimed at young Americans

You know, I think you know it's two hundred and fiftieth anniversary this year, and so reflecting back on what the founding fathers did, which is instill incredible trust in people, which was a radical idea at the time, and our history and our ability to live up to some of the principles and the Declaration of Independence is messy and unfinished. And so that's why that's why AFP put such a huge emphasis on volunteers and community based engagement and community building. It's because we want people to see themselves in that story and feel hopeful about their role in the future of our country. You know, one other thing I'd like to talk to you about is the future. I know, as a leader, you're always looking forward to listen to administrations changes and news, organization change, people change, and we're living in a very fluid society. As you look down the next couple of years, two to five years, what were your hopes, your dreams, but also what are you planning to do and what would you like to execute with your team. Well, you know, I worked on Capitol Hill twenty six years ago, and I remember being a young kid at that time and listening to lawmakers speak passionately about some really serious challenges that our country was facing. And those challenges were just far enough off but also just close enough that they felt urgent, but they felt it felt like we could tackle them. And I'm speaking specifically about what blawmakers would have said as the looming insolvency of major programs that millions and millions of people depend

structure.rationale: The interview follows a loose Q&A format with identifiable topic shifts (AFP's mission, differentiation, team, challenges, leadership, future), but pacing is uneven and transitions feel formulaic rather than deliberate.

relevance.rationale: The episode touches on civic engagement, institutional trust, and political participation but lacks any specific timely news hooks or current policy debates — it reads as evergreen promotional content for AFP rather than responsive political coverage.

clipability.rationale: The Wisconsin brewery anecdote and the Tocqueville quote offer brief standalone moments, but most exchanges require context about AFP and the host-guest relationship to land meaningfully.

card.tagline: AFP's CEO on why neighbors talking to neighbors beats political talking points
card.description: Emily Seidel explains how Americans for Prosperity rebuilds civic trust through grassroots organizing as institutional confidence hits historic lows.
card.reason: Because this episode explores why Americans feel politics is happening to them

scored.description: He’s not just the conservative voice at CNN — he’s the last man standing athwart the liberal mob.  Delivering the fiery truth. Surrounded. Outnumbered. Still undefeated. Fighting for free speech, Western civilization, and delivering Common Sense for the American People. He's Scott Jennings.

Next 0:16:51.360 --> 0:16:54.800 week he'll host representatives from more than sixty countries in 0:16:55.040 --> 0:16:59.280 Washington for a counter terrorism summit focused on the resurgence 0:16:59.800 --> 0:17:04.159 of transnational far left terrorism. The meeting will center on 0:17:04.200 --> 0:17:10.760 cooperation against violent extremist networks, including Antifa linked groups. This 0:17:10.880 --> 0:17:14.720 is following the administration's broader counter terrorism strategy, which was 0:17:14.800 --> 0:17:18.240 unveiled earlier this year. Here to discuss this and other 0:17:18.320 --> 0:17:22.920 matters of importance, the State Department's top spokesperson, Tommy Piggott, 0:17:22.960 --> 0:17:23.359 joins us. 0:17:23.400 --> 0:17:24.560 Tommy, welcome to the show. 0:17:25.160 --> 0:17:29.840 Let's start with his conference on fighting transnational far left terrorism. 0:17:29.840 --> 0:17:32.920 Tell us about the effort here, Well, this is. 0:17:32.880 --> 0:17:35.360 Part of a broader effort to really address what's been 0:17:35.400 --> 0:17:38.399 a gap in counter terrorism efforts. This has been a 0:17:38.440 --> 0:17:41.200 threat that has existed for some time, and we're seeing 0:17:41.200 --> 0:17:44.560 a resurgence of it. And so we're taking this seriously. 0:17:44.920 --> 0:17:46.880 I do want to stress I think what sometimes gets 0:17:46.920 --> 0:17:48.399 lost in some of the coverage is that this is 0:17:48.440 --> 0:17:52.000 based in activity that these groups are doing. We are 0:17:52.040 --> 0:17:56.120 seeing groups engage in terrorist activity, kidnappings, targeting of US 0:17:56.200 --> 0:18:01.040 law enforcement, violence against civilian populations is theivity that we're addressing. 0:18:01.119 --> 0:18:04.800 And we see many of these groups with transnational connections, 0:18:05.080 --> 0:18:07.840 which requires that we have that cooperation with allies and 0:18:07.880 --> 0:18:10.240 partners on this. So we have had a degree of meetings, 0:18:10.680 --> 0:18:13.639 a degree of cooperation, a set of policies put forward 0:18:14.040 --> 0:18:16.680 attempting to address this, and this is the latest example 0:18:16.720 --> 0:18:19.320 of that. One example also is the designations we had 0:18:19.760 --> 0:18:23.920 a few months ago on Antiful linked groups, targeting them 0:18:24.000 --> 0:18:26.520 for their terrorist activity, labeling them when they are as 0:18:26.680 --> 0:18:29.080 terrorist groups they are, and making sure we have that 0:18:29.200 --> 0:18:31.840 counter terrorism strategy to protect the American people. 0:18:33.160 --> 0:18:36.160 Tommy, the existence of this conference and bringing together all 0:18:36.160 --> 0:18:39.680 these countries seems to answer a question or an observation 0:18:39.760 --> 0:18:42.040 that many of us have had, and that is when 0:18:42.080 --> 0:18:45.160 we've seen some far left political violence in this country, 0:18:46.040 --> 0:18:50.120 it has felt like there has been ties to other countries. 0:18:50.160 --> 0:18:52.560 It has felt like this isn't just sort of isolated 0:18:52.560 --> 0:18:54.760 in a community that there must be some sort of 0:18:54.800 --> 0:18:58.040 international component to this. The fact that you're pulling all 0:18:58.040 --> 0:19:01.919 these countries together seems to confirm them. At when you 0:19:02.000 --> 0:19:05.600 invited the other countries, was there a resistance or did 0:19:05.600 --> 0:19:08.320 they readily admit, yeah, we think this is a problem too. 0:19:08.359 --> 0:19:10.280 How much convincing did you have to do to get 0:19:10.320 --> 0:19:12.359 these people in the building next week? 0:19:13.760 --> 0:19:16.520 Well, this is certainly a problem that other countries identified 0:19:16.560 --> 0:19:18.800 because they face as well, and so we have seen 0:19:19.320 --> 0:19:22.359 a degree of cooperation, and it really was just about 0:19:22.359 --> 0:19:25.159 making sure that we're continuing the cooperation we've had on 0:19:25.200 --> 0:19:28.320 the law enforcement and working level, continuing that through this 0:19:28.480 --> 0:19:31.920 ministerial and then onward from this ministerial as we continue 0:19:31.920 --> 0:19:35.760 to address these terrorist groups. So really we're seeing other 0:19:35.800 --> 0:19:37.600 countries from across the world, and we're going to see 0:19:37.600 --> 0:19:40.600 countries there from the Western Hemisphere, from Europe, from Asia 0:19:40.680 --> 0:19:43.280 that are all coming together because they also face this threat, 0:19:43.520 --> 0:19:46.680 and we do see these groups operating in a transnational 0:19:46.680 --> 0:19:49.959 way that does require these countries to come together, all 0:19:50.000 --> 0:19:52.280 of us to come together first to understand the full 0:19:52.359 --> 0:19:55.159 nature of the threat, because we see examples across the 0:19:55.200 --> 0:20:00.040 world of an antifa linked groups doing terrorist activity. We 0:20:00.040 --> 0:20:01.720 need to communicate, we need to make sure that we're 0:20:01.760 --> 0:20:05.240 working with law enforcement partners other countries to fully understand 0:20:05.520 --> 0:20:08.679 this ecosystem and then address it because we're seeing this resurgence. 0:20:08.880 --> 0:20:10.520 So that's part of what this is. Making sure that 0:20:10.560 --> 0:20:13.160 we fully understand and know the scope of the threat, 0:20:13.280 --> 0:20:15.119 but that we're seeing with our own eyes, and then 0:20:15.160 --> 0:20:18.320 also taking that coordinated action with our allies and partners 0:20:18.320 --> 0:20:20.199 to address these groups, to make sure that we're not 0:20:20.280 --> 0:20:23.160 doing things by ourselves or in ways that aren't coordinated, 0:20:23.359 --> 0:20:26.399 but having that full effective strategy to protect the American people, 0:20:26.440 --> 0:20:28.600 and also that our allies and partners can also be 0:20:28.640 --> 0:20:29.200 safe as well. 0:20:30.200 --> 0:20:32.720 Tommy, has the US government, is it, to your knowledge, 0:20:32.800 --> 0:20:38.160 ever engaged in this level of international coordination and cooperation 0:20:38.320 --> 0:20:41.640 to try to bite back against this sort of international violence. 0:20:42.680 --> 0:20:46.480 Well, this is truly a historic ministerial. We saw some 0:20:46.880 --> 0:20:48.560 going to the fact that this is not a new threat. 0:20:48.880 --> 0:20:51.520 From back into the seventies, for example, we saw examples 0:20:51.560 --> 0:20:54.119 of farup terrorism and really since then, but we really 0:20:54.160 --> 0:20:58.240 haven't seen in the decades since this coherent strategy to 0:20:58.280 --> 0:21:01.199 address what is this pressing threat. And of course we 0:21:01.240 --> 0:21:04.159 have counter terrorism efforts on a whole variety of different 0:21:04.200 --> 0:21:07.159 elements to address terrorist threats where we see them. But 0:21:07.240 --> 0:21:09.960 there really has been a gap in previous decades and 0:21:10.080 --> 0:21:13.199 addressing far left political terrorism. So we are going to 0:21:13.200 --> 0:21:15.280 make sure that we're addressing this threat as well. We're 0:21:15.320 --> 0:21:16.880 not going to turn a blind eye to it. Quite 0:21:16.880 --> 0:21:19.600 the opposite. So it truly is a historic ministerial. We're 0:21:19.600 --> 0:21:22.040 seeing many, many countries from around the entire world coming 0:21:22.080 --> 0:21:24.680 together to work on this, and it is the continuation 0:21:24.720 --> 0:21:26.680 of that effort, like I mentioned of other meetings we've 0:21:26.680 --> 0:21:27.320 had on this too. 0:21:28.560 --> 0:21:30.520 So Tommy, when you have all these countries in the 0:21:30.600 --> 0:21:33.840 room and you get this kind of cooperation, I assume 0:21:34.119 --> 0:21:37.639 that what could come out of it is unlocking certain 0:21:37.720 --> 0:21:41.560 kinds of investigatory tools, you know, ways to cooperate on 0:21:41.640 --> 0:21:44.879 sharing intelligence. Is that sort of what you're hoping for here? 0:21:45.240 --> 0:21:48.119 A lot more information sharing that would help law enforcement 0:21:48.160 --> 0:21:49.640 in all the countries that are included. 0:21:50.880 --> 0:21:52.600 That is certainly a part of this. And I think 0:21:52.640 --> 0:21:55.320 also understanding how we approach these groups in a coherent 0:21:55.359 --> 0:21:57.679 way means how do we even define these groups? I mean, 0:21:57.720 --> 0:22:00.320 we have different countries approaching terrorism threats in different ways. 0:22:00.400 --> 0:22:02.160 Is it a law enforcement issue, is it a national 0:22:02.240 --> 0:22:04.840 security issue? It truly is a national security issue and 0:22:04.880 --> 0:22:07.879 a law enforcement issue, But what organization within the country 0:22:08.000 --> 0:22:09.840 is even taking a lead on this that even making 0:22:09.840 --> 0:22:12.520 sure we're coordinated on that element is very important for 0:22:12.640 --> 0:22:15.320 the information sharing you just mentioned, but also making sure 0:22:15.320 --> 0:22:18.280 we can act quickly prevent threats, for prevent to violent 0:22:18.320 --> 0:22:21.640 actions before they happen. So that coordination, that law enforcement 0:22:21.960 --> 0:22:24.159 element is a key part of this, and that actually 0:22:24.160 --> 0:22:26.800 does stem from the law enforcement working groups we've had 0:22:26.800 --> 0:22:28.920 with our allies and partners on this. So that'll be 0:22:28.960 --> 0:22:31.200 a continued effort to make sure we're having that coordination 0:22:31.240 --> 0:22:33.920 on the law enforcement front, on the national security front 0:22:33.960 --> 0:22:34.919 to address these groups. 0:22:35.840 --> 0:22:38.480 Ad voiser hearing on the Scott Jennings Show Tommy Piggott. 0:22:38.480 --> 0:22:41.440 He's a top spokesperson at the US State Department, discussing 0:22:41.480 --> 0:22:45.480 this upcoming counter terrorism summit that will take place next 0:22:45.480 --> 0:22:48.640 week in Washington, Secretary of State Rubio bringing together more 0:22:48.640 --> 0:22:52.680 than sixty countries to talk about how to fight transnational 0:22:53.160 --> 0:22:56.240 left wing violence. Tommy, some of the violence that we 0:22:56.320 --> 0:22:59.560 have seen on the streets of America, I assume is 0:22:59.600 --> 0:23:02.520 going to be part of the discussion next week. Let's 0:23:02.520 --> 0:23:04.879 focus in for a moment on funding. I think we 0:23:04.960 --> 0:23:08.240 often look at the people who are involved, But when 0:23:08.240 --> 0:23:11.200 you see this violence that's happened here and in other countries, 0:23:11.400 --> 0:23:13.720 somebody is funding it. You know money is coming in 0:23:13.760 --> 0:23:18.800 from somewhere to fuel these terrorists. Is it Your belief 0:23:18.880 --> 0:23:21.159 is that the government's belief that money is coming in 0:23:21.160 --> 0:23:24.480 from overseas and finding its way into the hands of 0:23:24.560 --> 0:23:25.920 terrorists on the streets of America. 0:23:27.200 --> 0:23:30.840 Well, we've certainly seen threats against US law enforcement, US interests, 0:23:31.320 --> 0:23:33.600 and target attempts to target the American people and the 0:23:33.680 --> 0:23:35.760 US government as well as our allies and partners on this. 0:23:36.400 --> 0:23:38.800 That is why the designation's tool is so important when 0:23:38.840 --> 0:23:41.080 it comes to designating these groups as what they are, 0:23:41.119 --> 0:23:43.879 as terrorist groups, because it does open up more tools 0:23:43.880 --> 0:23:46.600 when it comes to addressing their financing. For example, it 0:23:46.720 --> 0:23:50.159 lets us go after institutions, groups, people, whatever it is 0:23:50.200 --> 0:23:53.080 that are offering material support to these groups. So it 0:23:53.119 --> 0:23:55.320 opens up a whole

structure.rationale: The episode follows a clear throughline from the assassination threat to Air Force One vulnerabilities to Iran's broader posture, with multiple expert voices (McCabe, Swan, Harward, Kudlow) providing organized segments, though transitions between topics like Strait of Hormuz oil flow feel loosely connected.

relevance.rationale: The episode is sharply focused on a specific, timely national security development — Israeli intelligence warning of an Iranian assassination plot against Trump, the Air Force One plane switch in Turkey, and the security implications of the Qatar-gifted aircraft — all directly tied to current geopolitical events.

clipability.rationale: Several moments stand alone well — particularly McCabe's 'this is not the time to ride in the fancy plane' and Swan's observation that Trump's fear of assassination is making him a more homebound president — both are quotable and require minimal setup.

card.tagline: Israel warned us: Iran has a specific plot to kill Trump mid-flight
card.description: Why Trump quietly swapped planes in Turkey, what Israeli intelligence revealed, and whether the Qatar-gifted Air Force One can actually protect him.
card.reason: Because this episode breaks down the week's most urgent presidential security story

structure.rationale: The conversation drifts organically between Strategy liquidation fears, trader migration to AI stocks, and Ethereum Foundation drama without clear segment breaks or deliberate signposting, making it feel like an unedited roundtable.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers timely crypto/Bitcoin market stress (Strategy/Saylor MNAV risk, Ethereum Foundation cuts) and AI-adjacent mining deals, but remains niche and doesn't connect to broader news/politics beyond the crypto ecosystem.

clipability.rationale: The 'Sorosification of Strategy' framing and the 'if we die, we die' Bitcoin spot holder quip are genuinely self-contained and quotable, though most exchanges require prior context to land fully.

card.tagline: Is someone trying to deliberately collapse Strategy and shake out Saylor's Bitcoin?
card.description: Hosts debate the 'Sorosification' theory targeting MicroStrategy's cap structure, Ethereum Foundation's 40% budget cut, and why crypto traders are fleeing to AI stocks.
card.reason: Because this episode breaks down the biggest stress test Strategy has ever faced

scored.description: AM is Australia's most informative morning current affairs program. With key political interviews and stories about the Australian way of life, AM sets the agenda for the nation’s daily news and current affairs coverage.

structure.rationale: Classic morning news magazine format with clearly delineated reporter packages and host-to-correspondent handoffs, though the excerpt shows abrupt transitions between unrelated stories without explicit signposting or segment framing.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers multiple breaking and substantive political stories — Lindsey Graham's death and its implications for Trump's Senate strategy, a Royal Commission into antisemitism at Australian universities, conflicting Strait of Hormuz reports, an ISIS-linked Australian woman's return, and affordable housing policy — all timely and news-driven.

clipability.rationale: The Lindsey Graham segment contains several self-contained, quotable moments — particularly the 'Senate whisperer' framing and the recap of Graham calling Trump 'the most flawed nominee in Republican Party history' — that would work as standalone previews with minimal context.

card.tagline: Lindsey Graham dead, Hormuz in doubt, and ISIS woman cleared to return
card.description: ABC AM covers Graham's shock death and its impact on Trump's Senate strategy, plus Australia's antisemitism royal commission opens fire on universities.
card.reason: Because this episode covers today's most consequential breaking political stories

transcript.scoringWindow: It's my pleasure to give some accolades to Mayor Raider. She has been fantastic and she's the most enthusiastic mayor. Hey, Carolyn, Senator Amy Grady here, congratulations on your retirement and thank you for dedicating so much time and energy to the people of Ripley. You're such a wonderful leader of the community in so many ways, from your years of service and education through your years as the mayor. Your servants art definitely shows for the Ripley community. In addition to my well wishes, my girls Easton and Chesney want to add we love you, Carolyn. Congratulations my friend. Hi. I'm Roger Hanshaw, Speaker of the Western House of Delegates. For most of my life I've lived in Clay, West, Virginia, but I've always said if I were going to move anywhere else in the state, I'd like to move to Ripley. It's a wonderful community, full of wonderful people who love West Virginia, and that's due in no small part to the work that Mayor Raider has put in during her nineteen years leading one of Westerni's most beautiful communities. So I'm happy to wish Mayor Raider well in her retirement, and we hope that she is still engaged in the community for the betterment of our state. Congratulations Mayor. In all the years I've been alive, no one has been a better champion for Ripley than Mary Raider. She has been the city's cheerleader from the moment she entered office. Every time she's gone into a room where she's had the chance to extol the virtues of Ripley, She's done it loudly and proudly. She has brought our town a lot of pride, a lot of positivity, and some very cool surprises, from the Budweiser Clydesdale's to hot air balloons. Ripley's grown a lot in the last nineteen years, a lot of it because a Marror Raider. We're known for the town of parades now, which I think is a cool thing. Marror Raider is always out there. She's always gives one hundred and ten percent. I go buy sometimes seven o'clock in the morning, she's already in office. To Mayor Raider and my friend Carolyn, thank you for being the most successible, approachable mayor a city could ever hope. structure.rationale: The episode is organized as a sequential string of tributes with brief host introductions, giving it a loose but repetitive structure that lacks a throughline beyond the occasion itself.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers a local political figure's retirement with tributes from state and federal officials, which has regional civic relevance but no connection to timely or broadly significant news/political developments.

clipability.rationale: Most moments are warm but formulaic tributes that require listener familiarity with Mayor Rader to land; Joe Manchin's closing remarks are the closest thing to a standalone quotable moment.

card.tagline: West Virginia leaders line up to say goodbye to a 19-year mayor
card.description: Local, state, and federal officials — including Joe Manchin and Shelley Moore Capito — pay tribute to Ripley Mayor Carolyn Rader on her final day in office.
card.reason: Because this episode captures a rare farewell moment in small-town American politics

scored.description: Philippine Politics Now is your essential guide to the complex, fast-paced, and ever-evolving world of Philippine governance, policy, and power dynamics. Everyday, we pull back the curtain on the headlines that matter, breaking down intense political debates, legislative battles, and grassroots movements into clear, actionable insights. Featuring candid interviews with lawmakers, journalists, academics, and key newsmakers, we explore how policy decisions made in Manila echo across the archipelago and the global stage. Tune in to understand the why behind the what in Philippine politics today.

latestEpisode.title: Philippine Politics Now — Evening for July 13 2026
