structure.rationale: The episode follows a clear evening-briefing format with an intro, segmented news blocks, a good/bad news framing device, and a closing synthesis, though the transitions between segments feel formulaic and the pacing is slightly uneven.

relevance.rationale: The episode is sharply focused on three concurrent Philippine political developments — the Sarah Duterte impeachment trial, a super typhoon emergency response, and a defense attaché recall from Beijing — all timely and substantive on the day of publication.

clipability.rationale: The framing around digital forensics and hash values in a high-profile impeachment trial produces several self-contained, quotable moments, though most require at least minimal context about the Philippine political landscape to land fully.

Welcome to the Mix 92.3 R&B Playback Podcast! 🎙️

Detroit's favorite R&B station just got even better—on demand.

Listen in for the best moments from the Steve Harvey Morning Show and Keith Sweat’s Sweat Hotel, plus exclusive celebrity interviews hosted by Mix 92.3’s own Cheron and Bushman bringing you the inside on music, culture, and real talk.

From Keith’s wild confessions, to Shirley's juicy Strawberry Letter, and Nephew Tommy's legendary prank calls, we’ve got your mornings and nights covered. 🎧

Missed it live? Want to play it back? Hit follow and take the Mix 92.3 experience with you, wherever you go.

structure.rationale: The episode jumps erratically between Iran deal analysis, Lindsay Graham speculation, a lengthy mid-segment product advertisement for bovine colostrum gummies, and teasers for upcoming segments, with no clear throughline or deliberate pacing.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers timely geopolitical topics — Iran nuclear negotiations, Ukraine war, Lindsay Graham's reported death, and Israeli lobbying in U.S. defense legislation — but is anchored in Alex Jones's conspiratorial framing rather than substantive news analysis.

clipability.rationale: The Lindsay Graham death speculation and the 'fight to the last Lindsay Graham' quip are punchy standalone moments, but most content requires surrounding context or is buried inside a long commercial read.

card.tagline: Lindsay Graham dead, Iran stalemate, and who's really running the war?
card.description: Alex Jones reacts to Lindsey Graham's reported death in Ukraine, argues U.S.-Iran talks are a sham, and claims Israel is funding domestic propaganda through the defense bill.
card.reason: Because this episode reacts to a breaking political death and active war diplomacy

Take some zany and serious journeys with The Pipeman aka Dean K. Piper, CST on The Adventures of Pipeman also known as Pipeman Radio syndicated globally “Where Who Knows And Anything Goes”.

Listen to & Watch a show dedicated to motivation, business, empowerment, inspiration, music, comedy, celebrities, shock jock radio, various topics, and entertainment. The Adventures of Pipeman is hosted by Dean K. Piper, CST aka “The Pipeman” who has been said to be hybrid of Tony Robbins, Batman, and Howard Stern. The Adventures of Pipeman has received many awards, media features, and has been ranked for multiple categories as one of the Top 6 Live Radio Shows & Podcasts in the world.

Pipeman Radio also consists of multiple podcasts showing the many sides of Pipeman. These include The Adventures of Pipeman, Pipeman in the Pit, and Positively Pipeman and more. You can find all of the Pipeman Podcasts anywhere you listen to podcasts. With thousands of episodes that focus on Intertainment which combines information and entertainment there is something for everyone including over 5000 interviews with celebrities, music artists/bands, authors, speakers, coaches, entrepreneurs, and all kinds of professionals.

Then there is The Pipeman Radio Tour where Pipeman travels the country and world doing press coverage for Major Business Events, Conferences, Conventions, Music Festivals, Concerts, Award Shows, and Red Carpets. One of the top publicists in music has named Pipeman the “King of All Festivals.” So join the Pipeman as he brings “The Pipeman Radio Tour” to life right before your ears and eyes.

The Adventures of Pipeman Podcasts are heard on The Adventures of Pipeman Site, Pipeman Radio, Talk 4 Media, Talk 4 Podcasting, iHeartRadio, Pandora, Amazon Music, Audible, Spotify, Apple Podcast, Google Podcasts and over 100 other podcast outlets where you listen to Podcasts. The following are the different podcasts to check out and subscribe to:

• The Adventures of Pipeman
• Pipeman Radio
• Pipeman in the Pit
• Positively Pipeman

Follow @pipemanradio on all social media outlets
Visit Pipeman Radio on the Web at linktr.ee/pipemanradio , theadventuresofpipeman.com, pipemanradio.com, talk4media.com, w4cy.com, talk4tv.com, talk4podcasting.com

Download The Pipeman Radio APP

Phone/Text Contact – 561-506-4031

Email Contact – dean@talk4media.com  

The Adventures of Pipeman is broadcast live Wednesdays at 1PM ET and Music & Positive Interviews daily at 8AM ET on W4CY Radio (www.w4cy.com) and replays on K4HD Radio (www.k4hd.com  – Hollywood Talk Radio part of Talk 4 Radio (www.talk4radio.com) on the Talk 4 Media Network (www.talk4media.com). The Adventures of Pipeman TV Show is viewed on Talk 4 TV (www.talk4tv.com).

The Adventures of Pipeman Podcast is also available on www.theadventuresofpipeman.com and www.pipemanradio.com,  Talk 4 Media (www.talk4media.com), Talk 4 Podcasting (www.talk4podcasting.com), iHeartRadio, Amazon Music, Pandora, Spotify, Audible, and over 100 other podcast outlets.

My hope is that this 11 00:00:40.200 --> 00:00:43.719 will save future critics from embarrassment and at the same 12 00:00:43.759 --> 00:00:48.240 time fuel a real and substantial debate on these important matters. 13 00:00:49.039 --> 00:00:52.880 For analogy, we will look to the discipline physics, or 14 00:00:52.880 --> 00:00:56.119 at least a simplified version of how physics research is 15 00:00:56.159 --> 00:01:02.079 often understood. Mainstream economics has long sufffered from physics envy 16 00:01:02.479 --> 00:01:05.319 and attempted to become the physics of the social sciences, 17 00:01:05.760 --> 00:01:10.680 so the analogy is not entirely untethered from reality. Physics 18 00:01:10.879 --> 00:01:15.719 is a powerful and accurate, if not real, predictive science. 19 00:01:16.159 --> 00:01:20.000 Whose success rests on the adoption of Pauperian falsification. At 20 00:01:20.000 --> 00:01:23.719 the core, it is an empirical science that follows what 21 00:01:23.840 --> 00:01:28.200 is commonly referred to as the scientific method. As such, 22 00:01:28.760 --> 00:01:33.040 physics research, at least is commonly understood, follows the well 23 00:01:33.079 --> 00:01:38.879 known sequence of scientism, formulating hypotheses, collecting data, and then 24 00:01:39.159 --> 00:01:44.480 testing those hypotheses against data. If praxeology is construed as 25 00:01:44.519 --> 00:01:48.519 a method for predictive science, much like physics, then the 26 00:01:48.560 --> 00:01:53.239 obvious critique would be its lack of falsifiability. After all, 27 00:01:53.680 --> 00:01:58.640 economic theory developed using praxeology is not tested against data, 28 00:01:59.400 --> 00:02:02.799 so ask how can we know that it is true? 29 00:02:03.640 --> 00:02:07.959 Leaving aside the fact that falsificationism cannot generate truth, but 30 00:02:08.080 --> 00:02:13.879 only leaves unfalsified hypotheses at some point rhetorically elevated to theory, 31 00:02:14.479 --> 00:02:20.639 praxeologically derived economic laws may at first glance appear unsubstantiated, 32 00:02:21.199 --> 00:02:25.400 but this conclusion is a mistake. Many critics of Austrian 33 00:02:25.439 --> 00:02:30.039 economics appear to be misled by how mainstream economists often 34 00:02:30.080 --> 00:02:35.599 appear to test economic theory using data. These economists make 35 00:02:35.719 --> 00:02:39.360 quantified predictions about the real world, much in the same 36 00:02:39.479 --> 00:02:43.639 sense as physics makes predictions about atoms or black holes. 37 00:02:44.520 --> 00:02:47.599 It is therefore easy to jump to the conclusion that 38 00:02:47.680 --> 00:02:52.080 Austrian economics also fits this same mold. But it does not, 39 00:02:53.120 --> 00:02:57.800 and the physics analogy illuminates how praxeology is different. While 40 00:02:57.879 --> 00:03:01.439 rarely mentioned, physics is not not only or purely an 41 00:03:01.439 --> 00:03:05.159 empirical science. I do not mean that there is theoretical 42 00:03:05.199 --> 00:03:10.599 physics too, but that physics in Toto relies heavily on mathematics. 43 00:03:11.400 --> 00:03:16.439 Without mathematics, physics would be impotent. But mathematics is not 44 00:03:16.560 --> 00:03:20.639 subject to falsification. It is accepted at face value and 45 00:03:20.719 --> 00:03:24.560 assume true. We thus have several different parts of physics, 46 00:03:24.919 --> 00:03:28.840 the body of physics theory specifically which seeks to explain 47 00:03:28.919 --> 00:03:34.759 correlations in historical already recorded phenomena and predict future outcomes, 48 00:03:35.240 --> 00:03:39.639 and the assumed flawless framework of mathematics. How does this 49 00:03:39.719 --> 00:03:44.479 apply to Austrian economics. Our economic theory does not pretend 50 00:03:44.560 --> 00:03:47.800 to be predictive in the sense that the natural sciences are, 51 00:03:48.400 --> 00:03:53.960 but is descriptive. It uncovers causalities, not mere correlations, in 52 00:03:54.000 --> 00:03:59.240 the social world, but cannot predict outcomes with quantifiable precision. 53 00:04:00.000 --> 00:04:04.360 For example, economic theory uncovers the fact that the more 54 00:04:04.400 --> 00:04:08.439 of a good of equal usability a person has, the 55 00:04:08.520 --> 00:04:15.080 lower his marginal valuation of this good, aka diminishing marginal utility. 56 00:04:15.360 --> 00:04:19.279 This is formally a predictive statement, because we know that 57 00:04:19.800 --> 00:04:23.160 all else equal, the value of a good to some 58 00:04:23.399 --> 00:04:28.920 person all persons will fall with increased quantity and vice versa, 59 00:04:29.839 --> 00:04:32.319 and that therefore means they are also willing to give 60 00:04:32.399 --> 00:04:37.160 up less to acquire it the law of demand always, 61 00:04:38.120 --> 00:04:40.360 but we do not know by how much they will 62 00:04:40.399 --> 00:04:44.920 reduce their valuation, so we cannot predict exactly how prices 63 00:04:45.000 --> 00:04:49.000 therefore will change. Obviously, this means that it is a 64 00:04:49.000 --> 00:04:53.480 fundamental mistake to assess economic theory based on the quantitative 65 00:04:53.480 --> 00:04:57.480 predictions that it does not and cannot make, and the 66 00:04:57.519 --> 00:05:00.120 predictions that it does make, such as in the ca 67 00:05:00.120 --> 00:05:05.399 case of diminishing marginal utility, cannot be falsified because they 68 00:05:05.399 --> 00:05:11.000 are one necessarily true and two not a matter of 69 00:05:11.040 --> 00:05:16.040 predicting quantities. Critics have a hard time grasping that economic theory, 70 00:05:16.399 --> 00:05:20.439 to Austrians is not like the mass of unfalsified hypotheses 71 00:05:20.759 --> 00:05:24.240 that physicists and other natural scientists prefer to call theory, 72 00:05:25.079 --> 00:05:29.240 economic theory economic law is much more akin to the 73 00:05:29.319 --> 00:05:33.959 unassailable math that physics and physicists depend on, and just 74 00:05:34.000 --> 00:05:38.879 as physics applies math to produce hypotheses, economics applies economic 75 00:05:38.959 --> 00:05:43.920 theory to generate specific pronouncements about the world. Those pronouncements, 76 00:05:44.120 --> 00:05:47.759 which depend on making accurate interpretations of the particular data 77 00:05:47.839 --> 00:05:53.480 and circumstances, can then be tested. Austrian economists, as opposed 78 00:05:53.480 --> 00:05:57.879 to mainstream economists, never suffered from physics envy, and therefore 79 00:05:58.279 --> 00:06:03.160 never attempted to become a quantityiveatively predictive science. In fact, 80 00:06:03.560 --> 00:06:07.120 this muddying of the waters by mainstream economists is a 81 00:06:07.199 --> 00:06:12.839 constant Austrian critique of what today is called economics, because 82 00:06:12.839 --> 00:06:16.800 the subject matter, the economic phenomena that arise from people's 83 00:06:16.839 --> 00:06:22.240 behavior directed by their subjective valuations, cannot be studied inductively 84 00:06:22.600 --> 00:06:26.800 or leaning heavily on empiricism. In fact, as its use 85 00:06:26.839 --> 00:06:31.680 of mathematics shows, even physics cannot. The sooner critics of 86 00:06:31.759 --> 00:06:36.560 Austrian economics in general and praxeology in particular realize what 87 00:06:36.639 --> 00:06:39.639 it is for and how it is used, the sooner 88 00:06:39.680 --> 00:06:42.240 they will be able to engage us in a constructive 89 00:06:42.279 --> 00:06:47.600 conversation about the nature and promise of social science. For 90 00:06:47.680 --> 00:06:51.600 more content like this, visit meses dot org.

structure.rationale: The essay follows a clear argumentative throughline — analogy setup, critique of critics, physics comparison, resolution — with consistent pacing typical of a read academic article.

relevance.rationale: This episode is a philosophical defense of Austrian economics methodology (praxeology vs. empiricism) with no connection to current news, legislation, or political events.

clipability.rationale: The diminishing marginal utility passage is the closest thing to a standalone moment, but most segments require the surrounding argument to land meaningfully.

card.tagline: Why Austrian economists say their theory can't be falsified — and why that's fine
card.description: A philosophical defense of Misesian praxeology, arguing economic law is more like mathematics than empirical hypothesis — unfalsifiable by design, not by flaw.
card.reason: Because this episode challenges how economic theory is evaluated and validated

structure.rationale: The excerpt meanders across prison sentencing, sexual assault prosecution, education reform, and AI without clear segment breaks, though a loose interview throughline keeps it from being entirely formless.

relevance.rationale: The episode engages substantively with criminal justice disparities, prosecutorial discretion, and libertarian-vs-government philosophy through a sitting DA, giving it a clear political/policy throughline despite the conversational format.

clipability.rationale: Several genuinely standalone moments — 'I wake up a libertarian, I go to bed an authoritarian' and 'now that I'm part of the government, it's even more terrifying' — land as self-contained, quotable hooks without requiring setup.

card.tagline: A sitting DA admits: being inside government made her trust it less
card.description: West Texas DA Sarah Stogner and comedian Jesse Payton expose sentencing absurdities, prosecutorial trade-offs, and why working in government turns you libertarian.
card.reason: Because this episode interrogates how the justice system actually works from the inside

structure.rationale: The episode follows a clear news-brief format with distinct story segments and a listener-suggested story section, though transitions are abrupt and pacing is uneven across topics.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers specific current political and policy developments — Trump crypto controversies, nurses strikes, LGBTQ civil verdicts, geothermal energy policy, and reproductive healthcare legislation — giving it clear news/politics relevance with timely hooks.

clipability.rationale: Several segments — particularly the Massachusetts nurses strike and the Virginia defamation verdict — contain self-contained, quotable summaries that could stand alone as previews without much setup.

card.tagline: Nurses strike, crypto scandals, and geothermal jobs — today's left-leaning news roundup

scored.description:
Jeffrey Epstein was a multi millionaire who had political and business ties to some of the most rich and powerful people in the world. From businessmen to politicians at the highest levels, Epstein broke bread with them all.

Yet for years the Legacy media and the rest of high society looked the other way and ignored his behavior as multiple women came forward with allegations of abuse.

Even after he was convicted and subsequently received a sweetheart deal those same so called elites welcomed him back with open arms.

Now after his death and the arrest of Maxwell, the real story is starting to come together and the curtain has begun to be drawn back and what it has revealed is truly disturbing.

From Princes to Ex Presidents, the cast of scoundrels in this play spans continents and political affiliations leaving us with a transcontinental criminal conspiracy possibly unlike any we have ever seen before.

In this podcast we will explore all of the levels of Jeffrey Epstein and his criminal enterprise.

From his most trusted assistants to obscure associates, we will leave no stone unturned as we swim through the muck searching for clarity and answers to some of the most pressing questions of the case.

From interviews with people directly involved in the case to daily updates, the Epstein Chronicles will have it all.

Just like our other project, The Jeffrey Epstein Show, you can expect no punches pulled and consistent content. We have covered the Epstein case daily(everyday since October 1st 2019) and will continue to do so until there are convictions. With a library of well over 1k shows, you can expect a ton of content coming your way including on scene reporting from the Maxwell trial and from places like Zorro Ranch.

Thank you for tuning in and I look forward to having you all along for the ride.




(Created and Hosted by Bobby Capucci)

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-epstein-chronicles--5003294/support.

transcript.scoringWindow: WEBVTT 00:00:00.000 --> 00:00:04.000 Welcome to Podcasting QA, brought to you by the people at Buzz Sprout. 00:00:09.679 --> 00:00:15.919 I'm your host, Cara Passetti, and I'm bringing you the best tips and strategies to keep you podcasting with confidence. 00:00:16.079 --> 00:00:20.800 Today's question is: do I really need a website for my podcast? 00:00:21.039 --> 00:00:26.879 First, I want to remove any and all pressure that comes with the thought of adding a website. 00:00:27.120 --> 00:00:32.960 This might feel like just one more thing to pack onto your already full podcasting plate. 00:00:33.119 --> 00:00:34.399 But I have some good news. 00:00:34.560 --> 00:00:41.200 A little over half of popular podcast hosting platforms actually provide a website with their hosting plan. 00:00:41.359 --> 00:00:45.439 So implementing a website may be much easier than you initially thought. 00:00:45.600 --> 00:00:53.119 I'd like to share three reasons how a podcasting website levels up your show, and then we can determine if it's truly a need. 00:00:53.280 --> 00:00:56.719 I want you to think of your website as your podcast home on the internet. 00:00:57.039 --> 00:01:16.079 Instead of sending listeners to multiple locations where they can hear your episodes like Spotify and Apple Podcasts or maybe even social media platforms, you can send them one link to your website, and that actually has everything available in one convenient spot and then let your listeners choose what listening platform works best for them. 00:01:16.319 --> 00:01:21.760 Since your website is the home base for your podcast, it's important to know what to include in that home base. 00:01:22.079 --> 00:01:26.640 So I want to break down some of the components of a really great podcast website. 00:01:26.799 --> 00:01:42.640 Typically, a podcasting website will include all of your episodes, your episode details like show notes, chapters, artwork, and more, your episode transcripts, if supported, and links to follow or subscribe through your favorite listening directories. 00:01:42.799 --> 00:01:46.879 Your website becomes the central hub where your audience can explore your content. 00:01:47.040 --> 00:01:56.079 And if you choose to build your own website or work with a host that gives you customization controls, then the best part is you get control over that experience. 00:01:56.239 --> 00:02:03.280 When you have control over those customization options, you want to focus on making your podcasting website really specific to you. 00:02:03.439 --> 00:02:07.760 Show off your personality and make sure that it fits your overall vibe of your podcast. 00:02:07.920 --> 00:02:13.680 Of course, you'll want to include the ability to not only display your episodes, but also play them on your website. 00:02:13.840 --> 00:02:20.639 Buzzprout websites include our built-in player, which can actually be customized to match the look and feel of your podcast. 00:02:20.800 --> 00:02:24.159 You'll want to provide a way of connecting with you as the podcaster. 00:02:24.319 --> 00:02:34.400 A website can give listeners an easy way to engage with you either by using email, comments, or message board, or even a direct message communication option like fan mail. 00:02:34.560 --> 00:02:40.240 Your podcasting website can highlight your hosts, your guest, and all of the contributors assigned to your podcast. 00:02:40.319 --> 00:02:45.039 But you'll even have the option of assigning each guest to the episodes that they're on. 00:02:45.199 --> 00:02:49.360 And then you'll see that list of episodes when you visit their page on your website. 00:02:49.520 --> 00:02:56.960 Your website should also include a follow page, of course, where listeners can subscribe easily to your podcast using their favorite listening directory. 00:02:57.120 --> 00:03:05.360 And last but certainly not least, we recommend including your social media links so that your listeners can easily join your community and the discussion around your podcast. 00:03:05.520 --> 00:03:10.800 A fun feature that we offer here at Buzzsproute for your podcasting website is actually PodRole. 00:03:10.960 --> 00:03:16.560 So PodRoll allows you to link to some of your favorite podcasts right on your podcasting website. 00:03:16.719 --> 00:03:24.719 And since podcasting is so easily spread through word of mouth, this is kind of like a virtual word of mouth recommendation for your favorite podcast. 00:03:24.960 --> 00:03:39.120 Since the goal here is to make this website personal to your podcast and avoid anything generic, you'll also want to make sure you are customizing things like the color scheme, the background image, the fonts you are using, and even the URL you are using for your website. 00:03:39.280 --> 00:03:46.080 If you have purchased your own domain name, we recommend go ahead and change the URL for your site to match that domain. 00:03:46.240 --> 00:03:50.080 This is building identity and branding around your podcast. 00:03:50.319 --> 00:03:58.400 I feel like this question about having a podcasting website naturally leads to the follow-up question: is the podcast host provided website enough? 00:03:58.560 --> 00:04:00.400 Or should I be building my own? 00:04:00.639 --> 00:04:08.879 Shoot us some fan mail if you'd like for me to dive a little deeper into this question and explore some of the pros and cons for building your own custom website. 00:04:09.039 --> 00:04:16.399 Whether you're using a website provided by your podcast host or creating something custom, a website helps to build your brand. 00:04:16.560 --> 00:04:21.199 A website isn't just about listening, it's about growing something bigger than your podcast. 00:04:21.360 --> 00:04:28.079 When you have a dedicated space on the internet, it gives you the opportunity to build brand identity, it becomes searchable. 00:04:28.240 --> 00:04:34.000 And as you grow, this will help with discoverability and even lead to some sponsorship opportunities. 00:04:34.240 --> 00:04:41.920 You can promote products or services that you offer through your website, and you can even turn listeners into subscribers and super fans. 00:04:42.079 --> 00:04:47.199 Your podcast website is essentially working as a marketing engine for everything else you're building. 00:04:47.439 --> 00:04:56.480 Even if you're not monetizing yet, having the foundation of a website early on makes a huge difference later down the road, depending on your goals and how they evolve. 00:04:56.639 --> 00:05:04.720 If you have questions about your podcasting website, you can reach out to us on the podcaster success team by emailing support at buzzsprout.com. 00:05:04.879 --> 00:05:12.319 If there's a question you'd like to answer on a future podcasting QA episode, send it to me by clicking the send us a text link in the show notes. 00:05:12.480 --> 00:05:16.959 Remember, your question could help hundreds of other podcasters just like you. 00:05:17.040 --> 00:05:18.319 So don't keep it to yourself. 00:05:18.480 --> 00:05:19.279 Just send it in. 00:05:19.439 --> 00:05:22.079 Be sure to join us every Monday to kickstart your week. 00:05:22.240 --> 00:05:23.519 Thank you so much for listening. 00:05:23.600 --> 00:05:26.000 And as always, keep podcasting.

transcript.scoringWindow: would mutiny on himself. 00:00:19.359 --> 00:00:25.359 Why on earth would anyone choose to open with a leaping, flying, roundhouse kick to the head? 00:00:25.519 --> 00:00:26.559 That's beyond me. 00:00:26.719 --> 00:00:33.600 Even if you aren't coming off a five-year layoff, that's an incredibly high risk reward move to attempt. 00:00:33.759 --> 00:00:38.240 I mean, surely Connor's coaches advised him to get going with the jab first. 00:00:38.560 --> 00:00:53.359 That's typically what most striking fighters would do, regardless of whether they're throwing hands only in a boxing or bare knuckle, or even if they're a Muay Thai or Dutch kickboxing style specialist, you almost always start out with a jab. 00:00:53.520 --> 00:01:01.759 If you're gonna choose to lead with a kick, well, why not maybe start out with a low kick to your opponent's thigh or calf? 00:01:02.000 --> 00:01:07.680 At least make sure you know you have your range finder attuned to judge distance well. 00:01:07.840 --> 00:01:15.280 Head kicks are always risky for the person throwing it because you are, by definition, putting yourself off balance. 00:01:15.519 --> 00:01:19.599 The canvas can be slippery, so your support leg can easily slip. 00:01:19.680 --> 00:01:23.599 And if you miss that head kick, you might wind up throwing yourself way off balance. 00:01:23.840 --> 00:01:33.920 Or, of course, that the kicking leg can be caught by the opponent and then they can just drive you down into the ground, like Josh Van did to Alex Pantoja. 00:01:34.239 --> 00:01:42.159 Now, a flying head kick is even more risky because both feet are off the ground and including the support leg, right? 00:01:42.239 --> 00:01:44.959 And then that's how Connor injured himself. 00:01:45.120 --> 00:01:48.879 It's when he landed back on his support leg, which was his right leg. 00:01:49.040 --> 00:02:01.040 I mean, nor I guess the normal injury I would expect to see when somebody lands from a flying kick is for that support leg to crumple and maybe have they would roll their ankle, come over on their ankle. 00:02:01.200 --> 00:02:06.879 But it's not easy to tell exactly from the camera angle that the UFC gave us. 00:02:07.040 --> 00:02:13.280 It's you can't really tell exactly where the weight distribution was when Connor landed on his right foot. 00:02:13.520 --> 00:02:21.759 Now, the key thing is whenever you're throwing some type of flying kick or any kick for that matter, the mechanics of the support leg are critical. 00:02:22.159 --> 00:02:34.400 You want to be loose enough so that your whole leg can be like a shock absorber, but you also have to be loose enough so that you could pivot on the ball of your foot of your supporting leg if you needed to. 00:02:34.560 --> 00:02:41.599 That's why you see those of us, even for just those of us who throw hands, we like to skip a lot, jump rope a lot. 00:02:41.919 --> 00:02:48.800 Tie boxers and kickboxers also spend a fair amount of time doing jump rope work, and that's precisely why. 00:02:48.960 --> 00:02:54.240 Because you want to get used to being on the balls of your feet and being very light on your feet. 00:02:54.400 --> 00:03:16.400 All fighters have to practice having feet that are feet-like hands in a sense that we can adjust, we can fine-tune these adjustments between a gliding motion where your foot is just skimming the surface of the canvas to planting your feet and having your weight solid so that you can get good leverage from the ground up for a punch or for a throw. 00:03:16.560 --> 00:03:21.120 So, of course, the rumor mill is running amok as far as Connor's injury goes. 00:03:21.360 --> 00:03:24.400 People have decided right away that he tore his ACL. 00:03:24.719 --> 00:03:34.319 Don't quite see that injury happening from landing on his right foot the way he did, because an ACE, the ACL, the anterior cruchet ligament, cruchet cross. 00:03:34.560 --> 00:03:40.479 There's actually ligaments that cross behind inside the knee, behind the kneecap, to stabilize everything. 00:03:40.639 --> 00:03:51.039 And typically that can get torn when imagine you have your leg straight out in front of you, and then you keep your thigh still and you then try to bring your toes backwards. 00:03:51.199 --> 00:03:53.199 Like your leg does not bend that way, right? 00:03:53.280 --> 00:03:59.039 It bends from your heel to your rear end, it doesn't bend from your toe towards your hip, the front of your hips. 00:03:59.199 --> 00:04:01.919 So that's how you tear your ACL. 00:04:02.000 --> 00:04:04.479 It's from that extension of the leg. 00:04:04.719 --> 00:04:14.319 When you twist or land awkwardly like that, that's typically what gets torn, is what's called the medial ligaments, the ones that are on the outside. 00:04:14.639 --> 00:04:26.319 It's never fun to tear a knee ligament, but as you guys may or may not know, tearing your ACL is just unfortunately a more devastating injury than tearing the medial ligament. 00:04:26.560 --> 00:04:28.560 Both would likely require surgery. 00:04:28.639 --> 00:04:33.199 It's just that the ACL takes a little, takes longer to recover from. 00:04:33.439 --> 00:04:37.920 So my I'm not convinced yet that Connor tore his ACL. 00:04:38.160 --> 00:04:43.199 The UFC just left it vague and they said he had an interior knee injury. 00:04:43.439 --> 00:04:53.199 So, in addition to ligament and a ligament being torn, Connor could have torn the meniscus, the inside of his kneecap, or had damage done to that. 00:04:53.360 --> 00:04:55.279 So, why did this injury occur? 00:04:55.439 --> 00:05:07.759 Well, the only thing, the only conclusion is it occurred when he threw that very first flying roundhouse kick to the head and landed back down on his right foot, which was the uninjured side, right? 00:05:07.920 --> 00:05:12.480 Connor previously had that titanium shin bone implanted into his left leg. 00:05:12.639 --> 00:05:22.240 Now, from the footage showing Connor landing on his right foot, again, it's hard to tell what the weight distribution is when he's him when his foot actually makes contact with the ground. 00:05:22.399 --> 00:05:27.839 But remember, he's got the weight of his body mass coming down on that foot. 00:05:27.920 --> 00:05:37.920 And that's because Connor's body mass is accelerating down towards the center of the earth, which increases the amount of force with which his foot hits the canvas. 00:05:38.160 --> 00:05:44.800 Now, on top of that, the pressure generated is the amount of force divided by the area. 00:05:44.959 --> 00:05:51.040 So the say the number of square inches or centimeters that Connor's foot is in contact with the canvas. 00:05:51.360 --> 00:05:53.600 And it is not his whole foot. 00:05:53.680 --> 00:05:56.720 You would not want to land flat footed from a kick like that. 00:05:56.879 --> 00:06:00.959 So you've got a greater amount of force coming down a very small area. 00:06:01.199 --> 00:06:09.680 So if Connor's mechanically incorrect, meaning his technique isn't perfect on landing, I can easily see how something could tear. 00:06:09.920 --> 00:06:19.439 I will 100% leave it to the kickboxing experts to evaluate Connor McGregor's landing technique off of that leaping kick. 00:06:19.680 --> 00:06:32.800 There's only one more little piece of intrigue that I'd like to add to the uh question of his injury, and that is did Connor's PED use over the last five-year span have anything to do with the injury? 00:06:33.040 --> 00:06:54.319 Athletes who are involved in sports that rely on very heavy PED use, such as bodybuilding and to some extent strength competitions such as powerlifting, those athletes know full well that the danger of using PEDs is that you can develop the muscular strength faster than you can develop ligament strength. 00:06:54.560 --> 00:07:01.680 There are two completely different types of tissues, and the ligaments get stronger with more, as we say, time under tension. 00:07:01.920 --> 00:07:07.120 So now I'm not an expert on the details of PED use either. 00:07:07.360 --> 00:07:24.319 So I do not know if whatever Connor took could actually weaken his ligaments, but I do know that depending on how much weight training he was doing, it's possible that his ligament strength relative to his musculature could be lowered. 00:07:24.480 --> 00:07:32.160 Remember, there was a period of time where Connor was posting these pics of himself on social media where he really bulked up. 00:07:32.399 --> 00:07:34.959 I think he admits fully that he was enhanced. 00:07:35.199 --> 00:07:46.480 So was there some type of imbalance created between his superdeveloped musculature and his relatively speaking underdeveloped ligaments? 00:07:46.639 --> 00:07:50.639 And did that play a part in this latest injury of his? 00:07:50.879 --> 00:08:03.920 Now, finally, in terms of uh Connor's injury from this leaping head kick, the biggest question is why on God's Green Earth would throw such a risky technique as your opening move? 00:08:04.160 --> 00:08:08.959 The only riskier kick Connor could have chosen would be to throw a flying sp. 00:08:09.680 --> 00:08:25.279 Maybe Connor figured if he could head kick Max with his titanium shin bone, he could get the fight over with in one move, and that would become, you know, a completely viral clip on social media. 00:08:25.519 --> 00:08:29.279 Unfortunately for Connor, his landing has become the meme. 00:08:29.439 --> 00:08:33.759 So the singular question remains: what exactly is Connor's injury? 00:08:33.919 --> 00:08:38.240 And I say it's going to be up to Connor to let us know what he wants us to know. 00:08:38.399 --> 00:08:45.039 He left the UFC building ASAP, did not want to get seen by their doctors on site. 00:08:45.200 --> 00:08:55.919 I'm sure Connor wants to get checked out by his own personal medical staff, and he will let us know the details that he feels best serve his narrative. 00:08:56.159 --> 00:09:02.720 Regardless, that too short main event did not take away from how great the rest of that card was. 00:09:02.879 --> 00:09:06.720 So tell me what your favorite moment from UFC 329 was. 00:09:06.879 --> 00:09:09.840 I look forward to reading your comments and reading your fan mail. 00:09:10.000 --> 00:09:10.320 Thank you.

00:14:01.519 --> 00:14:03.600 So that's what I strive to do when I assign. 00:14:03.759 --> 00:14:22.080 Like I assign uh 43 college teams, umpires for 43 college teams, and I try to, you know, uh put maybe a less strong person with a really strong person, just to kind of balance that out and help train that other individual. 00:14:22.320 --> 00:14:28.960 Um, at the high school level, we really haven't been doing that much, but we will be from now on because we've had a change. 00:14:29.120 --> 00:14:35.279 But um, do you have a different scheduling philosophy when it comes to the matchups on the games? 00:14:35.519 --> 00:14:35.840 Yes. 00:14:36.080 --> 00:14:37.279 Well, I do. 00:14:38.080 --> 00:14:40.879 But in years past, we haven't had that at that level. 00:14:41.039 --> 00:14:42.080 We will have that now. 00:14:42.399 --> 00:14:46.320 Because you are well aware of who the who the top matchups are each. 00:14:46.879 --> 00:14:47.440 Absolutely. 00:14:47.679 --> 00:14:54.559 And you know, in in in in the LL, it I it it hurts me to say this, but on any given night, think about it, Chris. 00:14:54.639 --> 00:14:58.960 There's probably what, four, five big matchups on any given night. 00:14:59.039 --> 00:14:59.440 That's it. 00:14:59.600 --> 00:15:08.159 Every other game is yeah, so what I've been striving to do, and what is gonna this year and the next year, it's gonna get a lot better. 00:15:08.799 --> 00:15:15.360 We we have a we we do have a lot of good umpires, but we need to get those good umpires paired on those good games. 00:15:15.440 --> 00:15:15.679 Yeah. 00:15:15.840 --> 00:15:23.600 And that after that one debacle we had where it was like three games in a night that were just awful, we've made those changes. 00:15:23.759 --> 00:15:27.279 So you will see more and more of better umpires on big games. 00:15:28.080 --> 00:15:33.759 I mean, you know, you got Solanko E Town, but you can't have you gotta have two good umpires on that. 00:15:33.840 --> 00:15:35.759 So that's where we're headed. 00:15:36.480 --> 00:15:44.559 Um, you got anything while I scroll through my list of questions? 00:15:45.279 --> 00:15:47.519 Um I mean, I always have great. 00:15:48.000 --> 00:15:48.480 I got one. 00:15:48.559 --> 00:15:52.720 So what rule in the book would you and I got this? 00:15:52.879 --> 00:15:54.639 I got two questions now that I thought about it. 00:15:55.039 --> 00:15:57.519 What rule would you like would you change? 00:15:58.480 --> 00:16:01.759 Is there if any, would you change any of the rules? 00:16:02.480 --> 00:16:09.360 Well, the only thing that I would do, given that I do three different rules codes, I would put the rules codes all the same. 00:16:09.679 --> 00:16:14.720 So we have different pitching rules for USA versus high school versus college. 00:16:15.440 --> 00:16:20.320 So as a kid grows up, we're teaching them one way, and then all of a sudden they need to change. 00:16:20.480 --> 00:16:24.480 And that's why we see the illegal pitch pitching situations that we have. 00:16:24.639 --> 00:16:26.559 Because you know in high school you're allowed to step back. 00:16:26.720 --> 00:16:28.799 We sure can't do that in college, right? 00:16:29.120 --> 00:16:34.240 So I I just and there's other rules too that I wish they would all just be this be the same. 00:16:34.480 --> 00:16:45.039 Yeah, because I've seen that that's actually a really good point because I've seen that, especially with the pitching, like with different positions and different, and someone will call something, and I'm like, where I I've never heard that before. 00:16:45.200 --> 00:16:46.639 Like, what is that rule? 00:16:46.879 --> 00:16:59.360 And the problem is, you know, with something like that, and if a pitcher's not trained, you can see they're they're like kind of rattled, especially at the young ages, like and you could see them getting all off, like with their mechanics, uh, because then they're trying not to get in trouble. 00:16:59.600 --> 00:17:01.360 And you can't change something like that in a game. 00:17:01.600 --> 00:17:05.200 So you basically you want to like basically a universal rule book across the board. 00:17:05.440 --> 00:17:05.599 Yes. 00:17:05.759 --> 00:17:12.960 I I would like it if all of our rules, because it's difficult to train umpires because we all do multiple levels because there just aren't enough of us. 00:17:13.119 --> 00:17:13.440 Right. 00:17:14.000 --> 00:17:19.039 And so one one day I could do a USA softball game, the next day do a high school game, and the next day do a college game. 00:17:19.119 --> 00:17:20.880 I got three different sets of rules codes. 00:17:21.039 --> 00:17:21.440 Yeah. 00:17:21.680 --> 00:17:23.519 So I mean, I'm not that bright. 00:17:23.839 --> 00:17:24.400 You're gonna confuse them. 00:17:25.039 --> 00:17:25.599 I'm not that bright. 00:17:26.079 --> 00:17:31.759 So uh I that's what I would I mean, there are some rules that I usually say. 00:17:31.839 --> 00:17:36.960 You know what, I don't make the rules, I just enforce them, but none are coming to me right now. 00:17:37.359 --> 00:17:41.359 So that uh that leads me to a specific rule. 00:17:42.240 --> 00:17:46.319 What is the book definition of an infield fly? 00:17:46.559 --> 00:17:47.920 Ah, yes, that's a good one. 00:17:48.160 --> 00:17:48.880 An infield fly. 00:17:49.440 --> 00:17:54.799 You probably don't remember, but when I was back in Rec, I had an issue with you on an infield fly call. 00:17:55.119 --> 00:17:55.920 Well, you've been holding on the call. 00:17:56.079 --> 00:17:56.799 I do not remember. 00:17:59.599 --> 00:18:00.880 You've never had an issue with this. 00:18:01.519 --> 00:18:02.799 It was not like an argument or nothing. 00:18:02.880 --> 00:18:07.839 I just didn't agree with you, did you call Did you set this recording up tonight just to bring that to bring this question up? 00:18:08.160 --> 00:18:10.240 I think it just popped in my head when we were talking about rules. 00:18:10.400 --> 00:18:16.400 Well, but see, the thing about Chris is I know he looked at the rules book, so I can't just BS him because he looked. 00:18:16.559 --> 00:18:16.640 Yeah. 00:18:16.799 --> 00:18:18.799 And he'll say, I can't find a rule for that. 00:18:19.039 --> 00:18:20.880 Why are you looking at the rule book? 00:18:21.119 --> 00:18:23.039 Um, the infield fly rule. 00:18:23.200 --> 00:18:25.920 Okay, that this that's a great question. 00:18:26.559 --> 00:18:31.359 It's in play when there's either runners on first and second or basis loaded less than two outs, right? 00:18:31.519 --> 00:18:31.839 Yep. 00:18:32.079 --> 00:18:43.759 We call infield fly when there is a pop in the infield, pop fly in the infield within the confines of the infield that can be caught with relative ease. 00:18:44.079 --> 00:18:45.839 And what's it normal? 00:18:46.000 --> 00:18:49.119 Well, there we go with that subjectivity again. 00:18:49.519 --> 00:18:58.160 Um I base that on the level that I'm at, right? 00:18:58.240 --> 00:18:58.400 Yeah. 00:18:58.559 --> 00:19:02.240 So a college kid's gonna catch a lot more than a 10-year-old is. 00:19:02.640 --> 00:19:07.039 So if I feel like that 10-year-old is not gonna get to that ball, I'm not calling it, right? 00:19:07.200 --> 00:19:10.240 The other thing that comes into play is the weather, the wind, whatever. 00:19:10.480 --> 00:19:18.240 Just I always always have to keep in mind that the infield fly is a rule that protects the offense. 00:19:18.480 --> 00:19:18.720 Right. 00:19:18.880 --> 00:19:19.119 Right? 00:19:19.279 --> 00:19:21.119 It is there to protect the offense. 00:19:21.440 --> 00:19:28.559 So I'm not gonna call it if it's a ball that I think might get down, because I'm not because then I'm giving the defense an out. 00:19:28.799 --> 00:19:29.039 Yeah. 00:19:29.200 --> 00:19:33.599 So, but it's the whole thing is with normal effort. 00:19:33.759 --> 00:19:34.000 Right. 00:19:34.160 --> 00:19:36.799 The play needs to be made with normal effort. 00:19:37.119 --> 00:19:39.599 Not like a diving awesome catch. 00:19:39.839 --> 00:19:40.079 Correct. 00:19:40.160 --> 00:19:42.240 So and it has to be in the field of play. 00:19:42.480 --> 00:19:47.039 And it's because I I I've seen it where as soon as that ball goes up in the air, into a fly. 00:19:47.279 --> 00:19:47.440 Absolutely. 00:19:47.680 --> 00:19:51.839 It's like without even without even glancing down to see what actions the infield's taking. 00:19:52.160 --> 00:19:52.480 Correct. 00:19:52.640 --> 00:19:56.799 We are taught to not make that call till the apex of the pop-up. 00:19:57.119 --> 00:19:57.359 Okay. 00:19:57.519 --> 00:19:58.319 So you shouldn't be. 00:19:58.799 --> 00:20:00.079 And you're not following the ball the whole way. 00:20:00.319 --> 00:20:00.799 You gotta look at it. 00:20:00.960 --> 00:20:02.319 You should not be following the ball. 00:20:02.480 --> 00:20:06.079 But we should umpires, and this is what we teach, you shouldn't ever follow the ball. 00:20:06.240 --> 00:20:09.279 The kid will take you to where the ball's going, right? 00:20:09.519 --> 00:20:15.920 Just like when I'm behind a plate and there's a pop-up that goes and I have no freaking idea where it is, I just watch the catcher's shoulder. 00:20:16.000 --> 00:20:17.440 She'll take me to it, right? 00:20:17.599 --> 00:20:24.480 So any pop-up, I watch the kid, I'm not watching the ball because there's a whole lot that can happen down here when I'm watching that ball. 00:20:25.119 --> 00:20:30.559 And that the the obstruction, the interference, those are the things we need to be aware of, not watch the ball. 00:20:30.720 --> 00:20:36.240 So when the ball goes up, I know it's in the infield, I just watch players and then I make my determination. 00:20:36.400 --> 00:20:38.880 But I don't say it as soon as the ball's hit, you know. 00:20:39.200 --> 00:20:42.240 Yeah, my my mine with you, mine with you was what was it? 00:20:42.400 --> 00:20:58.799 It was we were on our JV field, and it was a pop-up to the third towards the third baseman, and the it was the wind was blowing decent, and it was carrying her further and further out towards the outfield, and you called infield fly, and I'm like, that was not normal effort. 00:20:58.960 --> 00:21:02.640 Like she had to so that was my only disagreement with you. 00:21:02.880 --> 00:21:03.440 You were right then. 00:21:03.599 --> 00:21:04.000 You were right. 00:21:04.160 --> 00:21:06.559 That was my only in all these years, my only disagreement. 00:21:06.799 --> 00:21:09.119 If it was not normal effort, you're you were right. 00:21:09.279 --> 00:21:10.319 I I blew it. 00:21:10.559 --> 00:21:15.359 But yeah, that's my least favorite rule is the drop third strike. 00:21:15.599 --> 00:21:20.640 The the drop third strike just gets very frustrating to me, probably as a pitcher's dad. 00:21:20.799 --> 00:21:40.799 Um, probably it's just it's but like it's just a frustrating rule because and

scored.description: Welcome to 'ASX BRIEFS,' the definitive podcast for enthusiasts, investors, and professionals keen on staying ahead of the curve in the Australian Stock Exchange (ASX). Hosted by Andrew Musgrave, 'ASX BRIEFS' delves deep into the heart of Australia's financial markets, bringing you insightful conversations with the minds shaping the future of investing down under. Each episode, join Andrew as he interviews a diverse lineup of fund managers, executives, and industry insiders, offering you a unique blend of expert analysis, strategic insights, and the latest trends affecting the ASX. Whether you're a seasoned investor or just starting out, 'ASX BRIEFS' is your go-to source for comprehensive updates and thought-provoking discussions designed to inform, inspire, and empower your investment journey. Tune in to 'ASX BRIEFS' and take the pulse of Australia's financial markets right at your fingertips.

transcript.scoringWindow: Andrew Musgrave Welcome again to ASX Brief. And today we're joined by Phil Hodgson, the Managing Director and CEO of Calix Limited, an Australian industrial technology company developing platform solutions for industrial decarbonisation across cement, steel, alumina, critical minerals, as well as solutions in water treatment and carbon dioxide removal. Phil, great to have you with me today and welcome to the ASX Briefs Podcast. Phil Hodgson Thanks very much, Andrew. Andrew Musgrave Phil, for listeners who are new to Calix, can you explain the core platform technology, what it does, and why it matters for heavy industry? Phil Hodgson Certainly, Andrew. So, the core platform technology is a technology developed here in Australia. It's essentially around a new type of kiln or furnace, a new way to heat stuff up, as we say. A lot of today's minerals, cement, steel, etc., are made in furnaces and Calix has a new way to make these products. Essentially, what we do is we separate how you heat from what you heat. We do that with a rather large steel tube. The biggest tubes we've made are now as big as we'll ever make them, 1.8 meters in diameter and over 30 meters high. So, we're already a full scale on the core technology. We heat that tube to over a thousand degrees centigrade. So, this tube's glowing red hot. And whatever we heat goes down the middle of the tube. It's going to be a small particle size, much like sort of fine powder. So, iron ore fines, cement, sort of meal, all of absolutely the right size for our technology. That basically gets dropped down the tube. And as the dust or small particles floats down the tube, the red-hot walls of the tube radiate into the particles and heats them up and we can heat the tube any way you like. We can heat it with renewable energy, we can heat it with traditional fossil fuels on the outside of the tube and so the technology represents a way to heat stuff up that can use all sorts of different energy sources. But the other interesting thing is inside the tube, a lot of people may not realize that, say limestone, which is used to make cement, is nearly half by weight CO2. And so, when the cement and lime industries heat up that limestone, the CO2 comes out of the rock, and that's over half the emissions of the cement and lime industry. And so, when you're heating limestone in our tube, that CO2 doesn't escape. It doesn't head off into the atmosphere or get mixed with furnace gases. It comes out the top of the tube as a pretty pure stream. And so, the first applications of the technology we developed were to separate out CO2 when making lime and cement. But of course, since then we've gone on to look at all sorts of different applications, including our latest applications in making green iron, in which case we've got hydrogen inside the tube, which converts the iron ore to iron. So, you can see it's quite a versatile core technology platform that basically, as I say, is replacing the traditional kiln or furnace in all sorts of different industries. Andrew Musgrave And industrial decarbonisation is one of the hardest problems in climate. Sectors like cement and steel account for a significant share of global emissions and have few easy levers to pull. So why is Calix's approach particularly well suited to solving what others can't? Phil Hodgson Yeah, if you sort of think back to the tube, I described heated externally, it's actually a fairly simple, or we like to term it elegant solution. There are no moving parts, there's no complex pieces of equipment. It's basically a tube that's being heated externally. Of course, there's a lot of know-how that goes into developing the full-scale versions of this. But that's our first key advantage. The core concept is simple and elegant. The other advantage is, of course, decarbonisation, depending upon where you are in the world, is developing at different pieces. In some parts of the world, it's moving ahead, in other parts it's not. So, we need a technology that's going to be cost effective today, regardless of if there's a carbon price. And so, if you recall, I mentioned the energy flexibility of the tube, the ability to heat with electrons or heat with traditional fossil fuels or burning waste is actually one of the things that could make the technology cost effective today. So, you sort of get the decarbonization as a free ride or a cherry on top to economic solutions today with the tube. So, I think that's what separates us from other technologies attempting to achieve this. Andrew Musgrave Now, in April this year, the company announced the restructure of the lithium midstream project joint venture with PLS, another ASX listed company. Can you walk us through the logic of the deal? What does Calix give up? What does it retain? And why is this outcome strategically the right one for the company at this point in time? Phil Hodgson Yeah, this is an interesting one. We started our journey with PLS over three years ago, and that was to take a core, our core technology and use it in the lithium industry. PLS dig up spodumene, and we're using our core technology to take advantage of cheap energy, as in renewable energy at a mine site, to extract and concentrate lithium at PLS's site in the Pilbara. and so originally, we went into this deal as a joint venture partner with equity, us 45%, PLS 55%. but as the industry structurally changed and margins tightened on lithium, and that's the difference between the spodumene price and the lithium price, it became harder for us to participate in this simply because of the capital required. And so, we changed, we agreed to PLS to change the structure of the deal where we back out effectively our equity. We recovered all of the equity that we're put in, and PLS moved forward with carriage of the project, licensed from us. And so, the solution, which is the ability to produce lithium at a mine site with renewable energy, is jointly owned by us and PLS. And moving forward, we'll be sharing the margins on that when applied to third parties. But as part of the deal, PLS get a free license. So, so we got a capital back, PLS get a free license. So, the logic is that there's no downside for us. We're not exposed from a capital perspective. There's only upside with respect to license income from third parties, and we get paid engineering fees. It's really part of a capital light strategy that we're very focused on and doubling down on to some extent now as well. So that's the logic behind the deal. Andrew Musgrave Now turning to Leilac Technology. Leilac Limited is Calix's cement and lime application subsidiary. So, for our listeners who may not be familiar, can you explain what Leilac does and specifically why the ability to separate processed CO2 in cement is such a difficult and valuable problem to solve? Phil Hodgson Yeah. So, I described previously, I guess, our tube, the core part of our technology that we that we heat from the outside. And when we're processing cement, which is mainly limestone, that gets dropped down the middle of the tube and as that heats up, the CO2 comes out of the rock. And so that CO2 in a normal cement kiln gets mixed with furnace gases and ejected into the atmosphere. So separating CO2 from a whole lot of other gas is really difficult. You need big chemical plants to solve that problem and with our technology though, because the CO2 is trapped in the tube, it exits the top of the tube as a pretty pure stream. And so, the core technology has the ability to directly separate that CO2 that's coming out of the rock. And so that's what makes it a little different. We're not trying to separate a gas from a gas, we just don't allow the gases to mix in the first place. And of course, the ability to heat the tube, say with renewable electrons, you can see how we can start to get to very low to zero emissions cement using our technology. Andrew Musgrave And Leilac's hybrid heating system has the potential to lower fuel costs for cement producers independent of any carbon price. How important is that dual value proposition reducing costs now while enabling carbon capture later in convincing cement majors to commit to the technology? Phil Hodgson Well, I'd say it's critical. If you've in terms of the early part of the podcast, we talked a little bit about decarbonisation and the different pace at which different parts of the world are chasing decarbonisation. Some areas certainly stalled. So, if you have a technology that has a value proposition today without a price on carbon, then that can allow some of these cement majors to move forward on looking at the technology and committing to the technology. And they get the carbon and decarbonisation down the track as the cherry on top. So, the hybrid heating system, this is the ability of our tube to be heated in any way you like. We can have renewable electrons on the outside of the tube. We can have gas burning normal gases like fossil fuels, or we can burn waste. And so, the ability to switch between electrons, which may be expensive in some parts of the day, but cheap in other parts of the day, switching between those electrons and a standard fuel or even a waste fuel, which in some cases is very cheap to negative cost, that is what enables the technology to be economic today. And so, the hybrid heating system is a very important part of the technology that allows it to be economic today. Andrew Musgrave And you've landed a joint development agreement with the Adani Group's Ambuja Cements, one of the world's top cement producers, and it's a significant commercial milestone. So, how did this partnership come about and why has Leilac been selected as a strategic partner? Phil Hodgson Yeah, so we've been in discussion with many cement producers over several years, Adani among them. Adani, of course, became a major cement producer overnight with the purchase of Holcim's assets in India. They instantly became number two cement group in India and so in discussion with Adani and numerous other cement players, they've become

structure.rationale: The excerpt meanders from the West Texas cult story to Taco Bell diarrhea jokes to a parasite outbreak map to a prosecutor guest segment, with no clear throughline or deliberate segmentation — classic loose talk-radio format.

relevance.rationale: The episode touches on a real local story about a cult leader exploiting vulnerable women in West Texas and features a prosecutor discussing the justice system, giving it loose political/civic relevance, but the content is primarily conversational radio banter with no substantive policy or legislative focus.

clipability.rationale: A few quotable one-liners exist (the 'tell politicians the truth and they'll never believe you' gag, the sex offender math joke) but most moments require surrounding context or are mid-riff and wouldn't land as standalone previews.

card.tagline: A West Texas cult leader is recruiting desperate women — and prosecutors can't stop him yet
card.description: A local prosecutor explains why a cult preying on destitute mothers in West Texas remains untouchable, and what it takes to finally bring charges.
card.reason: Because this episode covers a local predatory scheme with no legal remedy yet

scored.description: Podcast by Alaskans for Sustainable Budgets. Our top 3 issues each week in the world of Alaska oil, gas & fiscal policy.

structure.rationale: The episode follows a numbered segment format ('Weekly Top 3') with a clear transition between topics, though the discussion within segments is conversational and occasionally rambling.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers specific Alaska state budget mechanics, oil revenue thresholds, K-12 education funding disputes, and LNG legislation — clearly tied to current state political developments but limited to a regional audience and niche policy detail.

clipability.rationale: Most moments require significant context about Alaska's oil revenue waterfall structure or specific legislative history to land, with only a few punchy lines — like the 'I want my two dollars' riff — that could stand alone.

card.tagline: Alaska's schools want their money — but the math just doesn't add up
card.description: Two Alaska policy analysts dissect why the Dunleavy administration can't release K-12 oil revenue funds yet, and whether the education lobby's outrage is theater.
card.reason: Because this episode breaks down a live Alaska budget fight over K-12 funding

Connect with us at podcast@cityofredding.org or on Facebook, Twitter or Instagram @cityofredding.

latestEpisode.title: Freedom Festival 2026: What You Need to Know

transcript.scoringWindow: Im Katie. And Im Steve, and this is the City of Reading Podcast. Today were talking about one of Readings biggest community traditions, the Freedom Festival Fireworks Celebration, happening Saturday, July 4th. Theres been some questions about how recent decisions involving the Reading Civic Auditorium impact the celebration. So lets clear that up right away. The Freedom Festival is still happening just like usual. This event is hosted by the City of Reading with fireworks generously paid for by the McConnell Foundation. And yes, the firework show is taking place at the Reading Civic Auditorium Grounds, just like usual. The event is not hosted by the Civic, so recent decisions regarding the building do not affect this years Freedom Festival and shouldnt affect it in years to come. Weve also heard some confusion between the Freedom Festival and the new drone show being hosted by the City of Shasta Lake. These are two separate events. Readings Freedom Festival will feature the traditional fireworks show our community knows and loves. The good news is that the Shasta Lake Drone Show is occurring on Friday, July 3rd, so by all means, enjoy both of them. The Freedom Festival is a full evening of activities, so lets talk about what people can expect. Concessions open at 5 p.m., giving attendees plenty of time to grab food and settle in before the entertainment begins. Live music starts at 7 p.m. with the Serena Caris band. Therell be a short intermission around 8 p.m. and the band will continue through the evening. As we get closer to the fireworks show, concessions and live entertainment will conclude at 9.30 p.m. This allows staff, vendors, and event crews to transition safely into the fireworks portion of the evening. The countdown will begin at 9.59 p.m. and the fireworks show begins promptly at 10 p.m. Because of the summer heat, we encourage everyone to plan their visit accordingly. You dont need to arrive early and spend the entire day outside. Consider coming later in the afternoon. Bring water and prepare for the weather. But since we know people like to come early to secure a good spot, lets talk about the process. Saving or reserving spots on the lawn is not allowed. Please dont use stakes, tape, or other items to hold a space. Any items used to reserve spots will be removed by staff. However, you are free to come early, set up, and hang out. As long as youre occupying the space, its yours to have. Pop-up tents are allowed, but they must come down when the sun sets. This helps protect everyones view of the fireworks and maintains clear sightlines throughout the event. Now lets talk about some important event reminders to help everyone have a safe and enjoyable evening. This is a family event, so no alcohol is left. Pets are also not allowed at the event. That includes dogs, cats, snakes, and other pets. Service animals are allowed, but we do want to share a reminder from our event staff. Every year there are situations where a service animal becomes frightened by the fireworks and loud sounds and runs away, creating a stressful situation for the attendee, the animal, and the staff helping to respond. Fireworks are extremely loud and can be upsetting for many animals. If you have a service animal, please consider whether attending a large fireworks event is the best environment for them. Parking is always one of the biggest questions. One great option is using Raba Shuttle Service. Raba will provide free shuttle transportation with designated pickup and drop-off locations before and after the event. The Park Marina 44 Overpass Bridge will close once parking lots reach capacity. The estimated closure time is about 7.30 p.m., but timing may vary depending on attendance. The bridge will remain closed until police provide the all-clear and after RABA has completed its initial shuttle runs. The goal is to clear as many pedestrians as possible before the flow of traffic out of the event begins. A reminder that RVs are not allowed in the parking lots, even in the parking lots in the very back of the event. This helps preserve parking availability for event attendees and supports the safety requirements around the fireworks perimeter. Now lets talk food. There will be approved food vendors at the event located in the designated vendor area. Approved vendors will have designated badges, identify them as part of the event. The city does not endorse or approve vendors outside of the designated vendor area. A few other important reminders. The Sundial Bridge will close at 9 p.m. on July 4th and will reopen after the event when its safe to do so. If you plan to cross the Sundial Bridge before the event, please make sure you do it before it closes. And after the fireworks show, you may hear additional shells being fired. This is a normal safety procedure as crews release any live shells that did not go off during the scheduled fireworks show. Every year people are alarmed when extra fireworks go off, but this is completely normal. The Freedom Festival is a community tradition, and we want everyone to have a safe and enjoyable night. So please plan ahead, bring what you need, follow event guidelines, and be patient with traffic and crowds. Thousands of people come together each year to celebrate, and a little preparation helps everyone have a better experience. For more information, including parking, shuttle details, and event updates, please visit the City of Readings Freedom Festival webpage at cityofreading.gov slash Freedom Festival. And one last important reminder before the celebration. So sit back, enjoy the evening, and let the professionals handle the show. Thanks for listening, and well see you there on July 4th.

structure.rationale: Clear sequential organization moving from event overview to schedule to rules to parking to food, with a consistent host-dialogue format and identifiable close.

relevance.rationale: Hyper-local municipal event logistics for a single city's July 4th celebration; no connection to broader news or political developments.

clipability.rationale: Nearly every segment is a list of logistical details that requires the surrounding context of 'this is a local July 4th event guide' to make sense as a standalone clip.

card.tagline: Everything Redding residents need to know before July 4th fireworks
card.description: City of Redding hosts clarify Freedom Festival logistics — parking, shuttle routes, tent rules, and the post-show shell-firing that surprises crowds every year.
card.reason: Because you want practical details before attending Redding's Freedom Festival

relevance.rationale: The episode engages substantively with specific current political dynamics — primary determinism, DSA electoral gains, gerrymandering, and Hamas-adjacent political figures — though the analysis leans more toward ideological commentary than breaking news coverage.

clipability.rationale: The explanation of why 90% of House seats are decided in primaries — not general elections — is a genuinely self-contained, quotable passage that could stand alone as a preview clip without much setup.

card.tagline: Why your general election vote may already be irrelevant before it's cast

card.reason: Because this episode explains a structural flaw shaping every congressional race

structure.rationale: The episode moves from a lengthy ad read into a loosely organized discussion that incorporates an audio clip from another show, but transitions are abrupt and the conversation meanders without clear signposting or a defined close.

relevance.rationale: The episode centers on a specific, timely political scandal — rape allegations against Maine gubernatorial candidate Graham Platner and whether he will formally withdraw before a ballot deadline — making it substantively on-topic for News/Politics listeners.

clipability.rationale: The segment speculating about a potential deal between Platner and the Maine AG — 'you step down, we won't charge you with rape' — is a genuinely standalone, provocative moment, but most of the surrounding discussion requires context about who Platner is and what preceded it.

card.tagline: Did Maine's AG cut a secret deal to keep Platner off rape charges?
card.description: Hosts dissect rape allegations against Maine Democratic candidate Graham Platner, a reported AG deal, and whether he'll actually file withdrawal paperwork by Monday's deadline.
card.reason: Because this episode breaks down a fast-moving state-level political scandal

More than forty years hosting shows, with most of those years in North Texas, have established him as a foremost conservative voice both locally and nationally.

scored.feedTitle: The Dana Show with Dana Loesch – Politics & Culture Commentary
scored.description:
The Dana Show with Dana Loesch is a daily conservative news and political commentary podcast delivering clear, fearless analysis of today’s biggest headlines. Each weekday, Dana Loesch breaks down American politics, culture, media bias, and government overreach with sharp insight and unapologetic conservative principles.From Second Amendment news and free speech debates to media hypocrisy and Washington dysfunction, The Dana Show cuts through the noise to explain what’s really happening—and why it matters. If you’re looking for common-sense political analysis, independent conservative commentary, and a daily news podcast that challenges the mainstream narrative, The Dana Show with Dana Loesch is your go-to source.

https://www.radioamerica.com/the-dana-show/
https://heliosmedia.us/dana-show/

scored.feedTitle: Answer Engine Optimization (AEO): The AI Search Podcast
scored.description:
Answer Engine Optimization (AEO) is how your brand gets cited, recommended, and surfaced inside ChatGPT, Perplexity, Google AI Overviews, and Claude. This is the daily podcast for marketers, founders, and SEOs who want their brand to be the answer AI engines give.
Each episode breaks down a new AEO tactic, a real algorithm change, or a brand that just won (or lost) visibility inside AI search. Topics include: how ChatGPT decides which brands to recommend, how Perplexity chooses its sources, how Google AI Overviews differ from traditional SERPs, how to structure content for LLM citation, schema strategies for answer engines, and the emerging field of Generative Engine Optimization (GEO).
Brought to you by AEO Engine — the platform brands use to monitor, measure, and grow their AI search visibility. Whether you're a B2B marketer, DTC founder, or in-house SEO, this podcast turns the daily chaos of AI search into a concrete playbook you can execute on.
New episode every morning. Transcripts on every episode. Subscribe to stay ahead of how AI engines rank and recommend brands.

[Host] Today we’re digging into something that’s been buzzing across the industry: Neil Patel’s G.E.O. tactics ROI mapping. I’ve got Marcus Reid here — ex-Google Ads, ex-failed martech founder, now an analyst who actually reads the footnotes. Marcus, welcome. [Guest] Hey everyone. Aria, I was hoping you’d bring up that dataset. I’ve been staring at the matrix for a week. [Host] Let’s start where the pain lives. You’re a marketer. You’ve been publishing content for months, maybe years. You’re tracking rankings in Google. But then someone asks ChatGPT a question about your industry — and it answers using a competitor’s blog, not yours. No link. No citation. Just a clean paragraph that drives zero traffic to your site. That moment stings. You realize you’re not optimizing for the right engine. [Guest] Right. And the worst part is you can’t just tweak a title tag and fix it. The AI model doesn’t care about your meta description. It cares about being able to extract a clean, authoritative answer from your content. [Host] There’s actually a name for this: Generative Engine Optimization, or G.E.O. Some call it A.I.O. — AI Optimization. And Neil Patel’s team just released something rare: actual data from a hundred marketers and a hundred campaigns mapping which tactics actually pay off versus which ones just burn time. [Guest] That’s the part that got my attention. Most G.E.O. advice is vibes. “Make your content more authoritative.” Great, how? Patel’s data plots eight specific tactics on an effort-versus-ROI matrix. It’s not academic. It’s operational. [Host] So what’s the headline? What’s sitting in the high-ROI, low-effort quadrant? [Guest] Two things stand out: comparison pages and list articles. According to the data, those two formats deliver the highest return for moderate effort. Think about why: when an AI like ChatGPT or Perplexity answers a question like “Which tool is better, X or Y?” it naturally looks for a page that compares them side by side. That’s a comparison page. And list articles — “Top 10 tools for Z” — those are easy for models to cite because they’re structured, factual, and answer evaluative queries. [Host] That aligns with something Patel said in his webinar: “Customer-facing teams are one of the most underused sources of G.E.O. intelligence.” The content brief shouldn’t come from a keyword tool. It should come from the actual language your prospects use when they ask your sales team a question. If a prospect keeps asking “How do I justify the ROI of this to my CFO?” — that exact phrase is your content brief. [Guest] Exactly. And that connects to another debate in the community. Patel’s take is that prompt volume shouldn’t drive strategy. Traditional S.E.O. is volume-driven: keyword A gets 10,000 searches, keyword B gets 100, you target A. But for G.E.O., the model doesn’t care about search volume. It cares about the natural language patterns that appear in customer conversations. Mining subreddits and sales call transcripts is more valuable than any vendor-curated prompt list. [Host] I’ve seen some pushback on that. The old-school S.E.O. crowd wants their volume data. But I think they’re missing the point. The model doesn’t optimize for impressions. It optimizes for being cited. And citations come from content that matches the natural phrasing of the question. [Guest] There’s a healthy skepticism in the community about attribution, too. How exactly is Patel calculating the ROI acceleration from 2024 to 2025? AI engines don’t show you a dashboard of “we cited you 12 times today.” So some of this is inference. But the direction is clear: early movers are seeing gains, and the window is closing. [Host] Let’s talk about what this means for the brands listening. If you’re a mid-market e-commerce brand or a B2B SaaS company, you can’t afford to wait until attribution is perfect. The data Patel published is the best we have right now. And it points to a clear starting point: build comparison pages and list articles that answer the real questions your customers ask. That’s exactly the kind of content we build at A.E.O. Engine — structured, authoritative, designed to be extractable. Our clients see an average 920% lift in AI-driven traffic when they focus on these formats. It’s not magic. It’s just understanding what the models need. [Guest] And it’s not just about comparison pages. The research also highlights FAQ sections with schema markup as a high-impact tactic. If you have a FAQ that directly answers a common question, and you mark it up with structured data, you drastically increase the chance the model pulls that exact answer. That’s a quick win. [Host] I’ll admit: I don’t know if this specific matrix holds in six months. AI models evolve fast. But the principle — optimize for citability, not for rankings — is not going away. The brands that act now will own the citation . [Guest] Agreed. And one more thing from the data: the community reaction to Patel’s masterclass was massive. Hundreds of marketers enrolled. That tells me the demand for actionable G.E.O. frameworks is real. People are tired of theory. They want a playbook. [Host] So here’s your playbook: start with one comparison page. Interview your sales team for the exact language. Build a list article around a top-10 question in your space. Add FAQ schema. Then measure your citations using a tool like A.E.O. Engine’s AI visibility audit. That’s the minimum viable G.E.O. strategy right now. [Guest] And don’t chase prompt volume. Chase understanding what your customer actually asks. [Host] Marcus, thanks for bringing the data to life. For everyone listening, if you want to see the full ROI matrix and start mapping your own tactics, head to aeoengine.ai. We’ve got the framework, the tools, and the team to help you become the answer. Until next time, keep optimizing for the models that are answering your customers’ questions.

structure.rationale: The episode follows a clear host-guest interview format with an identifiable intro, a logical progression through tactics, and a summarizing 'playbook' close, though pacing is occasionally interrupted by promotional asides.

relevance.rationale: This is a digital marketing and SEO/GEO tactics episode with no connection to news, politics, or current events — it is commercial content about AI search optimization strategy.

clipability.rationale: A few quotable lines exist — the 'optimize for citability, not rankings' framing and the comparison-page explanation — but most moments require the surrounding marketing context to land and the episode ends in an overt product pitch.

card.tagline: Why your competitor's blog answers ChatGPT questions and yours doesn't
card.description: Analyst Marcus Reid breaks down Neil Patel's GEO ROI matrix, identifying comparison pages and FAQ schema as the highest-return tactics for AI search citation in 2025.
card.reason: Because this episode offers a tactical framework for AI-driven content visibility

Ron Edwards is the host and founder of the award winning radio commentary, "The Edwards’ Notebook" and "The Ron Edwards American Experience". He is a talented and gifted commentator with over three decades of experience “Blowin’ away the Myths and Revealing the Truth". He brings truth to light and provides solutions for the concerns we all share in a thought provoking manner.

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-ron-edwards-american-experience-v2--2367941/support.
