scored.description:
The True Crime Tapes pulls you into the shadowy depths of the criminal underworld, where the line between justice and chaos is razor-thin. Each episode dissects the minds of history’s most infamous serial killers, unravels the inner workings of organized crime syndicates, and investigates baffling missing person cases that still haunt the public’s imagination. From the bloody reign of ruthless mob bosses to the chilling patterns of elusive predators, True Crime Time delivers gripping, deeply researched storytelling that leaves no stone unturned.

With a relentless pursuit of truth, True Crime Time goes beyond the headlines, diving into the psychology, motives, and investigations behind the world’s most shocking crimes. You’ll hear firsthand accounts, expert analysis, and rare archival material that shed new light on cases both well-known and obscure. Whether it’s the brutality of cartel wars, the sinister precision of serial murderers, or the eerie last-known moments of vanished souls, this podcast brings you face-to-face with the darker side of human.

Every week, True Crime Time takes you on a journey through the twisted corridors of crime, guided by immersive storytelling and chilling attention to detail. Expect heart-pounding narratives, intricate conspiracy threads, and unsettling truths that will leave you questioning everything you thought you knew. If you crave the rush of uncovering the darkest mysteries, brace yourself—because in this world, the truth is often stranger, and far more terrifying, than fiction.

structure.rationale: The episode follows the natural Q&A structure of a congressional transcript, which provides a clear throughline, but the format is inherently repetitive and lacks editorial segmentation or host-guided navigation between topics.

relevance.rationale: This episode is directly and substantively about a sitting Cabinet secretary's congressional testimony regarding the Epstein files investigation — a specific, timely political and legal proceeding at the core of current news.

clipability.rationale: Several exchanges — Lutnick's 'Always about Trump' outburst, his speculation about Epstein's blackmail money, and the nanny resume revelation — are punchy and self-contained enough to work as standalone preview clips.

scored.description:
WES BRYANT
Charlotte native Wes Bryant comes to WFNZ as a host, producer, editor, and voice-over artist for the ACC Digital Network in Charlotte, North Carolina.  He was previously the host for ACC Digital Network’s Kickoff Live, a college football pregame show and Wes Got Range, a lifestyle show on which Wes explored and visited locally owned restaurants in Charlotte and interviewed local area athletes. 
Wes was hired for the 2021-2022 season as a pre and post-game co-host for Charlotte Hornets broadcast on Bally Sports South. 
In addition to his work at the ACC Digital Network, Wes is a regular contributor on Charlotte Sports Live on Fox 46 Charlotte, where he discusses and breaks down all the action in the NBA, NFL, NASCAR, and across NCAA basketball and football. 
He began his career in 2013 as a beat writer for the Charlotte 49ers of the C-USA Conference for Rivals.com.  Wes was previously a reporter and producer for The Melanie Clark Show on Radio One.  In 2007, Wes was an intern at WBTV, Channel 3, in Charlotte.
Wes is a former offensive lineman, and three-year letterman for the Wake Forest Demon Deacons, graduated from the university in 2006.  He was a High School All-American football player at Vance High School in Charlotte.
WALKER MEHL
Walker grew up in Claremont, NC and played basketball at Bunker Hill High School. He then took his talents to UNC-Charlotte where he graduated in 2015.  Walker then started his broadcasting career in the Charlotte market the following year. Along with his start in radio, Walker began hosting the Locked On Hornets podcast in 2018 where his passion for the Hornets and the other local sports teams is apparent.  Prior to joining WFNZ as a full-time host, Walker has worked on the station in a number of different shows.
Growing up in and around the Queen City, Walker is a life-long fan and observer of the Carolina Panthers, the Charlotte Hornets and the University of North Carolina.   Walker loves the outdoors, going out and spending time in Uptown and loves music as much as he loves sports.

structure.rationale: The conversation follows a loose but identifiable throughline moving from App State's quarterback situation to defensive concerns to coaching job attractiveness, with natural transitions between topics.

relevance.rationale: This episode is entirely college football sports analysis — App State, UNC, and Sun Belt conference previews — with no substantive political or news content.

clipability.rationale: Most moments require prior context about App State's recent seasons or the dal Loggins hire to land, though the Bill Belichick-at-UNC aside and the 'Carolina football exists to let you down' quote offer brief standalone potential.

card.tagline: Is App State's golden era over — or can dal Loggins turn it around?
card.description: Wes and Walker debate whether App State's reputation is in freefall, what a second straight five-win season means for the coaching staff, and how tradition holds up in the NIL era.
card.reason: Because this episode breaks down App State's make-or-break 2026 season outlook

scored.description:
Neural Newscast delivers clear, concise daily news - powered by AI and reviewed by humans. In a world where news never stops, we help you stay informed without the overwhelm.

Our AI correspondents cover the day’s most important headlines across politics, technology, business, culture, science, and cybersecurity - designed for listening on the go. Whether you’re commuting, working out, or catching up between meetings, Neural Newscast keeps you up to date in minutes.

The network also features specialty shows including Prime Cyber Insights, Stereo Current, Nerfed.AI, and Buzz, exploring cybersecurity, music and culture, gaming and AI, and internet trends.

Every episode is produced and reviewed by founder Chad Thompson, combining advanced AI systems with human editorial oversight to ensure accuracy, clarity, and responsible reporting.

Learn more at neuralnewscast.com.

scored.description:
The Beyond Normal Radio Show is a paranormal podcast that dives headfirst into the strange, the mysterious, and the unexplained. Hosted by Emo-Von and LaShae, the show explores the shadowy edges of reality where logic fades and the unknown takes over. From chilling ghost encounters and haunted locations to UFO sightings, cryptid legends, and bizarre phenomena that defy explanation, nothing is off-limits.
​
Emo-Von brings a grounded yet fearless approach to the paranormal, blending research, personal experiences, and a passion for uncovering hidden truths. LaShae balances the conversation with sharp insight, curiosity, and an open mind, asking the questions listeners are thinking but may be afraid to ask. Together, they create a dynamic, engaging atmosphere that feels like you’re sitting in on a late-night conversation where the veil between worlds is thin.

scored.feedTitle: Pop Culture Weekly - Celebrity Interviews & Entertainment News
scored.description:
Welcome to Pop Culture Weekly with Kyle McMahon - your go-to podcast for celebrity interviews with the biggest names in movies, TV, music and more… plus everything happening across pop culture, streaming and technology (and yes, occasionally the craziness in between).

Each week, I sit down with your favorite actors, music artists, creators and icons for real, unfiltered conversations - then break it all down with honest reviews, hot takes and smart (sometimes questionable) insights on what’s actually worth your time.

From blockbuster movies and binge-worthy series to chart-topping music, industry shakeups and the trends shaping entertainment and tech, this is your weekly guide to staying plugged in without getting overwhelmed.

No fluff. No fake hype. No gossip. Just great pop culture...with a little chaos.

If you’ve got opinions, questionable watchlists, and a deep love for entertainment… welcome home, Pop Culture Degenerates.

New episodes weekly. Let’s go.


For deep dives, transcripts, ultimate guides and more, visit: Podcast.PopCultureWeekly.com

scored.description:
Moscow is a city located in northern Idaho, United States, with a population of approximately 25,000 people. It is the largest city and the county seat of Latah County. The city is situated in the Palouse region, known for its fertile soil and rolling hills, and is surrounded by wheat fields, forests, and mountains.Moscow is home to the University of Idaho, which is the state's flagship institution and a major research university. The university is a significant contributor to the local economy, and many businesses in the city are directly or indirectly tied to the university.

The city also has a thriving arts and culture scene, with several galleries, museums, and performance venues.In terms of recreation, Moscow has several parks and outdoor recreation areas, including the Latah Trail, the Moscow Mountain Trail System, and the Palouse Divide Nordic Ski Area. The city also hosts several annual events, including the Moscow Farmers Market, the Lionel Hampton Jazz Festival, and the Renaissance Fair.

However, things would change forever after Xana Kernodle, Ethan Chapin, Madison Mogen and Kaylee Goncalves were murdered in the early morning hours of November 13th, 2022.

What followed in the wake of the murders captivated not only the nation but the whole world as the authorities scrambled to find the person responsible for the heinous crime.

This podcast will document the Murders In Moscow from right after the murders were committed all the way through the real time evolution of the trial of the person that the authorities say is responsible, Bryan Kohberger.

We will also cover other stories that are based in the world of true crime that are currently in the courts or that are headed that way.

structure.rationale: The episode reads documents sequentially with commentary interspersed, but the structure is loose and reactive rather than deliberate, with abrupt transitions and no clear segmentation beyond topic shifts.

relevance.rationale: The episode is directly and substantively about the newly released Epstein DOJ files, covering specific political figures — Elon Musk, Trump, Larry Summers, Bill Clinton — with document-level specificity tied to a current news event.

clipability.rationale: Several self-contained moments work as standalone clips — particularly the Musk email sequence with the host's running commentary — though the profanity-heavy delivery limits platform versatility.

scored.description:
Dive into the wild world of Laura Cain After Dark...your new favorite escape....hosted by 
Laura Cain and Erik Rimmer...Best friends and complete opposites which creates an engaging, sometimes shocking, hilarious atmosphere. 
Expect awkward confessions, crazy true stories, unedited, unscripted, unapologetic conversations, and the latest showbiz gossip. 

We love interviewing people from all walks of life and introducing you to new products, games, and TV shows.   This is a perfect escape from your daily stress...as raw as it gets, sometimes a bit raunchy, always funny and bursting with heart.  Make this podcast your go-to happy place.  We are family.
Love your podcast!

scored.description:
Rattling the Bars puts the voices of the people most harmed by our system of mass incarceration at the center of our reporting on the fight to end it. The show was founded by the late Black Panther and political prisoner Marshall “Eddie” Conway, and is now hosted by Charles Hopkins, better known as Mansa Musa, who himself spent 48 years behind bars.

Rattling the Bars offers an honest look at the lives of prisoners, returning citizens, their families, and their communities. With Rattling the Bars, by presenting hard data and real-life stories, we examine and seek to shift public opinion around the misconception that incarceration, punishment, and increased policing make cities safer—the truth of which has been disproven by countless studies. The series examines the history and root causes of the current so-called justice system. It showcases individuals and communities nationwide who are grappling with real solutions to problems created by the prison-industrial complex.

Help us continue producing Rattling the Bars by following us and becoming a monthly sustainer.

Sign up for our newsletter

structure.rationale: The conversation follows a loose but identifiable throughline — BPP legacy, lessons for youth, coalition-building — though transitions are conversational and meandering rather than deliberately signposted.

relevance.rationale: The episode engages substantively with political organizing, state repression, and socialist community programs through the lens of Black Panther Party history, with explicit lessons drawn for current movement-building — timely given ongoing debates about radical left organizing strategies.

clipability.rationale: Several self-contained moments work as standalone excerpts, particularly the 'develop your railroad first' organizing advice and the critique of ideological purity culture, though most require at least minimal context to land fully.

card.tagline: Black Panther veterans explain what today's organizers are still getting wrong
card.description: Former Panthers and scholars break down the free breakfast program, FBI sabotage, and why today's movements must build underground infrastructure before anything else.
card.reason: Because this episode draws direct organizing lessons from radical left history

scored.feedTitle: AI Chat: AI News & Artificial Intelligence
scored.description: AI Chat is the podcast where we dive into the world of AI, cutting-edge AI news and its impact on our daily lives. With in-depth discussions and interviews with leading experts in the field, we'll explore the latest advancements in language models, machine learning, and more. From its practical applications to its ethical considerations, AI Chat will keep you informed and entertained on the exciting developments in the world of AI. Tune in to stay ahead of the curve on the latest technological revolution.

structure.rationale: The episode moves through distinct story segments (MuseSpark launch, Sunrun distributed compute, SK Hynix IPO, OpenAI leadership change) with a clear close, though transitions between topics are abrupt rather than signposted.

relevance.rationale: This episode covers AI product launches, chip IPOs, and tech industry news — not News/Politics subject matter at its core, and the incidental references to Trump's data center comments or Howard Lutnick's negotiations don't make it primarily a political episode.

clipability.rationale: The Zuckerberg-breaking-his-X-silence moment and the '70% of Americans oppose new data center construction' stat both work as near-standalone hooks, though most segments require at least minimal setup to land.

card.tagline: Zuckerberg tweets again, SK Hynix breaks IPO records, and AI moves into your home
card.description: This episode breaks down Meta's MuseSpark coding model launch, Sunrun's plan to turn residential solar homes into mini data centers, and SK Hynix's record-shattering $26.5B US IPO.
card.reason: Because this episode tracks the week's biggest AI product and market moves

scored.description:
Want to make sense of the seismic shifts in American news, politics and entertainment? Larry O’Connor has a way of breaking down and explaining the most complicated topics as he delivers succinct, bite-sized summaries of the stories everybody’s talking about.
Always good-natured and never preachy or shouty, LARRY will get you up-to-speed on today’s hottest topics with common-sense, conservative and, yes, funny analysis.
Like what you hear? Join Townhall VIP Gold with promo code "LARRY" to enjoy all the exclusive benefits at 50% off!

structure.rationale: The episode has a clear throughline — walking through trial evidence and rebutting conspiracy claims — but shifts between reading text messages, editorializing, and addressing Candace Owens without tight signposting.

relevance.rationale: The episode is squarely focused on a politically motivated assassination trial — a specific, ongoing legal proceeding with direct political dimensions including motive, media coverage, and conservative media conspiracy theories.

clipability.rationale: The 'Data Republican open letter' segment is a dense, self-contained recitation of specific evidence points that would work as a standalone clip, and O'Connor's media-vacuum argument is quotable on its own.

card.tagline: The Charlie Kirk murder trial evidence is now too overwhelming to deny
card.description: Larry O'Connor walks through DNA evidence, text message confessions, and trial testimony to dismantle conspiracy theories surrounding the Charlie Kirk assassination case.
card.reason: Because this episode covers an ongoing political murder trial dominating conservative media

structure.rationale: The excerpt reads as loose, meandering conversation moving between soccer, Bitcoin node debates, listener boosts, and gaming references with no clear throughline or deliberate segmentation.

relevance.rationale: The episode is primarily Bitcoin/crypto community commentary, gaming references, and casual banter, with only a brief passing mention of Mitch McConnell as a tangential aside — not substantive news or political coverage.

clipability.rationale: Nearly every moment requires familiarity with the show's in-group references — 'kayfabe,' 'plebs,' 'Orange Mart,' 'white goy summer' — making standalone clips largely inaccessible to outside listeners.

structure.rationale: The episode has a clear narrative arc with a deliberate time-machine flashback device moving from 2018 to 2022 to the present, though pacing is occasionally interrupted by tangential asides like the heavy metal digression.

relevance.rationale: The episode directly addresses a live geopolitical conflict — Trump's 2026 tariffs on Brazil justified as a free-speech intervention — connecting current US foreign policy to Brazil's disinformation laws and electoral history.

clipability.rationale: The street artist quote about fake news deciding the election and the explanation of Brazil's democratic fragility relative to its 1985 return from dictatorship are genuinely self-contained, quotable moments that work without heavy setup.

card.tagline: Trump is using 'free speech' to punish Brazil for fighting disinformation
card.description: A deep dive into how Brazil built legal defenses against electoral fake news — and why the Trump administration is now targeting those laws with tariffs.
card.reason: Because this episode unpacks a breaking US foreign policy confrontation over democracy

scored.description: J.J. Carrell is a speaker and the author of INVADED: The International Destruction of the American Immigration System. Carrell uses his 24-year career in the United States Border Patrol as a Journeyman Agent to a Deputy Patrol Agent in Charge to expose the who, why and how this treason is being perpetrated against our great nation. Carrell has been a guest on several national syndicated radio shows and television networks as he has become the expert on border issues and the criminal acts by Biden, his administration, and the DHS machine. Carrell is unafraid to speak the truth and he is unapologetically patriotic.

latestEpisode.title: Ukraine’s Endless Bloodbath: Corruption Fuels This Senseless War with J.J. Carrell

structure.rationale: The episode moves through clips and commentary but lacks clear segmentation — transitions between Zelensky footage, McConnell quotes, and Trump audio feel reactive rather than deliberately structured.

relevance.rationale: The episode directly engages a live geopolitical conflict, U.S. foreign aid policy, and Trump's stated peace commitments — substantive political content with timely hooks, though framed as opinion commentary rather than hard news reporting.

clipability.rationale: Several punchy standalone moments exist — the '24-hour war, 18 months later' line and the 'they just say it out loud' defense contractor riff would work as self-contained previews without much setup.

card.tagline: Why are we still funding a war Trump promised to end in 24 hours?
card.description: Carrell tears into U.S. Ukraine spending, Zelensky's credibility, and the defense contractor admission that he says proves the war is a corruption racket.
card.reason: Because this episode challenges the bipartisan consensus on Ukraine funding directly

scored.description:
"Amy & T.J." is hosted by renowned television news anchors Amy Robach and T. J. Holmes.
Hosts and executive producers Robach and Holmes are a formidable broadcasting team with decades of experience delivering headline news and captivating viewers nationwide. Now, the duo will get behind the microphone to explore meaningful conversations about current events, pop culture and everything in between. Nothing is off limits. “Amy & T.J.” is guaranteed to be informative, entertaining and above all, authentic. It marks the first time Robach and Holmes speak publicly since their own names became a part of the headlines. 
Follow @ajrobach, and @officialtjholmes on Instagram for updates.

scored.feedTitle: Check the FAQ w/ Qui Talks
scored.description:
Check the FAQ w/ Qui Talks is where fashion, politics, culture, and community collide. Hosted by Quisha “Qui Talks” Joacin, the show explores how clothing, identity, and power shape our world — from pop culture and red carpets to local issues and global news. Every episode blends real conversation, style analysis, and current events, giving listeners a unique mix of entertainment and education. Whether it’s decoding the politics of fashion, highlighting local changemakers, or breaking down headlines in real language, Check the FAQ w/ Qui Talks delivers stories with heart, humor, and honesty.

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/check-the-faq-w-qui-talks--2910746/support.

clipability.rationale: A few punchy, self-contained takes — particularly the 'his ego won't allow him to be anything but the first option' riff and the Cleveland prediction — could stand alone, but most moments rely on the surrounding rant for full impact.

card.reason: Because you want a no-holds-barred take on where LeBron lands next

scored.feedTitle: Your News Talk America, with Jake Smith
scored.description:
Your News Talk America with Jake Smith is a daily national politics and news commentary program featuring in-depth analysis of the stories shaping America. Hosted by broadcaster Jake Smith, the show includes political interviews, policy discussion, and media analysis from voices influencing the national conversation.


The program airs weekdays from 9 a.m. to noon Eastern on WGMD 92.7 FM and WUSX 98.5 FM. This podcast focuses on national issues and is separate from the local community coverage featured on WGMD.com.


Listeners can interact live with Jake and other audience members in the Digital Interactive Audience Lounge (DIAL) Monday through Thursday by listening, chatting, and submitting questions. On Fridays, the program is also available as a live video broadcast.


Visit RealNewsTalk.com for live participation, Friday video, show notes, and additional resources.

latestEpisode.title: Iran Escalation, Trump Air Force One Security, Reading Crisis in America | Your News Talk America | July 9, 2026

we going to power this? 00:53:18.079 --> 00:53:26.960 Because as you just saw in the Northeast PJM market, New York, New Jersey, the heat wave, which to me as a Floridian was really not that hot. 00:53:27.119 --> 00:53:29.440 Um, you your grid got stressed out. 00:53:29.519 --> 00:53:36.320 And they started the utility company, uh, started shutting down portions of the grid to conserve so they wouldn't have a total blackout. 00:53:36.559 --> 00:53:45.199 And this kind of goes to my whole point is that if you are relying on the government or the utility companies to power your home, you're doing this whole thing wrong. 00:53:45.360 --> 00:53:48.719 And so I look, I I am like a sovereign team maximalist. 00:53:48.880 --> 00:53:55.599 I love having guns, I love having a well, I love having septic tanks, but none of that matters if I don't have power. 00:53:55.760 --> 00:53:56.800 So you know this. 00:53:57.039 --> 00:54:08.719 I'm a huge advocate of residential, not solar farms, residential solar and batteries, because I'm not going to put my full uh trust in that I'm going to have power provided by the utility. 00:54:08.800 --> 00:54:10.400 Uh I just can't trust it. 00:54:10.480 --> 00:54:16.880 And so, what do I need to do to protect my family, especially in a time where we have war overseas and data centers on the way? 00:54:17.039 --> 00:54:18.960 What can I do to cover myself? 00:54:19.440 --> 00:54:26.880 Now, the situation with respect to the supply of oil right now, we don't get our uh our oil through the straight of war moves. 00:54:27.039 --> 00:54:28.880 We're the one of the few countries that doesn't. 00:54:28.960 --> 00:54:33.599 However, traffic through the strait has come to a complete standstill as of today. 00:54:33.760 --> 00:54:38.400 So, what are the immediate effects on price and supply when that happens? 00:54:38.960 --> 00:54:39.920 Yeah, no, you're right. 00:54:40.000 --> 00:54:44.800 You're absolutely right, is that we do not rely on the strait of war moves and thank God for that. 00:54:45.039 --> 00:54:51.280 However, it goes back to that conversation of it's a it is still oil, gas still remains to be a global commodity. 00:54:51.360 --> 00:55:02.880 And when most of Europe can't can't help themselves in their energy crisis ever, and Southeast Asia has no means of production either, that means that those nations are now seeking other suppliers. 00:55:03.039 --> 00:55:04.239 Well, who's the other supplier? 00:55:04.480 --> 00:55:04.960 It's us. 00:55:05.039 --> 00:55:12.159 And and and God God bless President Trump for allowing us to be the global supplier of oil and natural gas. 00:55:12.320 --> 00:55:22.559 That is an amazing asset to our nation's economy, but that also still means that we are going to be susceptible here at home to a global commodity market. 00:55:22.639 --> 00:55:34.639 And this goes back to my whole thesis is that we need to also look at how we can build non-commoditized energy generation here at home so that we can power our own homes, our own businesses. 00:55:34.800 --> 00:55:35.599 And that could be coal. 00:55:35.679 --> 00:55:37.440 That was coal can maybe the X word. 00:55:37.599 --> 00:55:39.840 Uh that could be nuclear, that could be solar, right? 00:55:40.320 --> 00:55:45.679 Building or high hydro uh dams, you know, all these things that you can't just stick on a boat. 00:55:45.840 --> 00:55:54.800 What that also means, too, then, is that we can take the commoditized energy sources and sell it for top dollar to the nations who are beholden to the Strait of Hormoes. 00:55:54.880 --> 00:55:55.840 This is a win-win. 00:55:56.000 --> 00:56:02.320 Build non-commoditized energy sources at home so we can export the commodity and make a ton of money. 00:56:03.039 --> 00:56:08.400 For those who may not understand, because we um we do not buy Brent crude. 00:56:08.559 --> 00:56:10.400 That that is for the rest of the world. 00:56:10.480 --> 00:56:14.400 Our our crude oil is West Texas Intermediate, WTI. 00:56:15.039 --> 00:56:21.199 So, how does WTI, our crude, have the same market volatility as Brent crude? 00:56:22.880 --> 00:56:25.440 Look, I'm not an oil expert by any means. 00:56:25.519 --> 00:56:33.440 I'm more in the utility and electricity space, but at the end of the day, it still comes back to the same thing is that our oil is is it's highly desirable. 00:56:33.519 --> 00:56:36.079 Uh I think people want, especially when you think about this. 00:56:36.159 --> 00:56:44.079 When when when other nations don't have oil, if they frames, they don't care whether it's Brent or WTI or what's just straws around, they just want oil. 00:56:44.400 --> 00:56:47.519 So all of the commodity prices are going to move up together. 00:56:47.760 --> 00:56:49.840 And and and you're right, there's different types of oil. 00:56:50.000 --> 00:56:52.559 There's there's sour, there's sweet, there's heavy, there's light. 00:56:52.800 --> 00:56:56.480 Uh you could get an oil guy here and talk for three hours about the different types of oils. 00:56:56.960 --> 00:57:03.440 At the end of the day, what I think Americans need to understand is that this situation is not going to get better anytime soon. 00:57:03.599 --> 00:57:09.440 And we are coming, we're about mid-summer right now, and it's only going to get worse when it actually comes to heating our homes. 00:57:09.519 --> 00:57:11.760 And so we need to think about what's the next step. 00:57:11.920 --> 00:57:25.119 And even if the Strait of Hormuz issue was solved tomorrow, which I don't believe it will be, we still have to address that AI data centers are now the biggest new load in American history, and they're landing on a grid that was built a hundred years ago. 00:57:25.440 --> 00:57:33.360 PJM is is is is their power prices tripled during the last heat waves, and we had to tell the data centers to shut down, right? 00:57:33.440 --> 00:57:42.159 Like this is another problem that even even if even if the Middle East issues were solved and they haven't been in in history, we still have another big issue we have to we have to address. 00:57:42.480 --> 00:57:56.079 So, from your vantage point, from an energy perspective, Sam Romain, Chairman, Americans for energy dominance, how important is it for President Trump to subdue this enemy quickly for the energy markets? 00:57:56.239 --> 00:57:57.280 What would you say? 00:57:59.119 --> 00:58:04.800 Look, uh, geopolitics is an interesting space, and I'm gonna trust CENTCOM to make the right decisions on that. 00:58:04.880 --> 00:58:09.679 But I I give a lot of credit where our credit's due to President Trump between Venezuela, straight-of-arm moves. 00:58:09.760 --> 00:58:14.000 We have we have reshuffled the chessboard to make America the energy-dominant nation in the world. 00:58:14.079 --> 00:58:16.159 We're now exporting energy at all-time high. 00:58:16.320 --> 00:58:17.280 These are good things. 00:58:17.519 --> 00:58:21.119 We just need to look at ourselves at home and how we can detach from that global market. 00:58:21.440 --> 00:58:29.440 You know, Sam, uh, next time we get together, let's talk more about how we can make uh our homes grid resistant. 00:58:29.599 --> 00:58:35.920 Because that's really what you're talking about, building isolation from the grid for our homes so that we're self-sufficient when we need to be. 00:58:36.079 --> 00:58:41.280 Uh, that's something uh maybe we should talk a little bit more about the next time you're on the broadcast. 00:58:41.760 --> 00:58:42.960 I would absolutely love that. 00:58:43.119 --> 00:58:43.760 Thanks for having me on. 00:58:44.000 --> 00:58:44.719 I really appreciate it. 00:58:44.880 --> 00:58:45.599 All right, thank you, Sam. 00:58:45.679 --> 00:58:46.880 We appreciate you coming on this morning. 00:58:47.039 --> 00:58:50.639 Sam Remain, Chairman Americans for energy dominance. 00:58:50.880 --> 00:58:53.840 Your news talk America on this Thursday. 00:58:54.079 --> 00:58:57.519 Back from DS2 or back to DS2 in just a moment. 00:58:57.679 --> 00:59:12.880 Stay with us.com. 00:59:23.199 --> 00:59:26.719 So much to talk about, and such little time in which to do it. 00:59:26.800 --> 00:59:30.000 Your Anchorman Jake Smith with you until noon Eastern time. 00:59:30.400 --> 00:59:39.920 Uh I just wanted to uh bring to you a um a story that just popped up uh on uh FoxNews.com just uh within the past hour. 00:59:40.239 --> 00:59:59.360 Uh we had talked about this morning that the uh strikes that the United States conducted last night against targets in Iran were um, well, you could make them offensive, defensive,

scored.description:
A community interview series focused on Calhoun County, Michigan, featuring voices from Battle Creek, Marshall, Albion and all around the county. Join host Richard Piet to discuss local events, non-profits, local schools, government and community leaders.


Underwritten by Lakeview Ford-Lincoln, Community Matters also airs as a radio program Saturday mornings on 95.3 FM in Battle Creek.

latestEpisode.title: (Community Matters 196) B Squared Foundation Brings Pro Level Coaching to Local Kids

transcript.scoringWindow: So my kids this year, which is the community kids, can be kids from any school district, any area, any age. 00:01:12.400 --> 00:01:14.719 We just we're fighting to get our trophy back. 00:01:14.959 --> 00:01:17.200 So they're motivated, then I presume. 00:01:17.439 --> 00:01:19.040 We're motivated, yeah. 00:01:19.359 --> 00:01:19.920 All right. 00:01:19.920 --> 00:01:21.599 So this is free. 00:01:21.599 --> 00:01:26.560 This is a uh meant to be a community engagement kind of opportunity. 00:01:26.560 --> 00:01:32.319 And so uh what if a parent is listening and says, Boy, you know, my kid might like to be in that. 00:01:32.319 --> 00:01:33.680 How do you figure that out? 00:01:33.920 --> 00:01:39.760 They can go on our Facebook page, which is B Squared, like the superscript two, like in a math problem. 00:01:39.760 --> 00:01:41.120 So B squared. 00:01:41.120 --> 00:01:45.680 It will say B2, B squared foundation on Facebook. 00:01:45.680 --> 00:01:52.239 We also have a website where they can go sign on and say, hey, we want to join that football game. 00:01:52.239 --> 00:02:01.920 www.b as in boy-squared sq u ar ed foundation.org. 00:02:01.920 --> 00:02:08.560 Again, b-squared foundation.org, where they can say, hey, I want to have my kid participate in it. 00:02:08.560 --> 00:02:12.159 It's totally free, and that's gonna be 3 p.m. 00:02:12.159 --> 00:02:13.759 on July 19th. 00:02:13.759 --> 00:02:15.520 Now I must put a disclaimer in there. 00:02:15.520 --> 00:02:16.879 We do have a rain date. 00:02:16.879 --> 00:02:20.960 So if it's raining, it will be moved to July 26th. 00:02:21.199 --> 00:02:24.319 It's not like the NFL where they play in any weather. 00:02:24.639 --> 00:02:25.120 Right. 00:02:25.120 --> 00:02:25.599 Right. 00:02:25.599 --> 00:02:28.560 And if it's severe thunderstorms, we're not gonna do it. 00:02:28.560 --> 00:02:30.400 But yes, we have a rain date. 00:02:30.639 --> 00:02:31.039 All right. 00:02:31.039 --> 00:02:44.879 So for now, we're expecting sun on July 19th at 3 at Battle Creek Central for the free community football day, which probably should mention it's flag football, so uh you're not gonna get tackled. 00:02:45.120 --> 00:02:45.919 That is correct. 00:02:45.919 --> 00:02:50.560 The BCT was like, we gotta play flag, we cannot do tackle. 00:02:50.800 --> 00:02:51.680 Yeah, all right. 00:02:51.680 --> 00:02:53.599 Well, that makes sense, of course. 00:02:53.599 --> 00:02:56.240 All right, so a fun opportunity. 00:02:56.240 --> 00:03:03.680 And uh how did you come up with this and uh talk for a second about B Squared Foundation and what that's about? 00:03:04.000 --> 00:03:04.719 Absolutely. 00:03:04.719 --> 00:03:08.319 Uh for guests to have heard the radio show in the past. 00:03:08.319 --> 00:03:16.639 Last year we had a mission was to send kids to the Detroit Lions camp that came to Battle Creek Central, and that's how it all started. 00:03:16.639 --> 00:03:19.680 My son had a goal with just sending one kid to the camp. 00:03:19.680 --> 00:03:24.560 The community got involved, and we ended up sending 32 kids to the camp at $249. 00:03:24.560 --> 00:03:30.960 During that time, we did team building exercises with the kids that we raised money with. 00:03:30.960 --> 00:03:40.479 We went to like the water park and soaring eagle, and then we came up with doing a game against the Battle Creek Police Department, which is our trophy initiative. 00:03:40.479 --> 00:03:41.039 Okay. 00:03:41.039 --> 00:03:45.120 After the camp and everything, I graduated from college. 00:03:45.120 --> 00:03:51.360 And so we I instead of having an open house, I did another challenge, which was called Ola to Diploma. 00:03:51.360 --> 00:03:59.199 And people were like giving me pop bottles and cans to help cover that cost for kids so that they can get free Spanish lessons. 00:03:59.199 --> 00:04:09.280 However, after the funding ran out, no donations, I was like, I think it's time to continue our sports initiative with some literacy as well. 00:04:09.280 --> 00:04:12.639 And so that's how B Squared was born. 00:04:12.639 --> 00:04:21.120 So we continue to still do the mother-son birthday challenge that will always be every March, uh, depending on what type of events that are going on in our community. 00:04:21.120 --> 00:04:23.519 That's what we will support with the mother-son challenge. 00:04:23.519 --> 00:04:29.920 And it's simply like you either pledge my son for a certain amount or me for a certain amount, and whoever raised the most money wins. 00:04:29.920 --> 00:04:33.360 So we're a very competitive family in our household, as you can tell. 00:04:33.360 --> 00:04:35.040 But Bentley noticed, yes. 00:04:35.040 --> 00:04:38.000 Bentley has won two years in a row with support. 00:04:38.639 --> 00:04:39.600 Has he now? 00:04:39.600 --> 00:04:42.079 Oh boy, he's not getting too cocky, is he? 00:04:42.480 --> 00:04:43.120 Not yet. 00:04:43.120 --> 00:04:44.959 I think next year he might get me. 00:04:45.199 --> 00:04:46.079 How old is he now? 00:04:46.319 --> 00:04:47.600 He is actually 10. 00:04:47.920 --> 00:04:48.399 10. 00:04:48.399 --> 00:04:51.040 Okay, we're into the double digits now. 00:04:51.279 --> 00:04:51.839 Yes. 00:04:52.160 --> 00:04:55.600 Okay, well, good for him, and uh congratulations to him. 00:04:55.600 --> 00:04:57.279 And Ola to Diploma. 00:04:57.279 --> 00:04:57.839 I like that. 00:04:57.839 --> 00:05:02.160 Yeah, that's a nice uh turn of phrase, I guess you'd call it. 00:05:02.160 --> 00:05:05.439 Okay, so the 19th, the community football game. 00:05:05.439 --> 00:05:10.959 You're also planning something in August, the Pistons Academy Clinic, right? 00:05:11.199 --> 00:05:13.120 Yes, we are super excited about that. 00:05:13.120 --> 00:05:17.360 One thing being in the nonprofit sector, we get to network with other nonprofits. 00:05:17.360 --> 00:05:21.680 And so I've heard about a nonprofit in my hometown, which is in Benton Harbor, Michigan. 00:05:21.680 --> 00:05:25.120 And we recently just came back from a Gusmacker tournament yesterday. 00:05:25.120 --> 00:05:33.519 Well, on June 28th, had the Gus Macker tournament because we were helping that foundation as well raise funds for that area. 00:05:33.519 --> 00:05:38.240 And then he got me introduced, Rodney Alexander, to the Pistons Academy. 00:05:38.240 --> 00:05:40.079 And so we're super excited. 00:05:40.079 --> 00:05:44.480 I talked to them and they was like, Hey, we want to come to Battle Creek. 00:05:44.480 --> 00:05:46.879 And I was like, Look, I seen some of your prices. 00:05:46.879 --> 00:05:49.040 We can't afford it this year this quick. 00:05:49.040 --> 00:05:52.720 And he was like, Nope, we really want to come to Battle Creek. 00:05:52.720 --> 00:05:56.240 And I said, Well, if we do it this year, let's make it affordable for family. 00:05:56.240 --> 00:06:01.759 So we are raising money to send kids for that academy, it's $87.55. 00:06:01.759 --> 00:06:07.199 The grades are grades three through nine, boys and girls. 00:06:07.199 --> 00:06:12.800 That is going to be August 22nd at Fool Blast Arena from 9 a.m. 00:06:12.800 --> 00:06:13.920 to 1 p.m. 00:06:13.920 --> 00:06:17.759 So again, the cost of the campus is $87.55. 00:06:17.759 --> 00:06:23.199 We are looking for sponsors to help cover that cost, but we also sponsor kids. 00:06:23.199 --> 00:06:29.759 So far, we've got 13 kids sponsored that wanted to go because we don't want to have anything uh stop these kids from going. 00:06:29.759 --> 00:06:32.160 And we're not just limited to kids in Battle Creek. 00:06:32.160 --> 00:06:33.519 I got 10 kids already. 00:06:33.519 --> 00:06:36.399 They're gonna just give me the names that are coming from Albion. 00:06:36.399 --> 00:06:39.600 I got another five out of Campbell Zoo coming out of foster care. 00:06:39.600 --> 00:06:41.920 So we're not just limited to Battle Creek. 00:06:41.920 --> 00:06:48.319 We want kids to attend at Albion Marshall, all the surrounding areas, Bellevue, Union City, you name it. 00:06:48.560 --> 00:06:53.040 And that 8755 is the cost per kid to attend. 00:06:53.040 --> 00:06:53.839 That is true. 00:06:53.839 --> 00:06:54.959 Yeah. 00:06:54.959 --> 00:06:55.680 Okay. 00:06:55.680 --> 00:06:58.879 And uh talk about what they're gonna experience. 00:06:58.879 --> 00:07:04.720 They're gonna obviously a clinic is it infers it, but let's hear what it it really means for them. 00:07:05.040 --> 00:07:13.360 What it means for them is that they're gonna get some shooting experience, drills, passing on a higher professional level coaching staff. 00:07:13.360 --> 00:07:13.839 Yeah. 00:07:13.839 --> 00:07:18.879 Uh, we're also going to experience the Detroit Extreme team. 00:07:18.879 --> 00:07:28.160 And I'm very excited, like them, if you've ever been to a piston game or any audience members, to me, they're just as good as the Harlem Globetrotters. 00:07:28.160 --> 00:07:29.759 I enjoyed their show. 00:07:29.759 --> 00:07:31.680 I was like a little kid when I seen it. 00:07:31.680 --> 00:07:40.240 And so to have them come to be a part of that and give those kids that experience that may not be having the opportunity to go to a business game. 00:07:40.240 --> 00:07:44.480 So we're just trying to bring that Detroit life to Battle Creek, our town. 00:07:44.720 --> 00:07:58.879 And these uh third to ninth graders are gonna get some professional level tips and coaching in this, and that's going to, I presume you know better than I inspire whatever uh basketball dreams they have. 00:07:59.199 --> 00:07:59.839 Exactly. 00:07:59.839 --> 00:08:12.000 Take it to the next level, and to say that I had this opportunity to work with the Pistons Academy, that is not only name-dropping, but it's a confidence builder. 00:08:12.240 --> 00:08:12.800 Absolutely. 00:08:12.800 --> 00:08:25.360 So if you've uh, for example, you hit a Lions clinic before and uh that was similarly situated, you got to see right uh how the kids respond to that. 00:08:25.360 --> 00:08:26.319 Talk about that. 00:08:26.319 --> 00:08:30.160 What did you see on that day and you expect to see on this one? 00:08:30.480 --> 00:08:42.639 I'm expecting to see a lot of smiles and a lot of hugs that the kids usually give, even not just to me, but to themselves and to their teammates, the encouragement after the Lions Academy. 00:08:42.639 --> 00:08:48.799 One of the cool things was parents will come up to me and say, they really put something through to my kids. 00:08:48.799 --> 00:08:53.600 They used to fight all the time and now they're engaging in teamwork and building and helping one another. 00:08:53.600 --> 00:09:01.039 And so that confidence alone, one of the things that our foundation wants to do is create opportunities on and off the court. 00:09:01.039 --> 00:09:07.120 So you have to be a class uh teammate, sportsman-like kind of not just on the field, but off the field as well. 00:09:07.120 --> 00:09:13.519 And so that is what I'm hoping to engage in this opportunities that most kids, bigger cities have. 00:09:13.519 --> 00:09:19.679 So if we can offer that to our kids in Battle Creek, we're really building in their future and surrounding areas. 00:09:20.720 --> 00:09:31.360 All right, Pistons Academy Clinic, August 22nd at full blast, nine in the morning until 1 p.m., looking for boys and girls in grades three to nine. 00:09:31.360 --> 00:09:34.639 What do they have to do to get registered, Brittany? 00:09:34.960 --> 00:09:40.320 They can go on to the Pistons Academy.com and register that way. 00:09:40.320 --> 00:09:40.879 Okay. 00:09:41.519 --> 00:09:51.919 And we'll put a link in the show notes too at battlecreekpodcast.com in case you can't uh write that down or get to it, so you can just remember that and then find this discussion and click through. 00:09:51.919 --> 00:09:53.200 So this is August. 00:09:53.200 --> 00:09:56.080 Do they have to be you probably recommend they get to it, right? 00:09:56.080 --> 00:09:57.519 If if they want to be a part of this. 00:09:57.840 --> 00:09:59.039 Get registered right away. 00:09:59.039 --> 00:10:00.960 It is filling up very quickly. 00:10:00.960 --> 00:10:04.240 I'm just hoping soon I check and it'll say sold out. 00:10:04.240 --> 00:10:14.159 So if they are looking for sponsorships, I encourage them to text me at 269-358-8383. 00:10:14.559 --> 00:10:14.960 All right.

Welcome to the Wall Street Truthbombs channel where we cover financial news, break down the markets, and deliver hard-hitting analysis with no corporate spin. We break down complex Wall Street stories and economic developments in a way that’s clear, direct, and unfiltered — so our audience gets the truth, not the talking points.

Wall Street Truthbombs is led by its host and creator, Mark Malek, a fearless financial commentator known for cutting through media noise, and delivering bold insights on what’s really happening in the markets. With a fast-growing audience of viewers tired of watered-down finance news,  brings honesty, urgency, and edge to every episode.

00:00:27.039 This is the raw data. 00:00:27.120 --> 00:00:31.359 The soft landing crowd is praying that you do not hear from me. 00:00:31.600 --> 00:00:34.960 Here is the story you've been told for several years now. 00:00:35.200 --> 00:00:38.719 Inflation peaked, it's on a smooth glide path back down to 2%. 00:00:39.200 --> 00:00:43.119 And any day now, the central banks declare victory and start cutting rates. 00:00:43.359 --> 00:00:45.600 That is the soft landing scenario. 00:00:45.759 --> 00:00:49.920 Clearly, that has changed a bit lately with energy prices. 00:00:50.079 --> 00:00:55.200 Listen, they were talking about this gentle slope back to normal with nobody getting hurt. 00:00:55.359 --> 00:01:00.399 Retail media has been selling that slope like it's a law of physics, and they still kind of do. 00:01:00.560 --> 00:01:01.759 You just don't realize that. 00:01:01.920 --> 00:01:05.760 And for a while, the story really actually sounded very credible. 00:01:06.000 --> 00:01:07.760 Inflation did fall. 00:01:07.840 --> 00:01:11.599 The disinflation trend had been in place since early 2024. 00:01:11.840 --> 00:01:16.640 Every month the number ticked lower, and every month the crowd penciled in the next rate cut. 00:01:16.799 --> 00:01:17.680 We know about this. 00:01:17.840 --> 00:01:22.640 The entire market is priced for that slope to keep going on a long-term basis. 00:01:22.799 --> 00:01:27.519 And the confidence was still everywhere, even though it's not being published that way. 00:01:27.680 --> 00:01:29.519 Economists even gave it a name. 00:01:29.680 --> 00:01:30.719 Guess what they called it? 00:01:30.799 --> 00:01:34.079 They called it the immaculate disinflation. 00:01:34.560 --> 00:01:39.439 Inflation melting away with no recession, no jobs crash, nobody feeling the pain. 00:01:39.599 --> 00:01:44.560 Wall Street threw a party and every strategist circled the victory date on the calendar. 00:01:44.719 --> 00:01:49.359 Remember, disinflation was when when inflation slows. 00:01:49.519 --> 00:01:51.040 It's not when prices come down. 00:01:51.120 --> 00:01:52.799 That's called deflation. 00:01:53.200 --> 00:01:59.599 Okay, it became the most crowded trade on the planet that soft landing trade it did. 00:01:59.760 --> 00:02:09.599 And crowded trades are exactly where this brand lives because on Wall Street, my friends, you know what we say here all the time at Wall Street Truth Bombs, there is no free lunch. 00:02:09.680 --> 00:02:15.039 And a disinflation with no pain always had a bill coming, just a matter of when. 00:02:15.199 --> 00:02:17.280 Except the slope just flattened. 00:02:17.360 --> 00:02:20.400 And the IMF put it in the official record. 00:02:20.639 --> 00:02:24.719 Before we get deeper, if you like this type of content, please click like and don't forget to subscribe. 00:02:24.800 --> 00:02:27.199 It's important to be in the know about this kind of stuff. 00:02:27.360 --> 00:02:28.479 And this is how you do it. 00:02:28.719 --> 00:02:34.479 Okay, on July 8th, the IMF dropped its July World Economic Outlook update. 00:02:34.560 --> 00:02:39.599 And buried in the text was a sentence that should have been the headline everywhere. 00:02:39.759 --> 00:02:48.000 The disinflation trend that had been in place since early 2024 has stalled, not slowed, stalled. 00:02:48.080 --> 00:02:49.759 And they backed it with a number. 00:02:50.000 --> 00:02:58.560 The IMF revised global headline inflation up to 4.7% for 2026, up from 4.1% in 2025. 00:02:58.719 --> 00:03:00.240 Guys, you want to hear that again? 00:03:00.400 --> 00:03:01.840 I'm going to read it to you again. 00:03:02.000 --> 00:03:02.319 Okay. 00:03:02.719 --> 00:03:05.680 It's 4.7% for 2026. 00:03:05.840 --> 00:03:08.400 That's up from 4.1% in 2025. 00:03:08.560 --> 00:03:09.759 It's important to remember that. 00:03:09.919 --> 00:03:12.080 Inflation is not gliding down to 2%. 00:03:12.719 --> 00:03:16.080 Globally, it's going up this year, not down. 00:03:16.240 --> 00:03:23.199 And that's three tenths of a percent, point percent higher than the IMF itself projected back in April. 00:03:23.360 --> 00:03:28.159 The commonest voice in the room just raised its own inflation forecast and global growth. 00:03:28.319 --> 00:03:33.039 Well, they cut that as well down to 3% for 2026. 00:03:33.280 --> 00:03:36.560 So put those two together: higher inflation, slower growth. 00:03:36.719 --> 00:03:39.599 Well, my friends, that's not a soft landing. 00:03:39.680 --> 00:03:41.439 That has a very different name. 00:03:41.520 --> 00:03:46.240 And it's a word that central bankers are terrified to say out loud. 00:03:46.400 --> 00:03:49.280 But here's the part almost nobody's talking about right now. 00:03:49.439 --> 00:03:51.840 The shadow data hiding inside the report. 00:03:52.000 --> 00:03:54.240 You guys, you know I love that shadow data. 00:03:54.400 --> 00:03:57.520 The global economy is no longer moving as one. 00:03:57.840 --> 00:04:00.560 It is splitting into two separate economies. 00:04:00.719 --> 00:04:03.280 And the IMF showed the exact scene. 00:04:03.439 --> 00:04:09.360 Growth is contracting in a handful of countries plugged into the AI hardware value chain. 00:04:09.520 --> 00:04:12.319 Everyone else is getting left behind. 00:04:12.479 --> 00:04:13.919 Look at South Korea. 00:04:14.159 --> 00:04:24.800 The IMF handed it the biggest growth upgrade of any major economy, raising its 2026 forecast by seven-tenths of a percent, of a point, excuse me, to 2.6%. 00:04:25.839 --> 00:04:26.319 Why? 00:04:26.560 --> 00:04:28.480 Well, because Korea makes the chips. 00:04:28.560 --> 00:04:30.399 The AI boom cannot live without. 00:04:30.800 --> 00:04:37.600 Its economy grew at a stunning 7.5% annual pace in the first quarter against a forecast of just 1.8%. 00:04:38.240 --> 00:04:47.279 Its chip exports exploded 199.5% from a year earlier to a record$44.82 billion. 00:04:47.839 --> 00:04:52.639 Its monthly exports crossed$100 billion for the first time in its history. 00:04:52.879 --> 00:04:54.560 Now, look at the other economy. 00:04:54.720 --> 00:04:59.519 The energy importers, the consumer-driven nations with no chip industry to ride. 00:04:59.680 --> 00:05:05.199 The Euro area is crawling at just nine-tenths of a percent growth for 2026. 00:05:05.360 --> 00:05:08.800 It's being dragged down by weak consumer and high energy prices. 00:05:09.040 --> 00:05:12.160 This is a K-shaped world that we're living in now. 00:05:12.399 --> 00:05:16.240 The top of the K is the AI supply chain ripping higher. 00:05:16.399 --> 00:05:19.680 The bottom of the K is everybody else sinking. 00:05:19.759 --> 00:05:23.600 And both lines are on the same page of the same IMF report. 00:05:23.759 --> 00:05:27.839 And the United States, well, it's stuck awkwardly in the middle. 00:05:28.000 --> 00:05:37.920 Growth held at 2.3% for 2026, propped up by its own AI giants, while its everyday consumer feels the same squeeze as everybody else. 00:05:38.160 --> 00:05:39.759 You know what I'm talking about. 00:05:39.920 --> 00:05:44.720 America is not immune to the split, it's living both halves of the K at the same time. 00:05:44.959 --> 00:05:49.600 So let me ask you the question that keeps central bankers awake at night. 00:05:49.920 --> 00:06:00.560 If half of your economy is overheating on AI spending, and the other half is freezing under a weak consumer and expensive energy, what interest rate do you set? 00:06:01.040 --> 00:06:03.839 There is no right answer, unfortunately. 00:06:04.000 --> 00:06:09.120 And that is the trap that I talk about all the time here at Wall Street Truth Bombs. 00:06:09.279 --> 00:06:11.519 Here is the mechanics of that trap. 00:06:11.759 --> 00:06:16.399 To save the weak side, the struggling consumer, you would cut rates. 00:06:16.560 --> 00:06:23.680 But the moment you cut, you pour cheap money straight into the AI spending fire that's already running too hot. 00:06:23.839 --> 00:06:27.680 You refuel the exact inflation that the IMF just flagged. 00:06:27.759 --> 00:06:31.839 And to kill that sticky inflation, you would hike or you would hold. 00:06:31.920 --> 00:06:36.240 But that crushes the weak consumer who is already drowning. 00:06:36.480 --> 00:06:41.680 Every single lever they pull helps one half of the economy by hurting the other half. 00:06:41.920 --> 00:06:45.360 Think of it like a house with one thermostat and two rooms. 00:06:45.439 --> 00:06:46.800 I've told you this before. 00:06:47.040 --> 00:06:48.480 One room is on fire. 00:06:48.720 --> 00:06:50.879 That is the AI spending boom. 00:06:51.040 --> 00:06:52.560 The other room is freezing. 00:06:52.720 --> 00:06:55.759 That is the broke and struggling consumer. 00:06:55.920 --> 00:06:57.360 And you have one dial. 00:06:57.519 --> 00:07:02.800 Turn the heat down to save the burning room, or and you freeze the cold one solid. 00:07:02.959 --> 00:07:04.800 Turn it up to save the cold room. 00:07:04.959 --> 00:07:05.839 And guess what? 00:07:06.000 --> 00:07:07.519 The fire roars. 00:07:07.680 --> 00:07:11.120 That's a central bank in 2026. 00:07:11.360 --> 00:07:15.600 One dial, two economies, and no setting that works for both right now. 00:07:15.759 --> 00:07:16.959 It's one or the other. 00:07:17.199 --> 00:07:20.399 This is why the soft landing is officially dead. 00:07:20.560 --> 00:07:26.879 A soft landing needs one economy moving in one direction, so one rate can guide it down gently. 00:07:27.120 --> 00:07:32.560 You cannot soft land two economies going in opposite directions with a single steering wheel. 00:07:33.360 --> 00:07:38.240 The IMF didn't say those exact words, but the data says them for them. 00:07:38.399 --> 00:07:42.079 And here's why this lands on your kitchen table, my friends. 00:07:42.240 --> 00:07:53.759 If you've been waiting on rate cuts to refinance that mortgage, to ease that credit card burden, to lighten that car loan, the IMF just told you the ground under that bet has shifted. 00:07:53.920 --> 00:07:57.439 Stalled inflation means higher for longer is not a threat anymore. 00:07:57.600 --> 00:08:06.560 It is the base case and a global inflation floor near 4.7% means the prices on your shelf are not going back to where they were. 00:08:06.800 --> 00:08:09.439 So here's what to actually watch. 00:08:09.600 --> 00:08:11.839 It's not the victory lap, that's for sure. 00:08:12.079 --> 00:08:14.160 Watch the 2027 number. 00:08:14.319 --> 00:08:18.399 The IMF only sees inflation easing to 3.9% next year. 00:08:18.560 --> 00:08:20.399 It's still miles away from 2%. 00:08:21.040 --> 00:08:23.199 Watch the decoupling I talked about. 00:08:23.360 --> 00:08:29.839 If the AI chain economies keep ripping while the rest sink, the trap gets tighter, not looser. 00:08:30.000 --> 00:08:37.039 And watch what your own central bank does when it finally admits it can't serve both halves at once. 00:08:37.279 --> 00:08:48.960 So your truth bomb for today is this the IMF just disqualified the soft landing in its own data because you can't land two economies going in opposite directions on one runway. 00:08:49.039 --> 00:08:53.039 And the AI boom is the thing ripping that runway in half. 00:08:53.279 --> 00:08:59.039 Join me every day for Wall Street truth bombs, my friends, where I drop them right here before the market figures them out.

transcript.scoringWindow: Its Storytime with Mitch Jesserich. Today, two short German folk tales written down by the Grimm Brothers. The Grimm fairy tales are arguably the most well known folk stories in the Western world. Disney has popularized many of them with such films as Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs, and Cinderella. We were told many of the stories as kids like Little Red Riding Hood, Hansel and Gretel, and Rumpel Stillskin. The Grimm Brothers, Jakab and Wilhelm, set out in the early eighteen hundreds to collect and write down oral stories in the German language. The writing down of oral stories is what a fairy tale is. The brothers, who were academics, saw how industrialization was changing the world, and they feared the loss of these stories. So they talked to many, many people, mostly women, and ended up collecting hundreds of tells. Today the stories are seen as innocent tells meant for children. Disney played a big role in that, but so did the brothers themselves, who revised the stories many times over the years to make them more socially acceptable. But with the first publication of these tells in eighteen twelve, the original stories they were told were much darker and stranger than the ones popularized later on. Today, I read for you two short tells. The first, a ghost story, connected to the high rate of infant mortality of the day. Its called the Stolen Pennies. The second one is shockingly gruesome and shows these werent necessarily for kids. These are oral stories that was the media of their day and meant for everyone. The stolen pennies. One day a father was sitting at the table with his wife and children, and a good friend who was visiting him, and they were having their noonday meal. As they were sitting there, the clock struck twelve, and the visitor saw the door open, and a pale child dressed in snow white clothes entered. He didnt look around or say anything, but went silently into the next room. Shortly thereafter he returned and went away just as quietly as he had entered. On the second and third day the child came again. Finally, the visitor asked the father who the beautiful child was that entered the room every day at noon. The father answered that he knew nothing about him. He hadnt seen anything. The next day, as the clock struck noon, the child entered again, and the visitor pointed the child out to the father. But he didnt see the boy. Neither did the mother nor the children. The visitor stood up, went to the door, opened it a little, and looked inside. There he saw the pale child sitting on the floor, digging and rummaging in the cracks of the boards. However, as soon as the child noticed the visitor, he disappeared. Now the visitor told the family what he had seen and gave an exact description of the boy. The mother was then able to recognize the child and said Alas, its my own dear child who died four weeks ago. Then they ripped up the boards on the floor and found two pennies that the boy had received from his mother at one time to give to a poor man, but the child had thought, You can buy yourself a biscuit for that. Therefore, he had kept the pennies and had hidden them in the cracks of the floor. This is why he hadnt been able to rest in his grave, and had come back every day at noon to look for the pennies. So the parents gave the money to a poor man, and after that the little child was never seen again. The second tell is part two of a story called How Some Children Played at Slaughtering. There once was a father who slaughtered a pig, and his children saw that. In the afternoon, when they began playing, one child said to the other, You be the little pig, and Ill be the butcher. He then took a shiny knife and slit his little brothers throat. Their mother was upstairs in a room bathing another child, and when she heard the cries of her son, she immediately ran downstairs. Upon seeing what had happened, she took the knife out of her sons throat and was so enraged that she stabbed the heart of the other boy, who had been playing the butcher. Then she quickly ran back to the room to tend to her child in the bathtub, but while she had been gone, he had drowned in the tub. Now the woman became so frightened and desperate that she wouldnt allow the neighbors to comfort her, and finally hung herself. When her husband came back from the fields and saw everything, he became so despondent that he died soon thereafter. Two Tales from the Grim Brothers. And that does it for story time. Until next time, be well.

structure.rationale: The episode has a clear intro contextualizing the Grimm Brothers, followed by two sequentially presented stories with a brief close, though pacing is entirely linear with minimal signposting between sections.

relevance.rationale: This episode is a reading of 19th-century German folk tales with no connection to current news or political events.

clipability.rationale: The gruesome second tale has shock value as a standalone moment, but both stories require the framing introduction to land properly and neither excerpt would function as a compelling news/politics preview.

card.tagline: Two dark Grimm tales read aloud — before Disney sanitized them
card.description: Mitch Jeserich reads the original, unrevised 1812 Grimm Brothers stories: a ghost haunting his own pennies and a shockingly violent children's game gone wrong.
card.reason: Because you enjoy narrative storytelling rooted in historical cultural context

move worth rewarding. 00:04:04.240 --> 00:04:08.479 September Chicago ended 20 and three quarter cents higher at 640 and a quarter. 00:04:08.639 --> 00:04:13.680 September KC closed at 676 and a quarter, 22 cents higher on the day. 00:04:13.919 --> 00:04:18.879 And canola actually continues to quietly be one of the stronger markets in the grain complex. 00:04:18.959 --> 00:04:28.959 And while today's WASDI offered only modest changes to the global oil seed outlook, demand for vegetable oils does remain supportive and continues to provide an underlying bid to the market. 00:04:29.120 --> 00:04:36.800 Global oil seed ending stocks were trimmed slightly while crushed trade do continue to expand, just reinforcing that long-term demand story. 00:04:36.959 --> 00:04:46.399 So our managed bushels clients are about 30% sold on new crop production, giving us flexibility to stay patient here while taking advantage of some additional rallies. 00:04:46.560 --> 00:04:59.279 Um November Canola Futures did finish the week at$7.77 Canadian, um, three three dollars and sixty cents higher today, actually gaining over$37 um Canadian dollars this week. 00:04:59.519 --> 00:05:02.480 And the livestock markets are moving in opposite directions. 00:05:02.639 --> 00:05:07.600 Cattle really struggled with poor technical action, while hogs actually continue to build some momentum today. 00:05:07.759 --> 00:05:14.399 August live cattle have closed lower for nine consecutive sessions and are testing the bottom of a broad three-month consolidation. 00:05:14.639 --> 00:05:22.399 Cash trade also added some pressure with light business reported at$2.48 in both the north and south, roughly$7 below last week. 00:05:22.639 --> 00:05:28.079 Hogs, meanwhile, are benefiting from fund short covering, uh strong volume and improving technicals. 00:05:28.160 --> 00:05:35.120 So August live cattle did slip five cents to settle at$235.20, while October fell$1.05, settling at$2.30.55. 00:05:35.279 --> 00:05:39.439 December dropped$1.27.5 to close at$2.30.27.5. 00:05:39.759 --> 00:05:41.199 Feeder cattle were also weaker. 00:05:41.360 --> 00:05:52.639 August feeders declined$1.55, closing at$354.60, while September and October both fell$1.97.5, settling at$351.025 and$37.35. 00:05:52.879 --> 00:05:55.759 Lean Hog Futures have the stronger setup. 00:05:55.839 --> 00:06:03.279 Um after October contracts held Wednesday sharp gains, rising volume, declining open interest do suggest the funds are short covering. 00:06:03.439 --> 00:06:11.279 Uh July Hogs did gain 45 cents, closing at 94.77.5, while August added 85 cents, settling at$99 even. 00:06:11.439 --> 00:06:15.600 October slipped 57.5 cents to 8507.5. 00:06:16.079 --> 00:06:20.879 Pullbacks here should attract some buying interest as long as that breakout here remains intact. 00:06:21.040 --> 00:06:24.079 So again, if you have any questions, as always, feel free to reach out. 00:06:24.160 --> 00:06:25.680 Otherwise, we hope you have a great weekend. 00:06:25.759 --> 00:06:26.240 Stay cool. 00:06:26.319 --> 00:06:27.519 We'll talk to you again next week.

scored.description: The Quantum AI Digest is your daily gateway to the fast-evolving world of artificial intelligence, delivering fresh episodes packed with the latest AI industry news. Each day, we curate the most significant advancements from the past 24 hours, offering a vibrant blend of breakthroughs, policies, and trends shaping the AI landscape. From pioneering research to transformative applications, our podcast distills complex updates into clear, engaging summaries for tech enthusiasts, developers, and curious minds.

latestEpisode.title: The Quantum AI Digest: 12 July 2026 Daily Dispatch

clip.text: customers to oppose proposed state legislation that could restrict driverless vehicle adoption. 00:01:55.200 --> 00:01:57.040 At the federal level, U.S. 00:01:57.200 --> 00:02:07.439 regulators are now requiring all autonomous vehicle companies to enhance safety protocols, particularly to avoid interference with emergency responders. 00:02:07.760 --> 00:02:12.879 Manufacturing and industrial automation are also seeing significant advancements. 00:02:13.120 --> 00:02:23.759 Former Tesla scientist Remy Kadin has introduced Northstar, a faceless humanoid robot designed for European factories, which can learn tasks by demonstration. 00:02:23.919 --> 00:02:37.840 French startup Mistral is moving into physical AI with Robostrol Navigate, a platform built for compatibility across diverse industrial systems, following its acquisition of MEAI. 00:02:38.000 --> 00:02:44.960

transcript.scoringWindow: WEBVTT 00:00:00.239 --> 00:00:08.800 In the fast evolving world of artificial intelligence, recent breakthroughs are propelling both technology and society into a new era. 00:00:09.199 --> 00:00:19.120 China has made history by successfully recovering its long March 10B rocket booster, landing it vertically on a floating platform just six minutes after liftoff. 00:00:19.359 --> 00:00:28.879 This accomplishment puts China in the elite company of SpaceX and Blue Origin, marking its official entry into the reusable era of orbital launches. 00:00:29.120 --> 00:00:38.960 At the same time, China is gaining ground in robotics innovation, with Tsinghua University winning the RoboCup Humanoid League two years in a row. 00:00:39.200 --> 00:00:44.960 Meanwhile, robotics and AI are making headlines in live demonstrations and sports. 00:00:45.200 --> 00:01:00.240 At the 2026 FIFA World Cup, Hyundai's Atlas Humanoid Robot performed goal celebrations and delivered the matchball right on the pitch, underscoring robotics' increasing reliability in high pressure, public settings. 00:01:00.479 --> 00:01:11.840 Elsewhere, Norwegian Company One Deggs debuted its neo-humanoid robots' highly dexterous hands, capable of delicate tasks like picking grapes and even communicating through sign language. 00:01:12.000 --> 00:01:26.000 In broadcasting, ESPN's coverage of the World Series of Poker now features an AI analyst that reads player body language to spot bluffs, giving us a glimpse into AI's growing role in entertainment. 00:01:26.319 --> 00:01:43.200 On the transportation front, Waymo has expanded its driverless robotaxi network into four more United States cities, San Diego, Las Vegas, Tampa, and Denver, bringing fully autonomous rides to more than a dozen metropolitan areas. 00:01:43.439 --> 00:01:54.959 Regulatory developments are following closely as Tesla is encouraging New Jersey customers to oppose proposed state legislation that could restrict driverless vehicle adoption. 00:01:55.200 --> 00:01:57.040 At the federal level, U.S. 00:01:57.200 --> 00:02:07.439 regulators are now requiring all autonomous vehicle companies to enhance safety protocols, particularly to avoid interference with emergency responders. 00:02:07.760 --> 00:02:12.879 Manufacturing and industrial automation are also seeing significant advancements. 00:02:13.120 --> 00:02:23.759 Former Tesla scientist Remy Kadin has introduced Northstar, a faceless humanoid robot designed for European factories, which can learn tasks by demonstration. 00:02:23.919 --> 00:02:37.840 French startup Mistral is moving into physical AI with Robostrol Navigate, a platform built for compatibility across diverse industrial systems, following its acquisition of MEAI. 00:02:38.000 --> 00:02:44.960 As corporate AI competition intensifies, several major players are rolling out new models and strategies. 00:02:45.439 --> 00:02:53.280 OpenAI has launched ChatGPT Work, a specialized enterprise platform designed to streamline business operations. 00:02:53.599 --> 00:03:06.400 Elon Musk's SpaceX AI is pushing the envelope with Grok 4.5, and Meta's Muse AI is entering the generative image sector, ramping up competition for creative media tools. 00:03:06.639 --> 00:03:20.159 Microsoft, meanwhile, has announced the layoff of 4,800 employees as it pivots to an AI-first strategy and invests$2.5 billion in AI and cloud infrastructure. 00:03:20.400 --> 00:03:24.080 On the consumer side, innovation continues at pace. 00:03:24.319 --> 00:03:34.240 Lissum has introduced the R1, a compact countertop dishwasher that uses AI-powered sensors to optimize water usage for small homes. 00:03:34.400 --> 00:03:40.719 Google's new home speaker, powered by Gemini AI, offers advanced device control for$99. 00:03:41.280 --> 00:03:43.599 Wearables are also evolving rapidly. 00:03:43.759 --> 00:03:57.280 The MemoCat Gem Wristband or Watch Clip On captures and contextualizes conversations, while the Memo Mind 1 smart glasses provide hands-free access to AI-powered information via a private micro LED display. 00:03:57.520 --> 00:04:02.400 Health diagnostics are seeing remarkable progress with young innovators leading the way. 00:04:02.639 --> 00:04:19.199 The Retina Mind system created by 17-year-old Edward Kang helps distinguish between autism, ADHD, and typical development using AI analyzed retinal scans, combined with genomics reporting 89% accuracy and early diagnosis. 00:04:19.439 --> 00:04:37.120 Broader trends include the integration of AI into live media, as seen in sports broadcasting, and the rapid deployment of robots into retail and food service, most notably in South Korea, where some cafes now operate staff free, with profit margins soaring above 40%. 00:04:37.600 --> 00:04:50.319 Experts are emphasizing the importance of data quality over quantity and AI model development and caution that commercial pressures can increase risks for harmful outcomes, underlining the ongoing need for strong governance. 00:04:50.560 --> 00:04:57.680 The field is also benefiting from innovations in material science, like active hydrophobic concrete that repels water. 00:04:57.920 --> 00:05:00.800 Research institutions continue to make headlines. 00:05:00.959 --> 00:05:12.480 At UC San Diego, preclinical surgeries are now being performed by teleoperated humanoid robots using standard surgical tools, a major step toward widespread deployment. 00:05:12.720 --> 00:05:22.879 In medical imaging, a new metamaterial MRI antenna enables faster and clearer scans of the eye and brain while reducing risk for patients with implants. 00:05:23.199 --> 00:05:32.000 Simple blood tests measuring immature immune cells now outperform previous biomarkers in predicting short-term survival after heart attacks. 00:05:32.240 --> 00:05:35.920 Significant scientific milestones extend beyond AI. 00:05:36.160 --> 00:05:46.720 For the first time, researchers recorded real-time formation of new ocean floor during an Indian Ocean earthquake, documenting three feet of seabed growth in a single event. 00:05:46.959 --> 00:05:50.879 Across the globe, regional AI landscapes are shifting rapidly. 00:05:51.120 --> 00:05:52.959 China is narrowing the gap with U.S. 00:05:53.120 --> 00:05:54.639 space and robotics leaders. 00:05:54.879 --> 00:06:03.920 South Korea is using robotics to tackle workforce shortages, and new robotics centers in the Netherlands aim to enhance Europe's position in the field. 00:06:04.160 --> 00:06:09.040 The United States continues to lead in autonomous mobility with Waymo's expansion. 00:06:09.279 --> 00:06:25.759 Public intrigue with human augmentation is making waves online as MIT's AI powered muscle stibulation technology goes viral, while staged viral videos such as the recent rogue robot incident in Indonesia highlight misunderstandings about robotic capabilities. 00:06:25.920 --> 00:06:34.480 Meanwhile, a Japanese study on phantom traffic jams is trending, illustrating how small factors can ripple through complex systems. 00:06:34.720 --> 00:06:47.519 Finally, the community behind AI innovation thrives on engagement, with newsletters urging feedback and collaboration, and sponsors supporting the growth of open source tools and advanced training resources. 00:06:48.160 --> 00:07:02.319 On a historical note, the infamous 1992 Pepsi lottery software glitch in the Philippines, which incorrectly notified 800,000 winners, serves as a reminder of the consequences that can arise from minute computer errors. 00:07:02.560 --> 00:07:10.399 And, as the science community reflects on its pioneers, tributes are paid to the legendary aviator Wally Funk. 00:07:10.560 --> 00:07:14.399 These are the latest highlights in artificial intelligence and robotics. 00:07:14.639 --> 00:07:17.920 We'll continue to bring you updates as new developments unfold.

structure.rationale: The episode moves through clearly segmented topic clusters (robotics, transportation, corporate AI, consumer tech, health, science) with brief transitional phrases, though the rapid-fire bulletin format lacks deliberate pacing or a meaningful close.

relevance.rationale: This is a technology and science digest covering AI, robotics, and space developments — not a News/Politics episode; while it briefly mentions regulatory actions around autonomous vehicles, the episode's core subject matter is tech industry and science news, not political or governmental affairs.

clipability.rationale: A few items — the China rocket recovery, the FIFA World Cup robot, or the 17-year-old's retinal scan diagnostic — could stand alone briefly, but most entries are too terse and list-like to function as compelling standalone previews without surrounding context.

card.tagline: China lands rockets, robots deliver World Cup balls, and AI reshapes everything
card.description: A rapid-fire daily dispatch covering China's reusable rocket milestone, Waymo's four-city expansion, Microsoft layoffs, and a teen's AI retinal diagnostic tool.
card.reason: Because this episode surveys the week's biggest AI and robotics breakthroughs

structure.rationale: Open-lines call-in format with loose conversational flow; some throughline around the WNBA/Fever topic but no clear segments, signposting, or deliberate pacing.

relevance.rationale: The episode is primarily sports talk — WNBA Fever analysis, Caitlin Clark commentary, NBA fashion debate — with no substantive political or news content driving the discussion.

clipability.rationale: A few quotable sports opinions surface (turnover ratio argument, fashion-vs-performance debate) but nearly all moments require surrounding caller context to land.

scored.description:
Tired of the mainstream media? You’re not alone.
Hosted by Dr. Steve Turley—best-selling author, political analyst, and the "Patriot Professor"—the Turley Talks Podcast delivers bold, faith-filled commentary on the biggest stories shaping politics and culture. From the rise of nationalism and populism to breaking news and election updates, this daily podcast is your front-row seat to the conservative revolution reshaping America and the world.
You'll love Dr. Steve’s hopeful, unapologetically pro-America first perspective. Learn how to stand strong for faith, family, and freedom—and discover how patriots like you are building a new conservative age.
Subscribe now and join the movement.

relevance.rationale: The episode substantively covers the U.S.-Iran military conflict, Trump's negotiation posture, Congressional war powers, and Strait of Hormuz energy geopolitics — clearly on-topic for News/Politics despite the misleading episode title.

clipability.rationale: Trump's 'they would be obliterated' Oval Office quote and the Strait of Hormuz pipeline analysis both work as near-standalone moments, though most caller exchanges require surrounding context.

card.tagline: Should Trump have kept bombing Iran instead of pausing for burials?

scored.description:
WagerTalk is a daily sports betting podcast directly from Vegas that prepares our audience to make the most educated bets possible. Listen to Vegas experts cover the National Football League (NFL), College Football, Major League Baseball (MLB), National Basketball Association (NBA), College Basketball, National Hockey League (NHL), Soccer, Mixed Martial Arts (MMA), Horse Racing and NASCAR from a Las Vegas betting perspective. Our panel of professional handicappers provides free sports picks, sports predictions, and betting odds covering all major sporting events to help you beat the spread and make money wagering sports.

Make sure to check out our handicappers at WagerTalk.com and follow the WagerTalk crew on Twitter @WagerTalk and Sportsmemo at Sportsmemo.com and @sportsmemo.
