structure.rationale: The episode has a clear conversational throughline moving from the LaMelo trade to Celtics/Jalen Brown discussions, with identifiable topic transitions, though pacing is loose and dialogue occasionally rambles.

relevance.rationale: This is NBA trade analysis and basketball operations content — sports subject matter that scores in the 0-20 range regardless of analytical depth or specificity.

clipability.rationale: A few quotable moments exist — particularly the 'verbal pretzel' line about Brad Stevens and the Giannis aging argument — but most exchanges require prior context about the trade rumors to land.

card.tagline: Evan Turner breaks down why the Celtics may be done with Jalen Brown
card.description: SI's NBA crew and Evan Turner dissect the Celtics' aggressive push to trade Jalen Brown after his best season yet, and what four first-round picks says about Boston's direction.
card.reason: Because you follow NBA front office moves and roster shake-ups

scored.description:
Manchester City news, opinions and updates brought to you on The City Social by the Esteemed Kompany YouTube channel. A whole range of guests join Steven to talk all things Man City and football multiple times a week. Blues who have followed their team for years, journalists with expertise knowledge of the goings on around the club, entertaining content creators from varied backgrounds and many more.

From previews, to reactions, transfer news, opposition debates and academy updates, this is a podcast for all Man City fans. If you want to join me and thousands of other blues from across the world, hit that subscribe button to support a lifelong blue!

Follow Esteemed Kompany on various other platforms:

🔗 www.esteemedkompany.com

📹 Youtube.com/esteemedkompany

🎙️ x.com/stevenmcinerney

📸 instagram.com/esteemedkompany

🤳 tiktok.com/@steven_mcinerney

structure.rationale: The episode follows a clear format of cycling through transfer ins and outs with named players, though individual segments meander with repetitive hedging and tangents.

structure.rationale: The segment is driven by listener texts with no clear throughline — it meanders from conspiracy theory debunking to ballistics to app complaints, with loose connective tissue throughout.

relevance.rationale: The episode centers on the Charlie Kirk murder case and Candace Owens conspiracy theories — a specific, timely political media story with named figures and ongoing legal proceedings.

clipability.rationale: The host's rebuttal listing Tyler Robinson's DNA evidence and confessions is a genuinely self-contained, quotable moment, and the 'canspiracy theorists' coinage is a usable standalone hook.

card.description: Kaliner walks through pretrial evidence in the Charlie Kirk murder case while fielding listener texts debunking Candace Owens' conspiracy claims point by point.

scored.description: Catch two of British Columbia's most admired and respected sports journalists Monday to Friday on CHEK. Don Taylor and Rick Dhaliwal host Donnie and Dhali - The Team. Follow along with Donnie and Dhali as they interview guests, and have fun discussing the latest in local sports news. Tune in live from 10am to 12pm on CHEK, the CHEK+ app or download the Podcast to listen whenever!

structure.rationale: The call-in format provides a loose but followable throughline, with the host guiding callers through distinct sub-topics, though transitions are conversational rather than deliberate.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers local municipal issues — illegal ATV/e-scooter use and a town hall rebuild — which are loosely political (local governance, provincial legislation) but lack national or broadly timely news hooks.

clipability.rationale: A few quotable moments exist (e.g., 'it's the wild West when it comes to dirt bikes') but most exchanges require the surrounding call-in context to land properly.

card.tagline: Paradise mayor calls out ATV chaos and demands provincial action now
card.description: VOCM's open line hears from Paradise's mayor on illegal dirt bikes, e-scooters tearing up parks, and a town hall still under repair after a fire.
card.reason: Because this episode covers local governance and public safety debates in your region

scored.description: Charles Hanagriff and Jimmy Ott, a partnership spanning over three decades of sports in Baton Rouge, are back together on Live at Lunch. The show will cover all local sports with an increased emphasis on sports gaming.

latestEpisode.title: Belgium vs Spain Preview, World Cup Weekend

Right if you are really trying to make sure that your game just comes out when you decide it comes out, then sure, do a digital only release, But like, are you wasting everyone's time with the boxes? Yeah? Why? And to that point, like I would imagine that retailer like probably pissing off retailers a little bit, because retailers are kind of in a no win situation here because it's like either don't carry Grand Theft Auto six or carry it. But you're also going to make significantly less margins on this because it's a digital download. Plus you have to deal with the aftermath of maybe people not realizing that there's a code if you don't explain it, or trying to return it. And it's trust as someone who lived in that world, digital downloads in the retail space, while while unfortunate, SSD are a headache to be sure, So, like I said, putting aside, and that's aside from the fact that just this is this to me is like one of the loudest and most final males in the coffin of physical games. Really, like, if GTA six isn't going to be physical, every other company out there could be like, well then why the fuck you know, do we need to be at that point? So it's this, This might be the true death knell of at least like modern big you know, big retailer triple games. You're still going to see like your limited runs and stuff like that out there doing their thing. I'm sure for the people that are the true sickos, but I think as far as like your big box game stores and stuff like that, we might see a dwindling effect, much as we did in the PC space in the late late aughts slash early twenty tens, where physical just simply ceased to be a thing as far as PC games go. The final bit of news on g t A this they they did confirm that this is going to be Uh there was a single player experience only at launch, meaning no online when the game first comes out. So but yeah, there it is. Pre Orders have opened already. Why you would rush the pre order again to the out of six, I don't know. I don't see them running out of digital key digital Solid game on marketplaces. So, but there is that for sure. Yeah, you should get to go. So uh kind of in the in the vein of the g this is a confluence of our two prior stories connected. Yes, yeah, so bringing it together. A senior games buyer for an unnamed retailer did an interview with a site called The Game Business, and according to them, the combination of component shortages and price increases meat combined with GTA six releasing this fall, thinks that it's likely their words, that we will have a situation this holiday season where consoles actually sell out at major retailers and people might not be able to get them for the holiday season. To put it frankly, according to this person, quote demand will likely outstrip supply during the year end period. Matthew Ball, who's Xbox's new chief strategy officer, basically echoed this a couple of weeks ago with the Game Business as well, saying that quote, I can tell you definitively demand for our console exceeds the supply, which if the Xbox people are saying that, I can only imagine what the PlayStation situation looks like, cidering no one's fucking buying anyways. To be interesting to see how this pans out this holiday season. You know, it's fewer consoles are being made because they're too expensive and people aren't buying them. So it's like the demand for a console right now isn't there. So it's like, are they going to try and stockpile for the holiday season knowing that a lot of people are just not going to be able to afford it anyway. So yeah, they kind I feel like they're going to have to because they are going to have like Q four earnings expectations that if if that demand does get out stripped, it's going to be really difficult to meet. And and by the way, it's coming again coming from the retail background, that is something that is not as uncommon as you think it is. Like we we very often would get like Black Friday bundles or contected to hold a specific allocation of certain things aside or big sales periods and stuff like that. Yeah, I remember getting shipments basically the week before Thanksgiving, right of like that Monday, Tuesday before Thanksgiving, it was just like you're getting a fuck ton of console ship to you because you know, the warehouses have just been sitting on them and they know that they're going to sell that week. So so it's happens. Yeah, but that I mean again, that'll be really really fucking interesting to see what the deal is there. And again with GTA specifically, the other thing that we did for that, we failed to mention no PC launch from the jump, It's only going to be on PlayStation and Xbox. So you know, if if people were holding out for this game to upgrade to that new console, you probably should do it now and maybe not wait until GTA comes out if you can, because yeah, you know, if this is if this stuff is true, then it's going to be pretty pretty ugly when we when we get there. So yeah, more shitty days. As I'm read here, really doesn't get any better. The inevitable has happened, but it doesn't make it any less frustrating. We knew this was coming. We're working with our suppliers to lower the product carbon 201 00:08:22,830 --> 00:08:24,110 footprints of the things we buy. 202 00:08:24,150 --> 00:08:27,420 But that's the actual plan that we're expectations. 205 00:08:33,330 --> 00:08:34,600 It's very similar. 206 00:08:35,230 --> 00:08:37,670 Sustainability can learn a lot from the finance field. 207 00:08:37,990 --> 00:08:38,990 It's not that different. 208 00:08:39,922 --> 00:08:42,702 Steve Odland: Now, at DuPont, you've talked about Scope 1, 2, and 3 209 00:08:42,802 --> 00:08:45,152 emissions, and you're tracking that. 210 00:08:45,442 --> 00:08:48,192 Can you just help our listeners understand what is Scope 1, what 211 00:08:48,192 --> 00:08:51,562 is Scope 2, what is Scope 3, and then how do companies directly 212 00:08:52,132 --> 00:08:55,342 impact each set of carbon emissions? 213 00:08:55,722 --> 00:08:56,002 Scott Collick: Sure. 214 00:08:56,102 --> 00:08:59,772 So Scope 1 would be your direct carbon dioxide or carbon dioxide equivalents. 215 00:08:59,772 --> 00:09:03,542 There's other gases that have a global warming potential. 216 00:09:03,542 --> 00:09:06,212 We convert all those into a carbon dioxide equivalent. 217 00:09:06,472 --> 00:09:07,892 So those are your direct emissions. 218 00:09:08,642 --> 00:09:11,902 Scope 2 is the electricity you use and the emissions from 219 00:09:11,902 --> 00:09:13,402 the electricity that you use. 220 00:09:13,782 --> 00:09:15,862 And then Scope 3 has two components. 221 00:09:15,862 --> 00:09:19,342 Your upstream, so all the raw materials, your purchased goods and 222 00:09:19,342 --> 00:09:21,702 services, your logistics upstream. 223 00:09:22,212 --> 00:09:25,872 And then you also have a downstream Scope 3, which is logistics. 224 00:09:26,162 --> 00:09:29,432 It's how the product is used in use, and it's also end of life, as well 225 00:09:29,432 --> 00:09:30,922 as a variety of other categories. 226 00:09:30,932 --> 00:09:34,792 But it's really, your direct emissions, electricity, and your upstream and 227 00:09:34,792 --> 00:09:36,982 downstream emissions make up all scopes. 228 00:09:37,442 --> 00:09:40,142 Steve Odland: Yeah, and so Scope 1 and 2 seem pretty straightforward. 229 00:09:40,142 --> 00:09:42,202 Scope 1 is in your control. 230 00:09:42,202 --> 00:09:46,548 It may not be easy to reduce, but it's in your control cause it's coming from your 231 00:09:46,548 --> 00:09:48,735 plants or your products or your offices. 232 00:09:49,255 --> 00:09:52,675 Scope 2 is a little harder, but also relatively easy, cause 233 00:09:52,675 --> 00:09:54,405 it's tracked to electricity. 234 00:09:54,475 --> 00:09:55,575 You know what it is. 235 00:09:56,225 --> 00:09:58,265 But Scope 3's hard, Scott, isn't it? 236 00:09:58,265 --> 00:10:01,895 I mean, cause you're dealing with up and down the supply chain, and it's 237 00:10:01,895 --> 00:10:05,635 through your vendors and suppliers, who you don't directly control. 238 00:10:05,635 --> 00:10:09,375 But you can influence it, and you guys have done a great job at that. 239 00:10:09,855 --> 00:10:12,085 Scott Collick: Yeah, so that's actually my biggest headache, so we're 240 00:10:12,085 --> 00:10:13,855 well on our way on our Scope 1 and 2. 241 00:10:13,855 --> 00:10:18,495 We're down 76% on our Scope 1 and 2 emissions since 2019, because 242 00:10:18,495 --> 00:10:20,435 that's been our constant focus. 243 00:10:20,435 --> 00:10:23,705 So much, most of my attention is now on Scope 3. 244 00:10:24,715 --> 00:10:27,255 And it's really our inbound raw materials, to be honest with you. 245 00:10:27,255 --> 00:10:29,865 So, right now, and most companies are doing that, you 246 00:10:29,865 --> 00:10:31,825 start with a spend-based model. 247 00:10:31,845 --> 00:10:34,905 I spend $1 million dollars times some factor, and then 248 00:10:34,905 --> 00:10:36,135 that's your carbon emissions. 249 00:10:36,165 --> 00:10:38,935 Well, the only way that you can lower the emissions is spend less. 250 00:10:39,285 --> 00:10:42,665 So it's really important for companies to switch from spend-based 251 00:10:42,665 --> 00:10:44,465 models to activity-based models. 252 00:10:44,475 --> 00:10:49,765 So I would tell you, we're almost at 60% activity-based model, and 253 00:10:49,765 --> 00:10:52,025 we're at almost 30% primary data. 254 00:10:52,025 --> 00:10:55,255 That's actually where our suppliers give us a product carbon footprint 255 00:10:55,275 --> 00:10:57,645 of the materials that we buy, and that's really important for 256 00:10:57,645 --> 00:10:59,045 the next phase of the journey. 257 00:10:59,285 --> 00:11:02,465 You have to transition from spend-based models to actual 258 00:11:02,585 --> 00:11:06,655 activity-based models to know the carbon of the inbound raw materials. 259 00:11:06,655 --> 00:11:08,905 But it's my biggest headache right now, Steve. 260 00:11:09,295 --> 00:11:13,085 Steve Odland: And this goes back to your point that there has to be carbon fluency. 261 00:11:13,508 --> 00:11:16,218 And that means that you've got to understand what it is, where 262 00:11:16,218 --> 00:11:17,468 it is, you've got to track it. 263 00:11:17,528 --> 00:11:20,448 It's not only in your direct operations, but it's all the way 264 00:11:20,448 --> 00:11:21,948 up Scope Two and Scope Three. 265 00:11:22,258 --> 00:11:23,348 So it's pretty broad here. 266 00:11:23,368 --> 00:11:29,168 Now, you know, you hear a lot about these ESG-style frameworks, or regulatory 267 00:11:29,178 --> 00:11:32,118 frameworks, and there are a lot of these competing ones around the world. 268 00:11:33,140 --> 00:11:36,603 How do you simplify this, Scott, so that it's truly applicable? 269 00:11:36,623 --> 00:11:40,443 Cause if it sounds like rocket science, people will freak out on it, and they 270 00:11:40,443 --> 00:11:42,543 don't really adopt it and make progress. 271 00:11:42,543 --> 00:11:45,553 But I think you've talked about simplifying the framework. 272 00:11:45,563 --> 00:11:46,543 What do you mean by that? 273 00:11:46,543 --> 00:11:48,523 And how do you advise companies to do that? 274 00:11:48,904 --> 00:11:49,274 Scott Collick: Yeah. 275 00:11:49,334 --> 00:11:52,024 So, one, I like to start with business, not the framework. 276 00:11:52,024 --> 00:11:55,963 So, I like to get, at my company, I like the carbon data at a company 277 00:11:55,963 --> 00:11:58,963 level, and then I break it down by business and a product level. 278 00:11:59,314 --> 00:12:01,173 It's got to be how your business runs. 279 00:12:01,173 --> 00:12:02,624 Don't fit to the framework. 280 00:12:02,824 --> 00:12:04,304 Look at it how your business runs. 281 00:12:04,593 --> 00:12:07,924 But I will tell you, the key step that I found is, and this is where most 282 00:12:07,934 --> 00:12:11,334 companies fall short, is I think it's really important to normalize the data, 283 00:12:11,374 --> 00:12:13,673 and turn it into an intensity metric. 284 00:12:14,253 --> 00:12:16,233 Greenhouse gas per million dollars of revenue. 285 00:12:16,253 --> 00:12:18,374 So I'll go back to my iPhone 17 example. 286 00:12:18,863 --> 00:12:23,044 So I was meeting with one of our LCA practitioners, talking about this 287 00:12:23,054 --> 00:12:26,884 iPhone 17, and I said, "Hey, this has 55 kilograms of carbon dioxide." 288 00:12:26,884 --> 00:12:30,490 And she goes, "Wow." And I said, "You know, that actually is 300 pounds 289 00:12:30,490 --> 00:12:34,510 per pound of unit," cause iPhone only weighs about 0.2 kilograms. 290 00:12:34,800 --> 00:12:38,010 She goes, "Wow, that's incredibly high." By the way, that would be 291 00:12:38,010 --> 00:12:41,790 higher than any product that we make at DuPont, 300 kilograms of carbon 292 00:12:41,790 --> 00:12:43,240 dioxide per kilogram of product. 293 00:12:43,640 --> 00:12:47,480 But I said, "You're looking at it wrong. This is incredibly value-added. 294 00:12:47,820 --> 00:12:51,660 If you look at it on an economic intensity, it's only about 70 metric 295 00:12:51,670 --> 00:12:53,190 tons per million dollars of revenue." 296 00:12:54,060 --> 00:12:56,460 And it's really important that you have to look at both lenses. 297 00:12:56,540 --> 00:13:00,500 Engineers look at it, things on a kilogram per kilogram, but financial 298 00:13:00,500 --> 00:13:04,990 people look at things from a metric tons or kilograms per million dollars 299 00:13:04,990 --> 00:13:06,460 of revenue, and I think it's important. 300 00:13:06,500 --> 00:13:09,360 And that's one of the things, is you got to link it much closer 301 00:13:09,360 --> 00:13:10,480 to how you run the business. 302 00:13:10,500 --> 00:13:12,240 We run business in dollars. 303 00:13:13,390 --> 00:13:13,620 Steve Odland: Yeah. 304 00:13:13,650 --> 00:13:16,340 Scott Collick: And then I like to look at every one of our businesses 305 00:13:16,370 --> 00:13:17,990 on all these intensity metrics. 306 00:13:18,040 --> 00:13:19,020 And it normalizes. 307 00:13:19,030 --> 00:13:22,330 Not all our businesses and not all companies are the same size revenue. 308 00:13:22,330 --> 00:13:25,946 So I think that's really important to add onto the frameworks, to start looking 309 00:13:25,946 --> 00:13:28,546 at things on an economic-intensity basis. 310 00:13:29,026 --> 00:13:31,756 Steve Odland: Yeah, and it does allow comparability too, across 311 00:13:31,756 --> 00:13:36,066 organizations, both kilogram for kilogram, but also kilograms per dollar. 312 00:13:36,096 --> 00:13:37,476 It's really an important point. 313 00:13:37,516 --> 00:13:37,726 Scott Collick: Yeah. 314 00:13:38,406 --> 00:13:42,356 Steve Odland: We're talking about carbon, carbon fluency, and sustainability 315 00:13:42,406 --> 00:13:44,736 metrics at DuPont and broadly. 316 00:13:44,976 --> 00:13:46,986 We're going to take a short break and be right back. 317 00:13:47,746 --> 00:13:49,636 Welcome back to C-Suite Perspectives. 318 00:13:49,676 --> 00:13:52,756 I'm your host, Steve Odland, from the Conference Board, and I'm joined 319 00:13:52,756 --> 00:13:56,376 today by Scott Collick, the chief sustainability officer at DuPont. 320 00:13:56,916 --> 00:14:01,286 Scott, really good discussion before the break on frameworks and how to 321 00:14:01,286 --> 00:14:02,776 simplify it and how to think about it. 322 00:14:02,776 --> 00:14:06,686 And really helpful to think about it not only in terms of kilogram per kilogram, 323 00:14:06,686 mistakes that companies 326 00:14:15,066 --> 00:14:17,490 make when they're trying to get into this, and they're trying to 327 00:14:17,490 --> 00:14:19,060 talk about their carbon footprint? 328 00:14:19,970 --> 00:14:20,230 Scott Collick: Yeah. 329 00:14:20,230 --> 00:14:23,190 So the first one is the numbers without the context, that we talked about. 330 00:14:23,190 --> 00:14:27,160 But the really second one is treating all carbon reductions as equally 331 00:14:27,160 --> 00:14:29,960 valuable without cost or business impact. 332 00:14:29,970 --> 00:14:32,120 So, like, we talk about a green premium. 333 00:14:32,120 --> 00:14:36,399 Lower carbon products typically have a premium, and we've seen them anywhere 334 00:14:36,400 --> 00:14:39,210 from 10% to 100% premium for these lower. 335 00:14:40,020 --> 00:14:41,620 Some make sense, but others don't. 336 00:14:41,630 --> 00:14:45,010 So I ask this question all the time: Does it make sense from a 337 00:14:45,020 --> latestEpisode.title: Australia-Fiji Defence Pact, Telstra Outage Fallout, Economic Squeeze

clip.text: In Sydney... the fallout from this week’s nationwide Telstra outage is still front and centre. The blackout hits mobile... internet... and some emergency services... disrupting transport and payments across the country. Hospitals and triple‑zero operators report delays and confusion... and businesses lose trading hours as EFTPOS goes down. Regulators now press Telstra for answers... and the government faces fresh questions on how resilient Australia’s critical infrastructure really is. For millions of Australians... it’s a reminder of how dependent daily life is on a handful of networks... and how fragile that can be. — — [serious] In Melbourne... economic anxiety ticks up as households face rising costs and patchy support. States

transcript.scoringWindow: [warm] This is NewsCard Daily for Sunday July 12, 2026 ... the biggest stories from Australia and around the world in just minutes. — — [serious] We begin in Canberra where Australia’s new defence pact with Fiji is reshaping security in our region. The two countries sign a mutual defence alliance... pledging to come to each other’s aid if either is attacked. It’s the strongest deal Fiji has ever signed with any partner... and a clear signal of Canberra’s push to counter China’s influence in the Pacific. The agreement also includes economic and security cooperation... from maritime patrols to climate and disaster resilience. For Australians... it means deeper ties with a key neighbour... and potentially more ADF presence and joint exercises across the Pacific islands. — — [serious] In Sydney... the fallout from this week’s nationwide Telstra outage is still front and centre. The blackout hits mobile... internet... and some emergency services... disrupting transport and payments across the country. Hospitals and triple‑zero operators report delays and confusion... and businesses lose trading hours as EFTPOS goes down. Regulators now press Telstra for answers... and the government faces fresh questions on how resilient Australia’s critical infrastructure really is. For millions of Australians... it’s a reminder of how dependent daily life is on a handful of networks... and how fragile that can be. — — [serious] In Melbourne... economic anxiety ticks up as households face rising costs and patchy support. States warn they’re not ready for thousands of people losing access to the NDIS under tighter eligibility rules. Health experts say many will fall back on the public hospital system... adding pressure to already stretched emergency departments. At the same time... retail closures like Lincraft’s planned shutdown of its remaining stores highlight the squeeze on bricks‑and‑mortar businesses. For families and workers... it all feeds a sense that the safety net is thinning just as living costs stay high. — — [curious] Now to our region... where a big weekend event in Melbourne shows the power of live experiences in a digital age. Thousands of players gather for Pokémon GO Fest along the Yarra... turning the city into a giant augmented‑reality playground. Tourism operators welcome the influx... with cafes... bars and hotels seeing a bump in trade. It’s a lighter story... but it speaks to how event‑driven tourism is helping cities like Melbourne recover from slower retail and office activity... and attract younger visitors back into the CBD. — — [serious] Overseas in Europe... rugby fans wake up to a bruising result for the Wallabies. Australia leads France early in their Nations Championship clash... but then concedes 30 straight points to lose 42–26. The collapse raises familiar questions about consistency and depth just years out from the next World Cup campaign. For Australian sport... it’s another sign that traditional powers like France are surging... and that Rugby Australia’s rebuild still has a long way to go. — — [urgent] Meanwhile in the United States... security and antisemitism remain in sharp focus as Australia continues to mourn the Bondi Beach attack victims. American outlets highlight Australia’s tightened New Year and public‑event security... and the global rise in extremist‑motivated violence. For Australians... it’s part of a broader conversation about how to keep crowded places safe... while protecting the open... everyday freedoms people expect at beaches... malls and festivals. Governments at home and abroad are under pressure to improve intelligence sharing... online monitoring... and community support to prevent similar attacks. — — [warm] That’s NewsCard Daily. For more top stories and quick summaries that keep you informed in minutes, download NewsCard ... available in the App Store.

structure.rationale: Clear segment-by-segment format with consistent tonal cues and a logical flow from hard news to lighter content, though the transitions between stories lack explicit signposting and the Pokémon GO segment disrupts the otherwise serious throughline.

relevance.rationale: The episode is anchored in timely, specific political and policy developments — a bilateral defence pact, critical infrastructure failure with regulatory fallout, NDIS eligibility changes, and public security policy — with only brief detours into sports and a lifestyle event.

clipability.rationale: The Telstra outage and Australia-Fiji pact segments each contain self-contained, quotable summaries that could stand alone as previews, but most segments are too compressed and context-dependent to clip cleanly without the surrounding episode.

card.tagline: Australia's new Pacific defence pact and the Telstra outage that exposed a nation's fragility
card.description: Australia signs its strongest-ever Pacific alliance with Fiji while regulators grill Telstra after a nationwide blackout hit hospitals, payments, and emergency services.
card.reason: Because this episode covers Australia's biggest defence and domestic policy stories this week

My name is Peter Pru aka Peter Prusinowski and I help busy professionals build income focused portfolios through options trading, dividends, and smart investing...without the stress or staring at charts all day. My goal? To help you take control of your financial future with strategies that actually work. Whether you’re new to options or looking to refine your approach, you’re in the right place.

Sell Options. Collect Premiums. Repeat

*Not financial advice, just sharing my thoughts*

structure.rationale: The episode has a recognizable review format with a clear throughline (overvalued IPOs and space stocks), but pacing is loose and the host frequently digresses into lists and asides without tight segmentation.

relevance.rationale: This is a financial markets and investment analysis show focused on stock valuations, IPOs, and space sector equities — not a news or politics program, despite passing references to presidential statements about nuclear and rare earth stocks.

clipability.rationale: The SpaceX valuation rant and the wall-of-analyst-buy-ratings sequence are punchy and quotable, but most moments require prior context about which stocks are being discussed to land fully.

card.tagline: SpaceX IPO called a joke — and the numbers back it up
card.description: Gary Kaltbaum tears apart SpaceX, Rocket Lab, and a wave of space stocks he says were hyped at insane valuations and are now cratering.
card.reason: Because this episode dissects one of the summer's most hyped IPOs

scored.description:
Why are so many Minnesota high school graduates unable to read or do math at grade level? Does Tim Walz actually think he's going to be the next president of the United States? And why does he keep swearing so much? 
 
These are the kinds of hard-hitting questions we get into every week on the American Experiment Podcast, where we unpack the week's biggest stories, interview Minnesota's movers and shakers, and "stop the tape" on clips of our state's most ridiculous elected officials. 
 
New episodes drop every Tuesday afternoon and are available on every major podcasting platform.

structure.rationale: The episode moves through distinct story segments with a clear conversational throughline, but transitions are informal and pacing is uneven, with tangents (the dog park drama) disrupting the policy focus before a guest interview segment begins.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers specific, timely Minnesota policy issues — SNAP penalties, a federal lawsuit, school budget negotiations, and a state paid leave program going dark — but the scope is largely state-level and the SNAP headline is the sharpest national hook.

clipability.rationale: The school board member's Facebook post about 'pissing on white corpses' and the observation that Minnesota's paid leave program has gone radio silent since April are genuinely self-contained, reaction-worthy moments, though most segments require at least minimal setup.

card.tagline: Minnesota owes $130M in SNAP penalties — and won't hand over the records
card.description: Two hosts unpack Minnesota's federal SNAP lawsuit, a billion-dollar school district budget crisis, and a state paid leave program that's mysteriously stopped reporting numbers.
card.reason: Because this episode tracks a live federal-state standoff over welfare fraud data

structure.rationale: There is a discernible throughline connecting Iran war tensions to oil prices to manufacturing decline, but pacing is uneven with tangential asides (e.g., Clinton net worth digression) that interrupt the flow.

relevance.rationale: The episode directly addresses current geopolitical tensions with Iran, oil supply disruptions, strategic petroleum reserve depletion, and long-term economic policy — all timely and substantive, though the analysis leans more editorial than breaking-news driven.

clipability.rationale: Several self-contained moments work well as standalone clips — particularly the strategic petroleum reserve warning ('next week we're in mid-July, we've reached that point') and the China shock job-loss statistics — though most require at least minimal setup.

card.tagline: Iran tensions are draining U.S. oil reserves — and the clock just ran out
card.description: Marco Gerace breaks down how the Iran conflict is choking oil supply, depleting strategic reserves, and why economists say prices won't recover soon.
card.reason: Because this episode connects the Iran war directly to your gas prices and grocery bills

scored.description: Politics, law, and culture collide as Newsweek Senior Editor-at-Large Josh Hammer charts a path forward for American conservatism and exposes the woke Left. A voice for the New Right, Hammer delivers blistering commentary and weekly interviews with today's top conservative thinkers.

structure.rationale: The episode moves through distinct topic blocks (Iran/Qatar Air Force One, immigration/Walz pardon, Alaska ballot fraud) with clear transitions, though pacing is uneven and segments bleed into each other without tight signposting.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers multiple timely, specific political stories — Iran assassination plot intelligence, Tim Walz's pardon of a foreign sex offender, and Democratic ballot-spoiling tactics in Alaska — all with direct policy and political stakes.

clipability.rationale: The Rubio audio clip on the Laotian sex offender deportation is highly self-contained and punchy, and the Qatar Air Force One/Iran assassination plot summary stands well on its own as a standalone hook.

card.tagline: Iran plots to kill Trump, Walz pardons a child molester, and ballot fraud in Alaska
card.description: Josh Hammer breaks down the WSJ's Iran assassination bombshell, Rubio's deportation of a Walz-pardoned sex offender, and a Democratic name-spoofing scheme targeting a GOP Senate seat.
card.reason: Because this episode covers three of this week's most explosive political stories

scored.feedTitle: Podmastery: podcasting insights and advice for indie creators
scored.description:
Are you a podcaster who's looking to improve and grow your podcast? You're in the right place. Together, we'll help you attain 'podmastery'. 
I'm Neal Veglio, a veteran podcaster who first started dabbling with RSS in the early 2000s.
Podcasting is such a complex medium, with so many factors that can impact your success. It's my goal to cover all these topics with you, and help you maximise the results you're getting from your efforts.
And while my shows now enjoy thousands of listeners every single week, it wasn't always easy. 
When I took a career break from radio for a few years in 2007, I had to learn how to build audience without the lift of an FM frequency. 
I learned a lot from that experience.
I now help other podcasters to achieve their goals.
Each episode, I'll be offering you some insights into what I've done and what I've helped my  podcasting agency clients to do with all our podcasts in the hope we can help YOU increase your podcast's chances of becoming more successful! 
And ensuring you can avoid the dreaded 'podfade'!
Take one step closer to Podmastery. Click follow in your favourite podcast app, and let's grow together.

latestEpisode.title: What 5,000 Podcast Listeners Just Told Us About Indie Show Growth

transcript.scoringWindow: Around two months ago, I made the argument both for and against Apple Podcasts HLS video, which meant that, of course, I then had to go and actually enable it to test it because there's a version of podcasting about podcasting that stays permanently theoretical, and I find that version slightly embarrassing. I mean, some of my peers have no problem with spouting so-called expertise around stuff that they've never actually tried or achieved, but I hold myself to a much better standard. So I did it. My other podcast, B2B Podcasting Insights, I enabled video in Captivate, and this is a real commercial show. I mean, I make that show as a lead generation tool And I wanted some actual results to present. So this is my report, and hopefully, my podcasting Padawan, you will benefit from it Welcome to Podmastery Podcasting Insights. It's the podcast for indie creators who want more from their shows. I'm Neal Veglio, the podmaster, and each episode, I'm doing my level best to help you make progress on your journey towards podmastery. Now, if you've been following the last few episodes, you know that I've been working through the case for and against this, not just for you, but also my own head, to figure out whether or not I want Podnos, my podcasting agency, to start leveraging this on behalf of clients. I need the theory of it, what it is, what it isn't. Well, this episode is gonna be different from the usual ones because this is an actual field report. I've done it. I've enabled video in Apple Podcasts for my show, B2B Podcasting Insights, and that show's a little bit like this one, but it's specifically targeting people wanting to get the most out of their B2B podcasts, their branded podcasts, a show they're doing for the benefit of their business. And that kind of podcast is in a crowded market. At last count, I noted around 50 shows that I could class as a direct competitor to my own. And I'd heard that shows with HLS video enabled were getting prioritized in the feeds within Apple Podcasts. More on that in a second. But the point is, the show is now a video podcast on Apple Podcasts via Captivate, and I've now got two weeks of data and an honest verdict on the whole thing to share with you. So let's start with the basics Let's talk about what HLS video actually is. HLS stands for HTTP Live Streaming. Three letters that sound less like a streaming protocol and more like something you might see mentioned in a scary letter from HMRC. Also, it isn't, despite what your inbox's AI summary might suggest, as a high-level strategy document that someone needs back by Friday, complete with your comments. It's actually an Apple-developed streaming standard. What makes it different from previous attempts at video in podcasting, and believe me, there have been lots of attempts over the years, as far back as the mid-2000s when the video-enabled iPod Nano was released. Anyway, what makes this tech different from the MP4 pass-through standard that Libsyn have bragged on providing for years is that this tech streams in chunks. Your video gets broken into small segments and delivered at whatever quality the listener's connection can handle right at that moment. So instead of a long delay while the podcast apps download enough of that MP4 file to begin playing it to you, this protocol means it can start playing almost immediately. And with a strong signal, you get high quality. If you hit patchy 4G through a motorway tunnel, the quality adjusts automatically with no buffering, no spinning wheel of screw you while you scream about your phone, your service provider, and the life choices that all led you to this exact tunnel just as John on your favorite podcast was starting to explain email open rate success. The previous method that I just alluded to, as in attaching a video file directly to an RSS enclosure, was technically possible. Some hosts offered it Like I say, Libsyn bragged about it. In fact, when I told Libsyn that I was leaving them to move to Captivate a few years ago, that was one of the things that they mentioned. "Oh, you won't be able to publish video anymore." Almost nobody was publishing video via Libsyn anyway because most podcast apps just stripped the video and played the audio version anyway, which is kind of like the digital equivalent of ordering a steak and being handed just the peppercorn sauce and an IU- and an IOU note where the steak should have been. HLS works because Apple Podcasts is genuinely built for it. In fact, they invented it. It's not a workaround that a bunch of Silicon Valley nerds came up with just so they could enjoy watching production companies nagging Amy Poehler into applying some makeup for recordings. And it's a clean experience. I know, that's not a word that gets pulses racing. Nobody is subscribing to a magazine called The Clean Podcast User Experience Quarterly. Three issues a year, free tote bag if you renew before March. But for anything in podcasting infrastructure, clean is pretty much the equivalent of a Michelin star. Someone follows B2B Podcasting Insights on Apple Podcast. The new episode lands. Wanna watch? There's a button. Tap it. Watch it. Driving? Just tap the button again. Listen to it from exactly the same place it was. Clean. There's no, "Head over to our YouTube channel for the full experience." There's no, "This episode contains visuals best appreciated on our website. Go to www.blah." No QR code that you're scanning one-handed while doing 60 on the A40. There's none of that. The video just sits there. Offline downloads work too, the video version specifically, and I'm mentioning that because things in podcasting that are supposed to work and usually don't has become kind of a meme in the podcasting industry, certainly over the last six or seven years whenever Apple Podcasts tends to update something Now, before you make any plans at all, your podcast host needs to support this. transcript.scoringWindow: WEBVTT 1 00:00:05.400 --> 00:00:11.080 Spirit Matters. Wonder Rome a whimsically soulful spiritual practice written 2 00:00:11.119 --> 00:00:15.519 by Jerlyn Zavada Novak and Spirit Matters. This article is 3 00:00:15.560 --> 00:00:18.519 being read by an automated voice brought to you by Everlett. 4 00:00:20.239 --> 00:00:23.120 This week, I realize wonderlust with an O is the 5 00:00:23.160 --> 00:00:27.280 driving force in my life. Whereas the more familiar wanderlust 6 00:00:27.519 --> 00:00:30.839 describes a hunger for travel and new experiences, those with 7 00:00:30.920 --> 00:00:33.520 wonderlust crave a sense of awe and wonder at the 8 00:00:33.560 --> 00:00:38.119 discovery of new things. Wonderlust can be practiced anywhere, even 9 00:00:38.200 --> 00:00:41.759 in our own backyards. By nurturing my wonder lust, I 10 00:00:41.840 --> 00:00:44.280 hold a mirror to my soul and allow my soul 11 00:00:44.359 --> 00:00:48.960 to reveal the mysterious and varied landscapes within. Wonderlust takes 12 00:00:48.960 --> 00:00:51.119 me into my inner and outer worlds with a posture 13 00:00:51.119 --> 00:00:54.399 of gentle receptivity and teaches me to notice what sparks 14 00:00:54.439 --> 00:00:57.880 my curiosity and interest. It allows me to travel into 15 00:00:57.920 --> 00:01:00.759 these worlds without an agenda or timeline, and let what 16 00:01:00.840 --> 00:01:03.200 I find speak to me about who I am or 17 00:01:03.320 --> 00:01:06.319 what I need at this moment in time. The practice 18 00:01:06.319 --> 00:01:09.319 of wonder lust softens rigidity and helps me remain open 19 00:01:09.359 --> 00:01:12.879 to changes of plans. The most basic way I appease 20 00:01:12.959 --> 00:01:15.200 my wonder lust is to go for a drive without 21 00:01:15.200 --> 00:01:18.200 a destination and allow my intuition to guide me where 22 00:01:18.200 --> 00:01:21.319 to go. It is a simple enchanting way to observe 23 00:01:21.359 --> 00:01:25.000 the four seasons as the landscapes change. This practice not 24 00:01:25.040 --> 00:01:27.760 only introduces me to sites before unseen, but it also 25 00:01:27.840 --> 00:01:30.439 has a way of soothing my inner child in ways 26 00:01:30.480 --> 00:01:34.920 beyond words. These drives often bring clarity to whatever is 27 00:01:34.920 --> 00:01:38.719 going on in my life. When I lived in Bloomington Normal, 28 00:01:38.840 --> 00:01:41.680 I practiced what I now call wonder rome at the 29 00:01:41.719 --> 00:01:44.799 mall and the bookstore. One of my favorite places to 30 00:01:44.840 --> 00:01:49.319 practice wonder rome was at Burgner's Department store. I went 31 00:01:49.359 --> 00:01:51.959 there on slow evenings and allowed myself to wander without 32 00:01:52.000 --> 00:01:56.359 looking for anything in particular. The housewares and bedding departments 33 00:01:56.599 --> 00:01:59.519 were particularly rich places for me to discover patterns and 34 00:01:59.560 --> 00:02:02.920 items that attracted me, and of course, the Christmas tree 35 00:02:02.920 --> 00:02:05.680 displays during the holidays offered a feast for my senses. 36 00:02:06.560 --> 00:02:09.680 At Barnes and Noble, I roamed the stacks, not looking 37 00:02:09.719 --> 00:02:12.479 for any particular book, but waiting for the book I 38 00:02:12.520 --> 00:02:15.479 needed to pick me. One of the most memorable times 39 00:02:15.520 --> 00:02:18.439 this happened. I walked slowly looking at the titles on 40 00:02:18.520 --> 00:02:21.800 the spines of books, when one with a soothing blue cover, 41 00:02:21.960 --> 00:02:25.439 called The Wonders of Solitude by Dale Silwok, seemed to 42 00:02:25.520 --> 00:02:28.719 jump off the shelf into my hands. At that moment 43 00:02:28.800 --> 00:02:31.680 in my life, this book was a exactly what I needed, 44 00:02:31.680 --> 00:02:33.560 and for quite a while I took it with me 45 00:02:33.599 --> 00:02:37.599 wherever I went. Salwok's collection of quotes about the healing 46 00:02:37.639 --> 00:02:40.919 properties of silence and solitude not only nourished my soul 47 00:02:41.000 --> 00:02:43.520 but gave me the courage to claim these practices as 48 00:02:43.560 --> 00:02:46.879 a countercultural necessity in a world that cringes at the 49 00:02:46.879 --> 00:02:51.479 thought of thirty seconds of uninterrupted quiet. Wonder Rome is 50 00:02:51.520 --> 00:02:55.000 more than just a spiritual practice. It describes my creative 51 00:02:55.039 --> 00:02:58.360 tendency to whimsical and playful elements, as well as an 52 00:02:58.400 --> 00:03:02.000 awareness of the beauty and oneness of creation. Much of 53 00:03:02.000 --> 00:03:05.400 what I make or design incorporates this sense of wonder, whimsy, 54 00:03:05.479 --> 00:03:08.879 and gentle beauty into it. In a world starving for 55 00:03:08.960 --> 00:03:14.280 authentic incarnational experiences, wonder Rome is perennial spiritual medicine. I 56 00:03:14.319 --> 00:03:16.159 have found that I cannot live without a sense of 57 00:03:16.159 --> 00:03:19.680 wonder lust. When I do I feel myself shriveling inside 58 00:03:19.719 --> 00:03:23.080 and I no longer recognize myself. It is foundational for 59 00:03:23.120 --> 00:03:27.080 my well being. Spirit Matters is a weekly column by 60 00:03:27.120 --> 00:03:29.879 Jeralins of Aado Novak that examines experiences common to the 61 00:03:29.960 --> 00:03:33.560 human spirit. Contact her at Jzblue three three at yahoo 62 00:03:33.599 --> 00:03:39.280 dot com.

card.tagline: One writer's case for aimless wandering as a spiritual necessity
card.description: Columnist Jerlyn Zavada Novak explores 'wonder roam,' a whimsical practice of purposeless exploration she argues is essential to creative and spiritual well-being.
card.reason: Because this episode explores a quiet, reflective approach to everyday wonder

scored.description: Russia Decoded explains how the Kremlin shapes its narrative of current domestic and international events through tightly curated state news broadcasts. Co-hosts Andy Kuchins, a senior fellow at CFTNI and adjunct professor at Johns Hopkins SAIS in Washington, and Chris Monday, a professor of economics at Dongseo University in Busan, South Korea, are veteran observers of Soviet and Russian media. Kuchins and Monday frame their discussions around the weekly Russian news round-up program Vesti Nedeli (“News of the Week”), broadcast on Russia’s Channel 2 on Sunday evenings. Viewed through the right lens, Vesti Nedeli offers a uniquely revealing perspective on how Vladimir Putin and Russian elites understand the world, and how they seek to influence it.

relevance.rationale: The episode directly engages with timely geopolitical developments — Putin's strategic options, Russia-China military alignment, a recent Chinese submarine missile test, and Russia's demographic crisis — making it sharply relevant to current news/politics listeners.

card.tagline: Does Putin have an exit — or is Russia running out of people first?

card.reason: Because this episode tackles Putin's strategic endgame as the war grinds on

structure.rationale: The show moves through distinct news stories in a roundtable format with loose but identifiable transitions, though pacing is conversational and occasionally meandering between topics.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers timely cybersecurity news including a DHS network breach claim, a SharePoint KEV addition, Pegasus spyware in the EU Parliament, and Apple's Hide My Email flaw — all current, specific, and substantive infosec stories with clear policy and government dimensions.

clipability.rationale: The Windows 11 device identifier tracking exchange ('Are you telling me that operating systems track and collect information about their users?') and the DHS press-release-over-security riff are genuinely self-contained and quotable, but most moments require surrounding context to land.

card.tagline: DHS got breached — and their response was a press release, not a patch
card.description: This week's infosec roundup covers a DHS network breach, a critical SharePoint CVE, and a Pegasus spyware twist inside the EU Parliament investigating Pegasus.
card.reason: Because this episode breaks down real government security failures happening right now

scored.description:
Free speech, culture, censorship, Big Tech, and the conversations shaping the future are all colliding in real time, and most people are only seeing a small piece of the picture. The Jeff Dornik Show brings together voices from across politics, business, health, faith, and culture to have real conversations that cut through narratives and get to the truth.

Hosted by Jeff Dornik, founder and CEO of Pickax, this show is built on one premise: the most important ideas are often the ones that are ignored, suppressed, or misunderstood. Through in-depth interviews and direct discussions, Jeff explores the forces influencing our world, from political agendas and cultural shifts to personal health, entrepreneurship, and the battle over free expression in the digital age.

This is not a scripted or controlled environment. Guests are free to speak, ideas are challenged openly, and conversations go where they need to go. Whether it is politics, business, faith, health, or culture, the goal is simple: honest dialogue without filters.

If you are looking for real conversations, diverse perspectives, and a deeper understanding of what is actually happening beneath the surface, this show is for you.

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-jeff-dornik-show--4788100/support.

latestEpisode.title: Mark Zuckerberg’s $800M Data Center Has Germ Drama | Interview on The Matt Gaetz Show

structure.rationale: The episode follows a clear interview format with a defined intro, sequential questions, and a clean close, but the conversation meanders through unsubstantiated comparisons (COVID shots, EMF) without a tight throughline.

relevance.rationale: The episode touches on a real regulatory and public health controversy involving a major tech company's infrastructure, but the discussion is speculative and loosely analytical rather than substantively political or policy-driven — it lacks engagement with legislation, regulatory action, or direct political developments.

clipability.rationale: The opening host setup on the Wyoming bacteria discovery is self-contained and usable as a standalone clip, but most of the guest's responses require context and trail off into vague speculation rather than quotable standalone moments.

card.tagline: Meta's $800M Wyoming data center may be poisoning local water systems
card.description: A rare bacteria found near Meta's Project Cosmo site in Cheyenne raises questions about data center safety, community pushback, and long-term health unknowns.
card.reason: Because this episode covers a breaking environmental controversy around Big Tech

structure.rationale: The episode moves with a clear throughline from disinformation claims to comparative US/France disclosure approaches to polling data, with identifiable segments and guest voices, though transitions occasionally meander into tangents.

relevance.rationale: The episode is substantively tied to current political developments — Trump's anticipated UFO speech, congressional hearings with David Grusch, and France's parliamentary UAP forum — making it genuinely timely news/politics-adjacent content, though the core subject matter is paranormal rather than strictly political.

clipability.rationale: Several self-contained, quotable moments land well — particularly the 'staged disclosure' framing from the political scientist and the closing argument about state control versus public right to know — usable without heavy setup.

card.tagline: Is UFO disclosure a political power grab disguised as transparency?
card.description: Analysts argue Trump's UFO speech and congressional hearings are 'staged disclosure' designed to frame aliens as a threat and expand state control — not reveal truth.
card.reason: Because this episode interrogates the political machinery behind this week's UFO headlines

scored.description:
NaturalQueen77 TV is a true crime podcast dedicated to the cases the mainstream forgot. While the biggest names in crime get documentaries and bestsellers, countless victims and unsolved mysteries slip through the cracks. This show pulls them back into the light.

Each episode dives deep into lesser-known murders, disappearances, and cold cases — examining the evidence, the suspects, and the systemic failures that left these cases gathering dust. And when a well-known case has more to the story than people think, we cover that too.

From small-town tragedies to overlooked urban cases to infamous crimes that still have unanswered questions, NaturalQueen77 TV tells the stories that deserve airtime.
New episodes drop weekly. Subscribe and follow on social media to join the conversation.

Tucker was named a person of interest, but 42 00:03:08.759 --> 00:03:13.159 without a body, without a confession, without enough to charge him, 43 00:03:13.439 --> 00:03:18.840 the case stalled. While Keyshay's case sat unresolved, Otis, Tucker's 44 00:03:18.879 --> 00:03:23.199 violence didn't stop. In twenty twenty two, in Florida, Tucker 45 00:03:23.280 --> 00:03:27.439 murdered a woman named Ashley Fowler. He was convicted and 46 00:03:27.639 --> 00:03:32.560 sentenced to life in prison. It was that conviction that 47 00:03:32.800 --> 00:03:36.479 separate act of violence that would eventually crack open what 48 00:03:36.639 --> 00:03:40.840 happened to Keisha. In twenty twenty four, nearly eight years 49 00:03:40.919 --> 00:03:46.759 after Keyshe disappeared, Richmond investigators traveled to Jacksonville, Florida, to 50 00:03:46.840 --> 00:03:50.520 sit down with Tucker again, this time with help from 51 00:03:50.520 --> 00:03:55.919 an unexpected source, Tucker's own father, who traveled with police 52 00:03:56.000 --> 00:04:00.280 and encouraged his son to finally tell the truth. Tuckerer 53 00:04:00.360 --> 00:04:04.319 admitted he was with Keishay when she died. He claimed 54 00:04:04.400 --> 00:04:07.879 she died of an overdose in his home. He admitted 55 00:04:07.960 --> 00:04:11.199 to disposing of her body, He did not confess to 56 00:04:11.280 --> 00:04:15.479 killing her. To secure that admission, Richmond police reached an 57 00:04:15.520 --> 00:04:19.000 agreement with Tucker they would not use his statements about 58 00:04:19.079 --> 00:04:23.480 Keishay's death against him in future prosecution. It was a 59 00:04:23.560 --> 00:04:29.319 trade the truth in exchange for accountability he'd likely never faced. 60 00:04:29.920 --> 00:04:33.920 On the eleventh of July twenty twenty four, Richmond Police 61 00:04:34.000 --> 00:04:38.279 Chief Rick Edwards stood in front of cameras and confirmed 62 00:04:38.319 --> 00:04:42.759 what Tony Jacobs had feared for nearly eight years. Her 63 00:04:42.879 --> 00:04:48.399 daughter was dead. Quote. It's a very sad story, Edwards said, 64 00:04:48.759 --> 00:04:51.759 and I know all of us hoped she would be found, 65 00:04:51.959 --> 00:04:56.560 but unfortunately she was not. End quote. The method Tucker 66 00:04:56.680 --> 00:04:59.879 used to dispose of Keishay's remains has made it nearly 67 00:05:00.120 --> 00:05:04.920 impossible for investigators to recover them. As of this recording, 68 00:05:04.959 --> 00:05:08.680 her body has never been found. Kishey's case has been 69 00:05:08.720 --> 00:05:14.120 classified as exceptionally closed, a designation law enforcement uses when 70 00:05:14.160 --> 00:05:17.560 they believe they know what happened but don't have enough 71 00:05:17.560 --> 00:05:22.160 admissible evidence to bring charges. Otis Tucker has never been 72 00:05:22.279 --> 00:05:26.399 charged in connection with her death. A December twenty twenty 73 00:05:26.439 --> 00:05:31.519 five investigative report revisited the case, raising hard questions about 74 00:05:31.519 --> 00:05:35.279 the strength of Tucker's confession and about how the investigation 75 00:05:35.519 --> 00:05:39.720 was handled in the years before it, those questions remain open. 76 00:05:40.199 --> 00:05:44.920 Keisha Jacobs disappeared in twenty sixteen. It took eight years 77 00:05:44.920 --> 00:05:47.439 for her family to get an answer, and the answer 78 00:05:47.480 --> 00:05:52.519 they got still isn't justice. No charges, no trial, no 79 00:05:52.639 --> 00:05:56.040 accountability for the man who admitted to disposing of her body. 80 00:05:56.480 --> 00:06:00.279 Her story sits inside a pattern we talk about allen 81 00:06:00.319 --> 00:06:04.759 on this show. Cases involving missing Black women that move slower, 82 00:06:05.240 --> 00:06:09.639 get less coverage, and too often get closed without consequence. 83 00:06:10.079 --> 00:06:15.199 Tony Jacobs is still out there, still carrying her daughter's name, 84 00:06:15.839 --> 00:06:19.800 still asking the same question. So many mothers on this 85 00:06:19.959 --> 00:06:23.839 show have asked us to ask on their behalf, who 86 00:06:23.920 --> 00:06:26.959 is going to answer for this? If you have any 87 00:06:27.000 --> 00:06:32.160 information about kish Jacob's case, please contact the Richmond Police 88 00:06:32.160 --> 00:06:36.680 Department Cold Case Unit. This has been the last known moment. 89 00:06:37.439 --> 00:06:41.720 Thank you for listening and for remembering her name. Known moment.

structure.rationale: The episode follows a tight, deliberate arc — cold open, content warning, sourcing disclosure, chronological case narrative, resolution, and closing call to action — with clear signposting throughout.

relevance.rationale: This is a true crime/missing persons episode — well-produced and socially conscious, but not a News/Politics episode; the racial disparity angle is noted but does not transform the content into political or policy coverage.

clipability.rationale: The opening hook ('Tomorrow never came. Nine years later, her mother is still waiting') and the closing indictment of systemic neglect of missing Black women both function as strong standalone moments requiring minimal context.

card.tagline: Eight years, one admission, zero charges — Keeshae Jacobs still has no justice
card.description: A Richmond woman vanished in 2016; the man who admitted to disposing of her body sits in prison for killing someone else, uncharged in her death.
card.reason: Because this case exposes how missing Black women are failed by the justice system

We cover the latest breakthroughs in large language models (LLMs), AI video editing, AI photography, AI audio tools, and creative automation. Each episode gives digital creators, content marketers, and creative professionals clear insights into how AI is transforming storytelling, production, and the creator economy. Stay informed, stay creative, and stay ahead with daily AI news made simple.

scored.description:
The Karel Show delivers bold commentary from Karel, an openly gay entertainer, author, and nationally syndicated talk show host known for saying what others won’t. With humor, heart, and zero patience for nonsense, Karel takes listeners on a fast-paced, no-filter journey through politics, culture, and modern American life—hilarious, incisive, and always honest.

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-karel-show--1368295/support.

structure.rationale: The episode meanders between personal anecdotes about bisexuality, Ukraine war coverage, LGBTQ news roundup, and art censorship without clear segmentation or deliberate transitions between topics.

relevance.rationale: The episode directly addresses current LGBTQ policy crackdowns, museum censorship under Trump administration directives, Ukraine/NATO developments, and Christian nationalism — substantive political coverage with specific timely hooks.

clipability.rationale: A few quotable lines exist — the Christian nationalist/traitor framing, the museum censorship rundown — but most moments require surrounding context or are interrupted by ad breaks and tangents.

card.tagline: Is the Trump administration quietly erasing queer art from American museums?
card.description: Karel and a Kyiv-based journalist document LGBTQ art censorship at the Smithsonian, a global rights crackdown, and Trump's shifting stance on Ukraine.
card.reason: Because this episode tracks a quiet but accelerating rollback of queer visibility in public institutions

scored.description: NBA Hall of Famer & 4x Champion, Shaquille O'Neal aka SHAQ, goes on the record from Shahookah Studios with co-host Adam Lefkoe to bring you nonstop laughter and unfiltered thoughts on sports, life, entertainment, and more. Along the way, they will be joined by a host of VIP guests for a high energy show you won't soon forget.New episodes every Thursday on YouTube at THE BIG PODCAST WITH SHAQ. This is a Playmaker & Big Podcast Network Exclusive.

As Thomas Jefferson wrote, when in the course 12 00:00:50.840 --> 00:00:54.200 of human events, it becomes necessary for one people to 13 00:00:54.280 --> 00:00:57.560 dissolve the political bands which have connected them with one another, 14 00:00:58.240 --> 00:01:01.200 and to assume the powers of the earth, the separate 15 00:01:01.240 --> 00:01:04.480 and equal station to which the laws of nature and 16 00:01:04.560 --> 00:01:07.879 of Nature's God entitle them. A decent respect to the 17 00:01:07.920 --> 00:01:12.359 opinions of mankind requires that they should declare the causes 18 00:01:12.400 --> 00:01:16.879 which impel them to the separation. We hold these truths 19 00:01:16.879 --> 00:01:20.040 to be self evident, that all men are created equal, 20 00:01:20.359 --> 00:01:24.439 that they are endowed by their creator with certain unalienable rights, 21 00:01:24.840 --> 00:01:28.920 that among these are life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness. 22 00:01:29.680 --> 00:01:33.319 That to secure these rights, governments are instituted among men, 23 00:01:34.040 --> 00:01:37.359 deriving their just powers from the consent of the government. 24 00:01:37.840 --> 00:01:41.640 That whenever any form of government becomes destructive of these ends, 25 00:01:42.040 --> 00:01:44.480 it is the right of the people to alter or 26 00:01:44.519 --> 00:01:48.920 abolish it, and to institute new government, laying its foundation 27 00:01:49.120 --> 00:01:52.920 on such principles, and organizing the powers in such form 28 00:01:53.439 --> 00:01:56.439 as to them shall seem most likely to affect their 29 00:01:56.480 --> 00:02:02.040 safety and happiness. Prudence indeed will dictate that governments along 30 00:02:03.599 --> 00:02:07.879 prudence indeed will dictate that governments long established should not 31 00:02:07.920 --> 00:02:11.960 be changed for light and transient causes. And accordingly, all 32 00:02:11.960 --> 00:02:15.800 experience has shown that mankind are more disposed to suffer 33 00:02:16.159 --> 00:02:20.319 while evils are sufferable, than to right themselves by abolishing 34 00:02:20.360 --> 00:02:23.159 the forms to which they are accustomed, but which a 35 00:02:23.159 --> 00:02:27.319 long train of abuses and usurpations, pursuing invariably the same 36 00:02:27.400 --> 00:02:31.599 object evinces a design to reduce them under absolute despotism. 37 00:02:32.000 --> 00:02:34.840 It is their right, it is their duty, to throw 38 00:02:34.840 --> 00:02:38.000 off such government, and to provide new guards for their 39 00:02:38.039 --> 00:02:42.319 future security. Such has been the patient's sufferance of these colonies, 40 00:02:42.639 --> 00:02:46.039 and such as now the necessity which constrains them to 41 00:02:46.120 --> 00:02:49.919 alter their former systems of government. The history of the 42 00:02:49.919 --> 00:02:52.759 present King of Great Britain is a history of repeated 43 00:02:52.840 --> 00:02:58.039 injuries and usurpations, all having indirect object the establishment of 44 00:02:58.080 --> 00:03:02.599 an absolute tyranny over these states. To prove lists let 45 00:03:02.719 --> 00:03:06.960 facts be submitted to a candid world. He has refused 46 00:03:07.000 --> 00:03:10.199 to assent to laws the most wholesome and necessary for 47 00:03:10.280 --> 00:03:13.719 the public good. He has forbidden his governors to pass 48 00:03:13.800 --> 00:03:17.759 laws of immediate and pressing importance unless suspended in their 49 00:03:17.759 --> 00:03:22.280 operation till his assent should be obtained, and when so suspended, 50 00:03:22.560 --> 00:03:25.719 he has utterly neglected to attend to them. He has 51 00:03:25.759 --> 00:03:28.879 refused to pass other laws for the accommodation of large 52 00:03:29.000 --> 00:03:32.800 districts of people unless these people would relinquish the right 53 00:03:32.840 --> 00:03:37.919 of representation in the legislature, a right inestimable to them 54 00:03:38.000 --> 00:03:42.759 and formidable to tyrants only. He has called together legislative 55 00:03:42.759 --> 00:03:47.280 bodies at places unusual, uncomfortable, and distant from the depository 56 00:03:47.319 --> 00:03:50.599 of their public records, for the sole purpose of fatiguing 57 00:03:50.639 --> 00:03:55.879 them into compliance with his measures. He has dissolved representative 58 00:03:56.039 --> 00:04:00.719 houses repeatedly for opposing, with manly firmness his invasions on 59 00:04:00.759 --> 00:04:04.400 the rights of the people. He has refused, for a 60 00:04:04.479 --> 00:04:08.800 long time, after such dissolutions, to cause others to be elected, 61 00:04:09.120 --> 00:04:14.080 whereby the legislative powers incapable of annihilation have returned to 62 00:04:14.120 --> 00:04:18.319 the people at large for their exercise. The state remaining 63 00:04:18.360 --> 00:04:21.560 in the meantime exposed to all the dangers of invasion 64 00:04:21.839 --> 00:04:26.600 from without and convulsions within, he has endeavored to prevent 65 00:04:26.680 --> 00:04:30.319 the population of these states. For that purpose, obstructing the 66 00:04:30.360 --> 00:04:33.959 laws for naturalization of foreigners, refusing to past others to 67 00:04:34.079 --> 00:04:37.399 encourage their migrations hither, and raising the conditions of new 68 00:04:37.439 --> 00:04:42.199 appropriations of lands. He has obstructed the administration of justice 69 00:04:42.240 --> 00:04:46.319 by refusing his assent to laws for establishing judiciary powers. 70 00:04:47.360 --> 00:04:50.279 He has made judges dependent on his will alone for 71 00:04:50.360 --> 00:04:53.160 the tenure of their offices and the amount and payment 72 00:04:53.199 --> 00:04:57.199 of their salaries. He has erected a multitude of new offices, 73 00:04:57.399 --> 00:05:00.360 and said, hither swarms of officers to harass us out 74 00:05:00.439 --> 00:05:05.120 people and eat out their substance. He has kept among us, 75 00:05:05.319 --> 00:05:08.439 in times of peace, standing armies without the consent of 76 00:05:08.519 --> 00:05:12.959 our legislators. He has effected to render the military independent 77 00:05:13.680 --> 00:05:17.519 of and superior to the civil power. He has combined 78 00:05:17.560 --> 00:05:20.399 with others to subject us to a jurisdiction foreign to 79 00:05:20.519 --> 00:05:24.639 our constitution and unacknowledged by our laws, giving his assents 80 00:05:24.720 --> 00:05:29.360 to their acts of pretended legislation. For quartering large bodies 81 00:05:29.360 --> 00:05:31.959 of armed troops among us, for protecting them by a 82 00:05:32.040 --> 00:05:35.839 mock trial from punishment for any murders which they should 83 00:05:35.839 --> 00:05:39.519 commit on the inhabitants of the States, for cutting off 84 00:05:39.560 --> 00:05:42.319 our trade with all parts of the world, for imposing 85 00:05:42.439 --> 00:05:45.519 taxes on us without our consent, for depriving us, in 86 00:05:45.600 --> 00:05:49.000 many cases of the benefits of trial by jury, for 87 00:05:49.160 --> 00:05:53.040 transporting us beyond seas to be tried for pretended offenses, 88 00:05:53.399 --> 00:05:56.639 For abolishing the system of English laws and the neighboring province, 89 00:05:56.879 --> 00:06:01.160 establishing therein an arbitrary government, and in larging its boundaries, 90 00:06:01.240 --> 00:06:04.519 so as to render it at once an example and 91 00:06:04.680 --> 00:06:09.079 fit instrument, for introducing the same absolute rule into these colonies. 92 00:06:10.120 --> 00:06:13.319 For taking away our charters, abolishing our most valuable laws 93 00:06:13.680 --> 00:06:19.319 and altering fundamentally the forms of our government, For suspending 94 00:06:19.360 --> 00:06:23.560 our own legislatures and declaring themselves invested with power to legislate. 95 00:06:23.680 --> 00:06:28.079 For all of us in cases whatsoever. He has abdicated 96 00:06:28.160 --> 00:06:31.399 government here by declaring us out of his protection and 97 00:06:31.519 --> 00:06:34.800 waging war against us. He has plundered our sees, ravaged 98 00:06:34.839 --> 00:06:38.240 our coasts, burnt our towns, and destroyed the lives of 99 00:06:38.279 --> 00:06:42.000 our people. He, at this time is transporting large armies 100 00:06:42.839 --> 00:06:46.920 of foreign mercenaries to complete the works of death, desolation, 101 00:06:47.079 --> 00:06:51.680 and tyranny already begun, with circumstances of cruelty and perfidy 102 00:06:52.120 --> 00:06:56.920 scarcely paralleled in the most barbarous ages, and totally unworthy 103 00:06:57.319 --> 00:07:00.319 the head of a civilized nation. He has trained our 104 00:07:00.360 --> 00:07:03.639 fellow citizens, taken captive on the high seas, to bear 105 00:07:03.800 --> 00:07:06.800 arms against their country, to become the executioners of their 106 00:07:06.800 --> 00:07:10.120 friends and brethren, or to fall themselves by their hands. 107 00:07:10.600 --> 00:07:14.560 He has excited domestic insurrections among us, and has endeavored 108 00:07:14.600 --> 00:07:17.439 to bring on the inhabitants of our frontiers the merciless 109 00:07:17.560 --> 00:07:22.319 Indian savages, whose known rule of warfare is an undistinguished 110 00:07:22.360 --> 00:07:27.480 destruction of all ages, sexes, and conditions. In every stage 111 00:07:27.519 --> 00:07:30.839 of these impressions, we have petitioned for redress in the 112 00:07:30.879 --> 00:07:35.360 most humble terms. Our repeated petitions have been answered only 113 00:07:35.720 --> 00:07:39.240 by repeated injury. A prince whose character is thus marked 114 00:07:39.319 --> 00:07:42.720 by every act which may define its tyrant, is unfit 115 00:07:42.920 --> 00:07:46.480 to be the ruler of a free people. Nor have 116 00:07:46.639 --> 00:07:49.959 we been wanting an attendance toward British brethren. We have 117 00:07:50.120 --> 00:07:52.879 warned them from time to time of attempts by their 118 00:07:52.959 --> 00:07:57.560 legislator a legislature, to extend an unwarranted jurisdiction over us. 119 00:07:58.120 --> 00:08:00.600 We have reminded them of the circumstances of our emigration 120 00:08:00.720 --> 00:08:03.839 and settlement here. We have appealed to their native justice 121 00:08:03.920 --> 00:08:07.399 and magnanimity, and we have conjured them, by the ties 122 00:08:07.439 --> 00:08:11.720 of our common kindred, to disavow their usurpations, which would 123 00:08:11.759 --> 00:08:15.680 inevitably interrupt our connections and correspondence. They too, have been 124 00:08:15.759 --> 00:08:19.279 deaf to the voice of justice and cons ingenuity. We 125 00:08:19.560 --> 00:08:24.319 must therefore acquiesce in the necessity which denounces our separation, 126 00:08:24.879 --> 00:08:27.439 and hold them as we hold the rest in mankind, 127 00:08:28.000 --> 00:08:32.519 enemies in war and in peace. Friends. We therefore, the 128 00:08:32.600 --> 00:08:36.919 Representatives of the United States of America in General, Congress Assembled, 129 00:08:37.279 --> 00:08:40.039 appealing to the Supreme Judge of the World for the 130 00:08:40.159 --> 00:08:43.759 rectitude of our intentions, due in the name and by 131 00:08:43.840 --> 00:08:47.120 the authority of the good people of these colonies solemnly 132 00:08:47.320 --> 00:08:51.679 publish and declare that these United Colonies are, and of 133 00:08:51.879 --> 00:08:55.720 right ought to be free and independent states. That they 134 00:08:55.759 --> 00:08:58.600 are absolved from all allegiance to the British Crown, and 135 00:08:58.720 --> 00:09:01.879 that they all political connection between them and the State 136 00:09:01.960 --> 00:09:05.159 of Great Britain is and ought to be totally dissolved. 137 00:09:05.759 --> 00:09:08.919 And that as free and independent states, they have full 138 00:09:09.000 --> 00:09:14.759 power to levy war, conclude peace, contract alliances, establish commerce, 139 00:09:15.159 --> 00:09:17.960 and to do all other acts and things which independent 140 00:09:18.000 --> 00:09:20.720 states may have right to do. And for the support 141 00:09:20.799 --> 00:09:24.039 of this declaration, with a firm alliance on the protection 142 00:09:24.120 --> 00:09:29.000 of Divine providence, we mutually pledge to each other our lives, 143 00:09:29.279 --> 00:09:35.519 our fortune, and our sacred And that is the Declaration 144 00:09:35.639 --> 00:09:40.200 of Independence, as written by Thomas Jefferson July fourth, seventeen 145 00:09:40.360 --> 00:09:43.240 seventy six, read here in New York City for the 146 00:09:43.279 --> 00:09:46.919 first time two hundred and fifty years ago on July nineteen, 147 00:09:47.000
