transcript.scoringWindow: in Mathematics from Western Kentucky University and an Education Specialist degree in Educational Leadership from Arkansas State University. Maury County Passes Budget (MSM) The Maury County Commission approved a $307.7 million budget for the coming fiscal year, with more than half those funds going to county schools. The school district has been directly endowed with $170 million, and Maury County Public Schools will receive another $11.3 million for school food service, $1.78 million of the capital-projects appropriations, and $23.5 million of the $32.46 million the county has allocated to debt service. County Commissioner Scott Sumners, chair of the county Budget Committee, explained in a social media post most of the schools’ funding comes from state Investment in Student Achievement (TISA) education grants. He noted 75 percent of county sales tax goes to schools, as well as half of the sales taxes from each of the three cities in Maury County. He expects schools will receive $31 million in sales taxes, compared to the county’s projected $1.6 million. The General Fund will funnel its diverse revenues into $59 million worth of expenses, including emergency response, infrastructure, the courts, law enforcement, administration and record-keeping. The Highway Fund will pay out $13.2 million, with an additional $1.3 million for highway capital projects. Solid Waste received $6.1 million. The county adopted a new certified, revenue-neutral property tax rate of $1.369 per $100 of assessed value, a 28-percent drop from the old rate of $1.91 per $100. Of those 137 property tax cents, 55 will go to the School Fund, 40 to the General Fund, 12.7 to debt service and 8 tax cents to capital projects. The budget is projected to run at a deficit of $14.5 million over property tax revenue. Between budgeted revenue of $54 million and expenditures of $59 million, the General Fund’s reserves will fall by $4.86 million; other funds’ deficits will bring the county-side deficit to $6.18 million. The School Fund’s deficit is projected at $8.34 million. At the meeting where the budget was passed, County Commissioner Scott Sumners, chair of the Budget Committee, congratulated the county for bringing its side of the deficit down from $10 million. “I expect that we will continue to overperform in our revenues and then not spend all of our expenditures,” Sumner told Main Street Maury, predicting another boom year for sales tax that will again exceed the county’s conservative revenue projections. “That is typical for our budgets[, but] I still want to get to where we have a true balanced budget.” The county also divided just over $2 million among local nonprofits. Allocations included $1.8 million for the volunteer fire and rescue department; $70,000 for the Senior Citizens organization; $35,000 to the Spring Hill Library; $20,000 for the Boys & Girls Club; $17,510 for the Tennessee Rehab Center; $10,000 each for the Center of Hope and The Well Outreach; $9,000 for Keep Maury Beautiful; $8,000 for A Kid’s Place; $5,000 each for Harvest Share food bank, CASA, Students in Crisis and the Family Center; and $3,000 for the Mediation Center. State Defers CPWS Loan (MSM) The Tennessee Local Development Authority (TLDA) voted June 22 to defer a $75 million state loan application from Columbia Power & Water Systems, delaying a decision on funding for a $205 million water treatment plant that CPWS says must be built to avoid mandatory water restrictions in the next few years. The plant would be capable of treating 12 million gallons per day (MGD) and is part of CPWS’s larger project, which is currently predicted to cost $540 million, to draw and treat sufficient water for its current and future customers. CPWS routinely draws more than 16 million of the 20 MGD its permit allows from the Duck River, and last August the utility used 93 percent of its permitted amount. Matt Wheeler, vice president at CPWS, warned that every month of delay adds $2 million to the cost of the overall project, and that utility is on track to having to restrict consumer water use by the summer of either 2028 or 2029. “We’ve got risk on the table, right now there’s a whole host of risks to our existing water intake,” said Wheeler. “We’ve got to start now to meet our next potential peak demand in summer 2029. If we don’t get started in the next month or so, we will miss that summer of 2029 and [risk drought or limiting usage] another year.” Columbia Mayor Chaz Molder also advocated for the state funding, noting that terms negotiated by CPWS and its consultants are on track to save the city $50 million in interest compared with market bond rates. “This is truly a matter of life and death, having… clear, clean drinking water,” said Molder. “It is our belief that the Duck River Partnership will come back with the recommendation for this project, because it is the only project that is anywhere beyond a feasibility study… [and] it’s taken 15 years to get to this point.” Vena Jones, the Tennessee Department of Environment and Conservation’s program manager for loans, told the TLDA board the state Drinking Water Fund currently has $163 million available for projects of this kind. She said TDEC has worked on and now endorses the planning, financial modeling and necessity of CPWS’s project. “We don’t know of another system in the state that is operating at such a level of demand… They have very, very little room for anything else. They could be in a position… to restrain folks from using water if they don’t get that plant,” Jones said. “I’m sure the governor’s report will support that expansion… [but] we expect that the plant capacity expansion has to occur regardless.” Opposition to the loan Opponents of the CPWS project submitted a letter to the TLDA board which they received a few hours before their meeting. The letter was written by Jason Gilliam, a board member of the Maury County Water System (MCWS), and its signatories included several Maury County commissioners; three members of the MCWS board; both Republican executive committee members for State Senate District 28; and state legislators Joey Hensley, Scott Cepicky, Kip Capley, Todd Warner, Clay Doggett and Chris Todd. Gilliam, a prominent local advocate for rebuilding a dam on the Duck River in east Columbia, argued that CPWS’s intake project reflects a pattern of flawed planning. “This CPWS project reveals a pattern of incomplete planning, shifting numbers, unacquired easements, potential legal challenges, negative environmental impacts, and a failure to adequately consider far cheaper and more collaborative regional alternatives,” Gilliam wrote. Gilliam estimated that even under the favorable loan terms CPWS promotes, the total investment is an unreasonable cost for only 12 MGD of capacity, and he predicted that the 30-year loan term might even outlast the adequacy of the water supply. “Maury County Water System, Spring Hill Water System and Mt. Pleasant Water System will experience the greatest impact since they all currently purchase water from CPWS,” Gilliam wrote. “This gives CPWS the distinct opportunity to control the water treatment and distribution, as well as growth throughout the county, putting every other utility at their mercy… Rate-payers, including those served by MCWS, Spring Hill and Mt. Pleasant, would bear the burden without meaningful input or representation.” At the TLDA meeting, Maury County Mayor Sheila Butt said she would prefer to see the state create a regional utility commission, which would impose equal rates on city and county customers and pursue projects cooperatively. MCWS’s water rates are much higher than those of CPWS customers, which its leaders have attributed in the past to their large service area, much smaller customer base, and the fact that they have to purchase all their water. County leaders including Gilliam and Butt have complained that CPWS’s project will further raise their own rates. “This is not just a Maury County problem. This is a statewide problem, and I hope you will see it as that, because eventually we’ve all got to quit competing and work together for all of the ratepayers,” Butt said. She also argued that it would be premature to approve the loan before the governor’s Duck River Watershed Planning Partnership (DRWPP) publishes its findings. Gilliam also complained of what he called TDEC’s “inexplicable bias” toward CPWS’s project and against other water supply prospects. April Grippo, director of TDEC’s Division of Water Resources, has spoken publicly in favor of the CPWS intake and against rebuilding the Columbia Dam. TLDA board deliberations The TLDA board, divided over the competing arguments, voted to take a month to hear each side’s case in full. While Secretary of State Tre Hargett said the board would benefit from a draft of the governor’s report, members agreed they could not wait for its September publication date to decide whether to lend Columbia $75 million. “We have a stark disagreement between the city and the county, which is unfortunate… [The impact of a large utility project is] not what this board is accustomed to mitigating,” said State Comptroller Jason Mumpower. “[And] I’ve had folks say to me… that this is a fight about control of the development of Maury County… [because] whoever controls the water source, controls the long-term development.” “It’s never been about control, it’s been about making sure that we have adequate water capacity… in the future,” Molder replied. “A value-added partnership [with Maury County Water]… is the way that it has always been, and they get it at very good rates, as do our other customers.” At Mumpower’s prompting, Wheeler and Jones predicted the useful lifespans of each element of the project. The water treatment plant, Wheeler said, should last 50-100 years depending on maintenance and expansion. The 12 MGD capacity it provides should sustain the city about 35 years, based on CPWS’s growth projections. After that, Wheeler said, the Duck River Watershed Planning Partnership (DRWPP) has talked about building upon CPWS’s work. In its November 2025 recommendations, the Partnership expressed interest in studying a pipeline that would take up where CPWS’s new intake ends, traveling about 35 additional miles to the Tennessee River, making that the new long-term water supply for the Duck River Watershed. Anticipating the state may take a line to the Tennessee River, CPWS has left room to expand on the campus of the planned water treatment plant, and sought easements to Williamsport twice as wide as what they need for their single pipe, so that the state can drop a second line alongside it. “This project doesn’t get ‘replaced’ by some project that is on review with the governor’s partnership, some future water source from another region,” Wheeler said. “It actually dovetails well with it. This project is a linchpin project for a future water source that would augment what we’re doing in Columbia and the county today.” “I think in order to have a long-term solution, you have to have a short-term solution in place,” Molder said.” Butt noted the DRWPP, on which she serves, is also examining other routes to the Tennessee River beyond the one that builds upon CPWS’s pipeline. Gallatin Mayor Paige Brown, a TLDA board member, said, “There’s nothing that has been said that could potentially take [the water treatment plant] off the table. It’s going to cost what it’s going to cost.” “The state… has a huge interest in this that overarches those of any county or city that’s involved,” said State Treasurer David Lillard. “A month or less than a month of delay is called for to understand these issues… because this is a huge [issue for perpetuity], as each of you have argued very eloquently and ably on both sides.” “The future of utilities in Tennessee is regionalization,” Mumpower said. Mayor Brown also pointed out while the local “water wars” described by Mayor Butt are indeed happening — Lillard corroborated from his experience as a Shelby County commissioner — and she herself supports regionalizing utility service. She noted, “There’s no way to force anyone to participate in a regionalization project without some deficiency

structure.rationale: The episode moves through distinct news stories with clear segmentation and attributed quotes, though the transcript excerpt reads more like a compiled news brief than a tightly paced broadcast with deliberate signposting.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers specific, timely local government decisions — a county budget vote and a contested $75M state loan deferral with named officials, dollar figures, and active political conflict — making it substantively on-topic for a news listener in Southern Middle Tennessee.

clipability.rationale: Several quotable moments exist — Mumpower's 'whoever controls the water source, controls the long-term development' and Molder's rebuttal are strong — but most require local context about CPWS, MCWS, and the Duck River dispute to land for a general listener.

card.tagline: Water wars, a $307M budget, and who controls Maury County's future
card.description: Maury County passes a revenue-neutral budget while a $75M state loan fight over a $540M water plant exposes a city-versus-county power struggle over regional development.

scored.description:
Welcome to the New Era Woman Podcast—
where purpose meets leadership and faith in real life.

This is a space for women who are healing, building, and becoming aligned. 

Each episode offers real conversations, biblical wisdom, and practical strategies to help you strengthen your inner world, hear God with confidence, and walk in peace, love, and emotional steadiness.

Whether you’re new to faith, returning to God, or seeking deeper discipleship, you belong here. 

Visit www.NewEraWoman.org and follow @newerawomanpodcast on IG to stay connected.

Follow us on Instagram at @newerawomanpodcast

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-new-era-woman-network--4771294/support.

structure.rationale: The show follows a recognizable morning radio format with distinct segments (news, employment law interview, business with Vicky Price), but transitions are abrupt and pacing is driven by clock breaks rather than editorial throughline.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers multiple timely political and economic stories — New Zealand's India FTA controversy, UK government transition, Middle East ceasefire collapse and oil markets — making it substantively news-driven, though the breadth dilutes depth on any single topic.

clipability.rationale: A few quotable moments exist — notably the John Bolton framing on peace deal futility and the AI-vs-coding curriculum observation — but most require surrounding context from the broadcast to land properly.

card.tagline: NZ's India trade deal has landmines — and the opposition is warning exporters
card.description: Ryan Bridge unpacks contradictions in New Zealand's India FTA, youth workforce readiness concerns, and the UK's political transition with economist Vicky Price.
card.reason: Because this episode covers a breaking trade deal controversy with direct export implications

scored.description:
Join Todd Herman on The Todd Herman Show offering unfiltered commentary on today’s critical issues. A former Seattle radio host and frequent Rush Limbaugh fill-in, Todd delivers sharp Christian conservative perspectives on politics and more. With a background at Microsoft and as a startup CEO, he blends real-world experience with fearless analysis. Subscribe for daily episodes, exclusive blogs, and show notes via Substack. Perfect for listeners seeking truth in a complex world.


For more information, visit radioamerica.com/todd-herman-show/

At Real America’s Voice, we cover a wide array of topics—politics, culture, and current events—through the lens of American values and freedom. We pride ourselves on delivering honest content that challenges mainstream narratives. Our passionate hosts are experts and commentators who offer unique insights on the issues that matter most to you. Whether you start your day with American Sunrise, follow breaking news in the War Room with Steve Bannon, gain political insights from Charlie Kirk, explore cultural commentary with Jack Posobiec, or tune-in for the latest with investigative reporter John Solomon and Just The News, you’ll find all your favorite programs on RAV right here on the RAV podcast, including coverage of our live events featured on the network. Join the Real America’s Voice community to stay informed, engaged, and inspired. Subscribe now and never miss an episode!

structure.rationale: The episode moves through identifiable segments (Portland protest footage, Idaho border interview, upcoming musical tribute) with clear transitions, but pacing is uneven and the segment breaks feel loosely stitched rather than deliberately structured.

relevance.rationale: The episode directly covers current immigration enforcement, state-level legislation, and northern border security — timely political subject matter with specific bills and legislative actors named, though the framing is advocacy rather than news reporting.

clipability.rationale: The Idaho legislator's 'seventy percent of the dairy industry would fail an E-Verify audit' claim and the 'modern day slavery' exchange are self-contained and punchy enough to stand alone as preview clips without much setup.

card.tagline: Idaho lawmaker says dairy industry is running on illegal labor — and nobody will stop it
card.description: A state rep at the Idaho-Canada border reveals how agriculture lobbies blocked E-Verify bills even in a Republican supermajority legislature, while the northern border goes unwatched.
card.reason: Because this episode covers active state-level immigration battles most outlets ignore

scored.feedTitle: The Daily Mojo with Brad Staggs
scored.description:
The Daily Mojo with Brad Staggs and Producer Ron Phillips is your first look each weekday, at the news, politics, faith, and absurdity that America is fast becoming. Brad and Producer Ron Phillips lens it all through a filter of truth, cynicism, and laughter (that’s getting tougher by the minute).

Our core value – We The People are at the heart of these United States and WE need to wrest our power back from the DC swamp dwellers. If you believe in freedom, personal responsibility, and individual rights, you may have found soulmates.

The Daily Mojo is life in the Conservatarian lane each weekday from 8-10a ET at TheDailyMojo.com, and wherever you get your favorite podcasts! Follow @RealBradStaggs @RealRonPhillips

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-daily-mojo-with-brad-staggs--3085897/support.

structure.rationale: The episode meanders through data centers, grid security, Chinese EVs, and carbon lifestyle without clear segmentation or deliberate pacing, held together loosely by interview format rather than intentional structure.

relevance.rationale: The episode addresses timely geopolitical and policy concerns — Chinese infrastructure infiltration, AI data center security, EV trade competition, and Texas energy legislation — with specific current examples, though the conversation is discursive rather than rigorously reported.

clipability.rationale: The grid-spoofing explanation — where a guest describes how Chinese entities could fake power output to collapse the Texas grid — is a genuinely self-contained, alarming moment that would work as a standalone preview without much setup.

card.tagline: Could China collapse the U.S. power grid through AI data centers?
card.description: An energy policy expert explains how foreign actors could spoof grid data to trigger blackouts, and why Texas moved to block Chinese infrastructure access near military bases.
card.reason: Because this episode breaks down a real and underreported national security threat

structure.rationale: The episode follows a clear recap-then-playoffs structure with identifiable segments, a break, and consistent throughline, though pacing is conversational and loose in places.

relevance.rationale: This episode is entirely about Blood Bowl, a fantasy tabletop sports game, with no connection to news or political events.

clipability.rationale: Most moments require familiarity with the league, teams, and players to land — the Leroy Jenkins running gag is the closest thing to a standalone moment but still needs context.

scored.description:
Join the journey with David Knight, a true veteran in the realm of news and analysis, and become part of a community that values historical context, critical think, and spiritual insight in navigating the challenges of our era.  
LIVE every weekday morning at 9am Eastern where you'll find an honest, fresh perspective, not partisan cheerleading.  WIth a background as an electrical engineer, David is focused on the interplay of technology & liberty from a Christian perspective.

David has had decades of experience in the trenches of politics as an activist, organizer, candidate, reporter, analyst, and news anchor









Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-real-david-knight-show--5282736/support.

Weren't generating any revenue. 147 00:06:50,200 --> 00:06:54,599 The new plant would add capacity for 250,000 more smart sofas 148 00:06:54,599 --> 00:06:58,280 and 10,000 more smart beds a year, most of it aimet North American 149 00:06:58,280 --> 00:06:58,840 buyers. 150 00:06:58,840 --> 00:07:02,440 And the Cambodia expansion is partly about reducing tariff exposure. 151 00:07:02,760 --> 00:07:05,600 Interestingly, none of the official filings even mention the 152 00:07:05,600 --> 00:07:06,680 Palisur acquisition. 153 00:07:07,160 --> 00:07:08,680 Here is why this matters for you. 154 00:07:08,920 --> 00:07:12,760 Moto Motion spent years as the invisible engine, the Smart Motion 155 00:07:12,760 --> 00:07:15,400 motor tucked inside upholstery from well known brands. 156 00:07:15,640 --> 00:07:18,040 Now it's pushing its own name at U.S. retail. 157 00:07:18,200 --> 00:07:22,040 A capacity expansion this size plus the move into Cambodia tells 158 00:07:22,040 --> 00:07:24,720 you this is a supplier building to serve North America at 159 00:07:24,720 --> 00:07:26,600 scale while hedging its tariff risk. 160 00:07:26,760 --> 00:07:30,120 If you carry motion or smart upholstery, the competitive picture 161 00:07:30,120 --> 00:07:32,640 is shifting because the company that used to just supply 162 00:07:32,640 --> 00:07:35,160 the engine is becoming a branded competitor. 163 00:07:35,480 --> 00:07:38,280 Let me close the main stories with a compliance warning that could 164 00:07:38,280 --> 00:07:39,240 save you real money. 165 00:07:39,640 --> 00:07:43,000 U.S. customs recently seized a shipment of kitchen cabinets coming 166 00:07:43,000 --> 00:07:43,640 from China. 167 00:07:43,800 --> 00:07:46,800 The container's paperwork claimed it held just 10 cabinets 168 00:07:46,800 --> 00:07:50,040 worth $500 plus a bunch of unrelated home decor. 169 00:07:50,120 --> 00:07:53,080 But when inspectors opened it, that decor wasn't there. 170 00:07:53,320 --> 00:07:57,760 Instead, they found 781 cartons of cabinets all subject to 171 00:07:57,760 --> 00:08:01,960 anti dumping duties with an estimated value north of $120,000. 172 00:08:02,570 --> 00:08:05,370 Now, Chinese made wooden bedroom furniture has faced anti 173 00:08:05,370 --> 00:08:08,610 dumping duties since early 2005, so this kind of enforcement 174 00:08:08,610 --> 00:08:09,210 isn't new. 175 00:08:09,450 --> 00:08:13,810 Back in 2015, two companies paid $15 million to settle claims 176 00:08:13,810 --> 00:08:16,010 that they mislabeled furniture to dodge duties. 177 00:08:16,250 --> 00:08:19,890 Here's the part every importer needs to understand if you are the 178 00:08:19,890 --> 00:08:23,130 importer of record, whether you buy direct or through a wholesaler, 179 00:08:23,130 --> 00:08:26,450 a factory's mislabeling becomes your bill, plus potential 180 00:08:26,450 --> 00:08:29,860 penalties and fines with duties and tariffs here for the foreseeable 181 00:08:29,860 --> 00:08:30,180 future. 182 00:08:30,340 --> 00:08:32,820 This is a live risk, not a hypothetical.

scored.description:
Join the journey with David Knight, a true veteran in the realm of news and analysis, and become part of a community that values historical context, critical think, and spiritual insight in navigating the challenges of our era.  
LIVE every weekday morning at 9am Eastern where you'll find an honest, fresh perspective, not partisan cheerleading.  WIth a background as an electrical engineer, David is focused on the interplay of technology & liberty from a Christian perspective.

David has had decades of experience in the trenches of politics as an activist, organizer, candidate, reporter, analyst, and news anchor









Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-david-knight-show--2653468/support.

Some council members have 0:02:35.399 --> 0:02:37.679 suggested the council censure Lorenz for what could be a 0:02:37.679 --> 0:02:39.698 violation of the town ethics code. 0:02:40.740 --> 0:02:43.029 A McLean County real estate leader says the passage of 0:02:43.029 --> 0:02:45.949 the new federal housing bill addresses one of the biggest 0:02:45.949 --> 0:02:49.699 issues every community faces a limited supply of housing units. 0:02:50.029 --> 0:02:53.869 Kamel Tedrick is incoming president of the Mid-Illinois Realtors Association. 0:02:54.029 --> 0:02:58.470 First-time homebuyers still want the opportunity to build wealth through 0:02:58.470 --> 0:02:59.169 home ownership. 0:02:59.649 --> 0:03:04.100 But many are really facing the challenges with affordability, the 0:03:04.100 --> 0:03:07.440 higher interest rates that are going on, and the limited inventory. 0:03:07.699 --> 0:03:10.740 The act is the first major federal housing legislation in 0:03:10.740 --> 0:03:11.740 30 years. 0:03:13.020 --> 0:03:15.070 The mayor of Normal says the town needs to pay 0:03:15.070 --> 0:03:18.679 attention to demographic trends that show an aging population. There's 0:03:18.679 --> 0:03:21.990 more people staying in the community after retirement. Chris Koos 0:03:21.990 --> 0:03:23.750 says that's one of the challenges laid out in the 0:03:23.750 --> 0:03:27.649 town's new 3-year strategic plan. We're paying more attention to 0:03:27.649 --> 0:03:29.169 the ADA issues. 0:03:29.538 --> 0:03:33.959 In the built environment, I think encouraging the type of 0:03:34.100 --> 0:03:38.100 housing in the community that would address that population's needs. 0:03:38.380 --> 0:03:39.979 I think those are the things we can do as 0:03:39.979 --> 0:03:43.740 a community. Demographics show normal is becoming less white with 0:03:43.740 --> 0:03:46.220 growth in other ethnic and demographic groups. 0:03:47.300 --> 0:03:49.940 The city of Bloomington will get some help in revitalizing 0:03:49.940 --> 0:03:53.020 several West Side neighborhoods from the public service arm of 0:03:53.020 --> 0:03:57.259 a realtor's organization. Ed Neaves with the Mid-Illinois Realtors Association 0:03:57.259 --> 0:03:59.899 says the counselors of Real Estate Consulting Corps did a 0:03:59.899 --> 0:04:03.080 similar plan in Peoria to revive a blighted area formerly 0:04:03.080 --> 0:04:05.160 occupied by two school buildings. 0:04:05.369 --> 0:04:07.520 Their plan that they came up with 5 years ago 0:04:07.940 --> 0:04:12.779 was to put in senior facilities, health nursing facilities. 0:04:13.539 --> 0:04:15.169 Homes habitat by humanity. 0:04:15.559 --> 0:04:19.019 Neves says the city of Bloomington has identified about 145 0:04:19.019 --> 0:04:21.420 houses on the west side that need to be rehabbed 0:04:21.420 --> 0:04:24.140 and 24 that are candidates to tear down, which will 0:04:24.140 --> 0:04:26.779 be the target of the study. You can find more 0:04:26.779 --> 0:04:30.380 on all these stories at WGLT.org. I'm Ben Howell.

structure.rationale: The episode circles the same points repeatedly without clear segmentation, transitions, or a defined close — it reads as a stream-of-consciousness reaction rather than a structured argument.

relevance.rationale: The episode is directly pegged to a specific political news event — the reported death of Senator Lindsey Graham — and uses it to argue a concrete policy position on congressional age restrictions, keeping it substantively on-topic for News/Politics.

clipability.rationale: A few punchy standalone lines exist (e.g., 'they die in power instead of retiring') but most moments require the surrounding context of Graham's death to land, and the rambling delivery undercuts clean extraction.

card.tagline: Senator Graham is dead — is it finally time for age limits in Congress?
card.description: DarkSkies reacts to Lindsey Graham's sudden death and makes the case that aging politicians dying in office proves America needs mandatory retirement age legislation.
card.reason: Because this episode reacts to a major breaking political death story

structure.rationale: The conversation meanders between sports diplomacy, political analysis, and candidate-specific commentary without clear segmentation, and the hosts frequently interrupt or redirect without deliberate pacing.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers timely 2026 midterm election dynamics, NRCC strategy, Democratic Party internal tensions, and specific races in New York, Colorado, and Maine, giving it clear news relevance, though the sports diplomacy opening dilutes focus.

clipability.rationale: LaHood's three-point Republican majority argument (candidate quality, resources, Democratic radicalism) is the most self-contained passage, but most exchanges require surrounding context to land and the crosstalk reduces standalone usability.

card.tagline: GOP's House majority strategist breaks down why Democrats are their own worst enemy
card.description: Illinois Congressman Darin LaHood, a key NRCC leader, argues socialist primary wins in New York and Maine are handing Republicans a 2026 midterm lifeline.
card.reason: Because this episode maps the Republican strategy for holding the House in November

scored.description: After many years on national television as a broadcaster for The Shopping Channel, Norm Murray has turned his attention to the people, events, and news from his own backyard – Peel Region. A Mississauga resident, Norm travels to various happenings across the GTA to find out – what IS “The Norm” in Peel? On-air, Norm invites conversation, as he engages in thorough discussion through live interviews. Notable interviews include: performers of The Living Arts Centre, Paramount Fine Foods Centre, and The International Centre – everyday people who entertain the community, specifically those who shy away from the “norm,” subsequently establishing a new “norm.” As he likes to say, “we’ve got a lot to talk about.”

latestEpisode.title: The Norm - July 7, 2026 - Ward 1 Update: Community, Growth, and the Future of Mississauga with Councillor Stephen Dasko

clipability.rationale: Most exchanges require context about local figures and prior conversations to land, though Dasko's declaration that he's already campaigning regardless of competition offers one moderately self-contained moment.

structure.rationale: The episode meanders between multiple unrelated stories with frequent personal digressions (watching TV with dad, cooking dinner) and no clear segmentation or throughline connecting the topics.

relevance.rationale: The episode touches on real news items — a Mississippi Medicaid fraud indictment, a foiled mass shooting, and a criminal case update — but coverage is anecdotal, reactive, and loosely structured around personal commentary rather than substantive political or policy analysis.

clipability.rationale: Most moments require surrounding context to land, and the host's frequent filler phrases and self-interruptions make it difficult to isolate a clean, standalone 30-45 second excerpt.

card.tagline: July 4th shootings, Mississippi Medicaid fraud, and a pastor's jailhouse suicide
card.description: A solo host connects a foiled Nevada mass shooting, a $12M Mississippi Medicaid fraud indictment, and a youth pastor murder case that ended before trial.
card.reason: Because this episode covers recent crime and fraud stories making headlines

scored.feedTitle: The Truth Behind The Truth with Jonathan Prell
scored.description: Everything and anything that is even remotely controversial. Politics,social issues, sports and everything in between. We are after the real truth.

latestEpisode.title: Autopsy - The Death of Local Terrestrial Radio

clip.text: But you know what? 00:23:26.640 --> 00:23:30.079 All of that was preventable. 00:23:31.359 --> 00:23:46.480 And again, I wanted to talk about this since I left in 2015 because since I've lived where I live now, and people find out that I was in radio, one of the most common questions that I get even today is what's what's going on with radio? 00:23:46.720 --> 00:23:48.400 Does it exist anymore? 00:23:48.640 --> 00:23:50.240 What about these morning shows? 00:23:50.799 --> 00:23:52.559 What's going on with radio? 00:23:52.720 --> 00:23:56.400 It is nowhere near what it used to be, and it wasn't. 00:23:56.640 --> 00:24:03.599 So the thing that killed, not

transcript.scoringWindow: --> 00:22:08.000 It is almost impossible to win because again, you're competing with the rest of the nation. 00:22:08.079 --> 00:22:10.559 You're not competing with people in your community. 00:22:10.720 --> 00:22:14.319 You're competing with the entire competing with the entire nation. 00:22:14.559 --> 00:22:17.839 And that is just absolutely not going to work. 00:22:19.279 --> 00:22:31.839 And even the industry, you know, figured out that it was going to have to deal with massive layoffs, cost-cutting measures. 00:22:32.160 --> 00:22:36.319 Let's get back to the point that I brought up just a minute ago, the cost of a radio station. 00:22:36.480 --> 00:22:47.359 Back then, when all of this started, there was a simple formula that would determine the sale price of any one particular radio station in a particular market. 00:22:47.759 --> 00:22:52.720 And that formula was generally 12 times cash flow. 00:22:52.960 --> 00:23:13.279 So whatever your cash flow is in your radio station or your cluster, the sale price is going to be 12 times that amount, which means if you're the purchaser, you're the company buying these radio stations, that means you are so far in the red before you even close because you had to write a humongous check. 00:23:13.440 --> 00:23:15.519 So now you are going to have to look at things. 00:23:15.680 --> 00:23:18.000 You are going to have to look at cost-cutting measures. 00:23:18.160 --> 00:23:21.039 You're going to have to look at mass layoffs. 00:23:21.200 --> 00:23:24.000 You're going to have to increase commercial loads. 00:23:24.079 --> 00:23:25.440 And that's one of the biggest things. 00:23:25.599 --> 00:23:26.480 But you know what? 00:23:26.640 --> 00:23:30.079 All of that was preventable. 00:23:31.359 --> 00:23:46.480 And again, I wanted to talk about this since I left in 2015 because since I've lived where I live now, and people find out that I was in radio, one of the most common questions that I get even today is what's what's going on with radio? 00:23:46.720 --> 00:23:48.400 Does it exist anymore? 00:23:48.640 --> 00:23:50.240 What about these morning shows? 00:23:50.799 --> 00:23:52.559 What's going on with radio? 00:23:52.720 --> 00:23:56.400 It is nowhere near what it used to be, and it wasn't. 00:23:56.640 --> 00:24:03.599 So the thing that killed, not that local terrestrial radio is necessarily dead, but it's clearly on life support. 00:24:03.839 --> 00:24:05.839 And you know whose fault that is? 00:24:06.799 --> 00:24:08.880 The companies that did this. 00:24:09.680 --> 00:24:14.640 They did not think this through when they made these purchases. 00:24:14.799 --> 00:24:22.240 They started thinking on grander scales, on scales that would benefit the shareholders. 00:24:22.640 --> 00:24:24.240 That's what it was all about. 00:24:24.480 --> 00:24:37.680 They prioritized profit over anything and everything else, including people, community, neighborhoods, localism. 00:24:38.000 --> 00:24:44.240 They prioritized making sure that the shareholders got their share. 00:24:44.400 --> 00:24:50.880 And they were going to do whatever they had to do to keep that stock price up at a particular level. 00:24:51.039 --> 00:25:00.400 Now, one of the most common complaints that listeners had about terrestrial local radio up to that point were commercial loads. 00:25:00.640 --> 00:25:14.000 And believe me, as frustrated as you might have been at some time, or even maybe are now when you're watching even a TV station or you're listening to a radio station in the car, the amount of commercials is maddening. 00:25:14.079 --> 00:25:15.519 I was in radio for 30 years. 00:25:15.680 --> 00:25:18.559 I can't stand to listen to terrestrial radio anymore. 00:25:18.720 --> 00:25:22.000 They take a break and it just seems like these breaks go on forever. 00:25:22.160 --> 00:25:27.759 And it used to be radio commercials were made up of 60s and 30s. 00:25:27.920 --> 00:25:29.119 That's really what it was. 00:25:29.279 --> 00:25:45.839 And in most cases, outside of your what they call morning drive time, which is 6 to 10 a.m., outside of that, you're going to run probably three commercial breaks an hour and roughly four minutes worth of commercial units in that break. 00:25:46.000 --> 00:25:50.000 So when you're biding that up between 60s and 30s, it's really not going to be that much. 00:25:50.079 --> 00:25:53.200 And study after study has shown that listeners understand that. 00:25:53.359 --> 00:25:58.319 They know that's how radio and TV stations pay their bills, and they're willing to give you a little bit of commercial time. 00:25:58.559 --> 00:26:09.519 But what the industry decided they needed to do, because all the program directors basically revolted and said, playing 25 to 28 minutes an hour of commercials is ridiculous. 00:26:09.680 --> 00:26:14.240 We're going to lose our entire audience and our ratings are going to tank. 00:26:14.480 --> 00:26:15.440 And they did. 00:26:15.839 --> 00:26:20.720 And that's why the programmers all got together and said, look, we got to figure this out. 00:26:20.960 --> 00:26:27.599 Our thing was three breaks an hour with a maximum length of four and a half minutes. 00:26:27.759 --> 00:26:31.759 And that's when these companies decided, you know what we can do? 00:26:32.720 --> 00:26:39.440 We can start making 10-second commercials and 15-second commercials. 00:26:40.319 --> 00:26:43.839 Insert those into the commercial breaks. 00:26:44.160 --> 00:26:49.200 We'll be able to charge almost as much for those as we're charging for a regular spot. 00:26:51.440 --> 00:26:54.480 And we're going to get the deal of a lifetime. 00:26:55.119 --> 00:26:56.480 Didn't work out that way. 00:26:57.680 --> 00:26:59.039 Didn't work out that way. 00:26:59.599 --> 00:27:12.400 Because what you had is you had commercial breaks that consisted of maybe four or five elements, maybe four or five commercials, and then the break would end with some kind of a station promo or something like that. 00:27:12.559 --> 00:27:15.920 Well, now what you have, you still have the four-minute break. 00:27:16.160 --> 00:27:26.480 But instead of having four or five units to have to sit through, you've got 10, 12, 14, 16 units. 00:27:26.960 --> 00:27:43.599 And when you're listening to this in your car, at home, on your iPod, whatever, when you're listening to this, you're thinking, my God, this is the longest commercial break ever in the history of broadcasting. 00:27:43.839 --> 00:27:46.079 When time-wise, it isn't. 00:27:46.240 --> 00:27:48.640 It's the same amount that it's always been. 00:27:48.880 --> 00:27:52.319 But element-wise, yeah, it is crazy. 00:27:52.480 --> 00:27:56.400 And they also did studies when programmers started complaining about this. 00:27:56.559 --> 00:28:16.240 And the studies showed, and us programmers were begging our general managers and our VPs of programming to look at this information because what they found out was that listeners couldn't care less if a commercial is 10 seconds, 15 seconds, 30 seconds, or 60 seconds. 00:28:16.400 --> 00:28:18.880 All they know is that's a commercial. 00:28:19.039 --> 00:28:23.359 They view 10-second commercials the same way they view a 60. 00:28:23.519 --> 00:28:31.920 And when you've increased the number of elements from 5 to 13, not going to happen. 00:28:32.559 --> 00:28:34.319 Absolutely not going to happen. 00:28:34.559 --> 00:28:37.279 And the other thing, the advertiser. 00:28:37.839 --> 00:28:42.559 The advertiser gets completely hosed in that situation. 00:28:42.799 --> 00:28:54.319 Again, when you have a format where you've got three four and a half minute breaks per hour, which, and again, the number of elements might be four or five. 00:28:54.559 --> 00:29:06.079 Well, the problem came when the 10s and the 15s came into play because how would you like to be an advertiser and you're kind of skittish about spending money on advertising? 00:29:06.160 --> 00:29:12.240 You've never done it before, but you've got a radio salesperson in there all the time who's trying to convince you to do this, and you're thinking, I don't know. 00:29:12.319 --> 00:29:13.759 I don't know if it's going to work for me. 00:29:13.920 --> 00:29:15.759 Um, I'm going to have to see results right away. 00:29:15.839 --> 00:29:18.240 And then they have to be too, well, you know, it's going to take time. 00:29:18.319 --> 00:29:21.920 You know, it's going to have to get a little bit repetitive. 00:29:22.079 --> 00:29:24.559 You can't expect results right off the bat. 00:29:24.880 --> 00:29:32.559 But what they have done is they have sold these advertisers on the idea of 10s and 15s, which overall are cheaper. 00:29:32.799 --> 00:29:46.079 But what if you are an advertiser and you're so skittish about spending money on doing commercials and you are the second to last element in a 13-element break? 00:29:46.240 --> 00:29:51.119 Do you think that advertiser is getting his or her money's worth for their investment? 00:29:51.519 --> 00:29:52.799 Absolutely not. 00:29:53.279 --> 00:29:54.400 Absolutely not. 00:29:54.640 --> 00:30:00.799 And that's when they started doing things like sponsoring, you know, the studio that you broadcast in. 00:30:01.920 --> 00:30:09.839 Hey, you know, we're coming to you live this morning from the ABC Mechanic Garage and Wrecker Service. 00:30:10.319 --> 00:30:13.759 You know, for example, they don't, no, that's our station. 00:30:14.000 --> 00:30:15.359 We own the station. 00:30:15.599 --> 00:30:27.039 That advertiser does not own the station, but the perception could be that that advertiser has a stake in the radio station, which means they have a say in what's going on. 00:30:27.119 --> 00:30:30.240 And that was very, very frustrating for programmers to deal with. 00:30:30.960 --> 00:30:46.400 But the main thing that programmers dealt with was this corporate conglomerate landscape that for the local person was impossible to navigate. 00:30:46.559 --> 00:30:54.559 Because more often than not, you weren't in on the initial discussions for a new idea or something like this. 00:30:54.720 --> 00:30:59.200 Basically, they would get together in their corporate offices, they would decide this is what we're going to do. 00:30:59.279 --> 00:31:01.519 And then they send a memo to all the radio stations. 00:31:01.680 --> 00:31:04.720 Here's what you're going to do, here's how this promotion is going to work. 00:31:04.880 --> 00:31:08.880 And you're sitting there in your market thinking, man, I don't know if that's going to work. 00:31:09.200 --> 00:31:11.839 We just don't have that kind of a makeup here. 00:31:12.000 --> 00:31:16.319 The people who live here, this is not really who they are. 00:31:16.559 --> 00:31:18.319 But, you know, it doesn't matter. 00:31:18.559 --> 00:31:22.559 And so the local guys were ignored. 00:31:23.519 --> 00:31:27.599 And uh again, radio did this to itself. 00:31:27.920 --> 00:31:36.559 But when all the new technologies came in, and this ties into what I was talking about earlier: people asking questions about what happened to radio. 00:31:36.799 --> 00:31:40.240 Uh, did music streaming kill it? 00:31:40.480 --> 00:31:41.920 Did card.tagline: A 30-year radio veteran explains exactly how the industry destroyed itself

structure.rationale: The episode follows a clear interview format with identifiable segment breaks, a logical progression from legal basics to enforcement gaps to reform proposals, though pacing is somewhat uneven and transitions rely on repetitive show-ID bumpers.

relevance.rationale: The episode addresses specific recent Austrian animal cruelty cases (Husky Buba, cat Schmotzer) and critiques current law, making it timely and substantive, though it is a niche legal/policy topic rather than top-tier political news.

clipability.rationale: The lawyer's explanation of why judges literally cannot sentence first-time offenders to prison under current law is a self-contained, quotable moment, and the international comparison segment also stands alone reasonably well.

card.tagline: Why no one in Austria goes to prison for torturing animals — explained
card.description: A criminal defense lawyer breaks down the legal loopholes that make Austria's animal cruelty law effectively toothless, citing two shocking recent cases.

scored.feedTitle: Off the Record:  Portland Real Estate Insider
scored.author: Joe and Makayla
scored.description: Welcome to Off the Record: Portland Real Estate Insider, your ultimate source for all things real estate in Portland Metro! Join Joe and Makayla, as they take you on a thrilling journey behind the scenes of the bustling Portland Metro real estate market. From jaw-dropping success stories to nail-biting challenges, we spill the beans on it all, without holding back. Get ready to gain insider knowledge, uncover hidden gems, and discover the secrets of the trade that no one else will tell you. With our  expert insights, you'll be hooked from the very first episode. So, whether you're a seasoned investor, a first-time homebuyer, or just curious about the world of real estate, be sure to tune in to Off the Record: Portland Real Estate Insider. Don't miss out - hit that subscribe button now and join us on this thrilling ride!

scored.author: Taylor Johnatakis
scored.description:
Peasants Perspective: A Voice from the Edge of Freedom  


Join Taylor Johnatakis, a self-proclaimed “peasant” turned podcaster, on an unfiltered journey through family, faith, and the fight for American ideals. From the depths of DC Jail—where he recorded during a 14-month sentence tied to January 6—to his triumphant return home after a Trump clemency in 2025, Taylor delivers raw, heartfelt commentary for the common man. Expect a mix of gritty storytelling, reflections on liberty lost and reclaimed, and timeless lessons drawn from his life as a septic designer, father, and reluctant rebel. Whether he’s reading Dr. Seuss to his kids or dissecting the state of the republic, Peasants Perspective is a bold, unpolished call to stay grounded amidst chaos. Subscribe for a front-row seat to a story that’s as real as it gets—no filter, no apologies.

structure.rationale: The tertulia format produces a recognizable roundtable throughline with clear thematic progression from SEPI corruption to coalition dynamics to left-wing political entrapment, but transitions are conversational and meandering rather than deliberately signposted.

relevance.rationale: The episode directly addresses live Spanish political scandals — SEPI corruption with 24 implicated officials, Marlaska backing the Guardia Civil director, and potential implication of María Jesús Montero — all framed around the Sánchez government's survival prospects heading into anticipated early elections.

clipability.rationale: Several standalone-worthy moments exist — particularly the 'corrupción estructural' framing, the SEPI-as-economic-arm argument, and the left-wing 'harakiri' metaphor — though most require at least minimal context about Spanish political actors to land fully.

card.tagline: 24 imputados en un día: ¿es la SEPI el mayor escándalo de Sánchez?
card.description: Tertulia analiza cómo la macrocausa de la SEPI, con posible imputación de Montero, revela corrupción estructural y deja a los socios del gobierno atrapados sin salida.
card.reason: Because this episode breaks down Spain's biggest active corruption investigation in real time

latestEpisode.title: The Ghost of Ben Franklin: "Did you keep our republic?" [God, Law, and Liberty]

clip.text: political conservatives and Christians of all stripes, often think of our nation and states as a democracy. The US Supreme Court in 2016 even said states can be, quote, laboratories of democracy. So what are these protests telling us? These protests and associated fears for the future of democracy mean too many people no longer believe in any independency of sovereignty. They have begun to believe that sovereignty rests in the individual. That leads to thinking that the state sovereignty is formed out of an aggregation of our individual sovereignty. The state's sovereignty is no longer deduced from a pre-existing sovereignty outside of ourselves, namely the sovereignty of God, and

transcript.scoringWindow: believe many today think Franklin's statement refers only to a particular form of government administration. For example, in Federalist No. 10, published in 1787, James Madison contrasted a republic with what he called a periodmocracy, a form of government administered directly by the people. More on that in a moment. But Madison did correctly define a republic as, quote, a government in which the scheme of representation takes place. But the question for the Christian is, what is being represented by that scheme? Now in this regard, consider the question Franklin actually answered, quote, well, doctor, what have we got? A republic or a monarchy? End quote. The question was drawing a distinction between a republic and a monarchy, not a republic and a democracy. McGee-on-Groen van Prinstruer's book, Unbelief and Revolution, I highly recommend it, helped me understand better the crucial difference between a republic and what Madison was talking about. And appreciating the difference will help us understand if our republic has been preserved and give us some insight into some things that I think must change. So what is a republic? Well, to appreciate the question Franklin was asked and his answer, let's begin by considering the first of four points Van Prinstruer made in his book, in his outline of, quote, the basic principles underlying the constitutions, end quote, that existed in several European nations prior to the various revolutions in 1848. And here was his first preset, or basic principle, quote, the essence of the state, its sovereignty, he wrote, was defined as independence of power and authority, end quote. And notice he's speaking of the state and of a state having an essential nature, not about its form of administration. An unbelievable Van Prinstruer's statement would likely frighten many today. I can hear them say, what an authority and state or government independent of our votes, independent of the mutual consent of the governed as it says in the Declaration of Independence, why how horrifying. I've also seen professing Christians frightened by such a thought as Van Prinstruers. With respect to them, though, to me it's an indication that they have an underdeveloped view of the glory, wisdom, knowledge, and goodness of God that's revealed in the face of Christ. But developing their understanding is not my present concern. I'm simply drawing a contrast between how a nation, or we might equally say, a state, was understood to have been constituted before and after 1848. That's what Van Prinstruer meant by Constitution. Now after 1848, a Constitution began to be looked upon as a collection of positivistic statements declared in writing, by men, instead of a description of the principles and applications of them, worked out over time that already constituted a particular society as a state or nation. For example, Magna Carta was not a statement of the philosophic abstract thoughts of various English noblemen on their philosophies and theories of government. It was a reduction to writing of what they already understood their nation's Constitution to be. Or perhaps think of Van Prinstruer's independency as analogous to the Ten Commandments. It was just a transcription of what had always constituted the law of a righteous person in God's sight. And I hope those two examples demonstrate the way in which any written Constitution was once understood. Now having defined the essence or true nature of a state, Van Prinstruer next explains the various forms of government, for a state and the history of their development. He says, quote, they were known to have evolved in time from patriarchal authority through power and dominion based on military conquest. Next, the expansion of land holdings or people's recognition of justice and wisdom to, finally, either monarchies or republics. Now asked him monarchies vis-à-vis republics in the question asked Franklin, Van Prinstruer wrote, quote, in the former case referring to the monarchy, a king is the center and soul of government. In the latter, which he called a republic, right, association resulted in a commonwealth in which the sovereignty belongs to the community. Now notice that Van Prinstruer equated a republic, mentioned in the first quote, with a commonwealth, mentioned in the second quote. But Van Prinstruer added that the administration of a republic or commonwealth is entrusted to an elected magistrate, instead of resting in one person, the monarch or king. You see in a monarchy one person has both the sovereignty that constitutes the state and the administration of the government and the republic, the two are split. So Franklin was not being asked if we had a republic because we elected people to represent us to administer the state. He was being asked about the essential nature of the state itself. In sovereignty, in our nation, our state, our national state, vest in a monarchy or republic, meaning the community as a whole. So in our nation state, the magistrate of elected administration is divided between the executive, legislative, and judicial branches. And moreover, the atom of sovereignty, the Supreme Court once called it, is further split between the federal government and various other states with their own sovereignty and forms of elected administration. This should have kept us from confusing our elected form of administering our state, our national republic, with the older understanding that in a monarchy, both the sovereignty that forms the state and its administration are resident in one person, a king. Now let's apply this to the no-king rallies. Failure to understand the distinction between a republic and a monarchy led some to think of President Trump as a king. And he may look and sound to them like a monarch or a king, but until our constitution, our written constitution, and those of the state governments are overthrown, there can be no king. And it's not just because the president is elected under our present written constitution. But notice that these protesters see king Trump as a threat to democracy, not a republic. And many others, including political conservatives and Christians of all stripes, often think of our nation and states as a democracy. The US Supreme Court in 2016 even said states can be, quote, laboratories of democracy. So what are these protests telling us? These protests and associated fears for the future of democracy mean too many people no longer believe in any independency of sovereignty. They have begun to believe that sovereignty rests in the individual. That leads to thinking that the state sovereignty is formed out of an aggregation of our individual sovereignty. The state's sovereignty is no longer deduced from a pre-existing sovereignty outside of ourselves, namely the sovereignty of God, and of his Christ, his king of king, as we discussed last week. So in some it would seem that people would rather live under the fear of man and what he might do than the fear of God. But that's because they don't know the God revealed in the person of Christ. Now mention the term democratic republic, what about it? Well, I believe that same spirit of democracy explains why even political conservatives, and even many Christians use the phrase democratic republic, I've used it, because adding democratic seem to be more acceptable to the modern democratic ear than just republic. But I've come to believe that using that term comes across to others as an unacknowledged concession to the present belief that authority and power are really resident in the individual. By it, I think many believe we are agreeing with them in principle, that we have a democracy. And by our consent, we have democratically allowed our individual sovereignty to be represented by executive and legislative branches of civil government, with a judicial branch to settle disputes. So do we still have a republic? I personally think the kind of thinking I've just described as foreign to the kind of republic ban printer was speaking of. For me, that kind of thinking is a sign that, generally speaking, we have lost the kind of republic he spoke of, and ban printerer described. We seem to have fallen into a democracy, or at least the democratic view of authority and power that matters in spoke of. And why? Well, as ban printerer said, it's because of a revolution of unbelief in God. It's taken place in our nation. So where do we go from here? I don't believe this revolution that we've experienced will be reformed or reversed by legislation. Revival will be required, no matter what some Christian political podcasters of blade have said. However, that's not to say law and politics have no place in that process. But I think their role must change. On this, when printerer wrote, quote, the consequences of revolution ideas cannot be combated with any success unless one places himself outside their influence. End quote. In other words, doing law and policy advocacy work according to the revolutionary ideas of unbelief now entrenched throughout every branch of federal and state government just won't be successful. Working that way means our thinking has conformed to that of the world. Well, have we done that? Well, I believe so. I believe we do that when our leading advocates bow the need of science on every legal or policy question pertaining to human anthropology. The human meaning is male and female. That to me is employing the revolution's principles for what constitutes true knowledge about what it means to be human. I think too, not to insist on proper definitions when words like state, democracy, sex, gender, or thrown about is to allow the revolutionary principles now behind those words to go unchallenged and our acceptance of them assumed. Those are just two practical suggestions for how to begin account or revolution. But I believe there's more required. At bottom I believe that law and the concept of sovereignty that necessarily underlies it must be reconciled back to Jesus Christ. He is the only sovereign who is outside the influence of the revolutionary's ideas. And God the Father has armed him in God the Holy Spirit with a sovereign strength to combat their influence and, quote, reconcile all things unto himself. In, quote, Colossians 1.20. In other words, generic talk of a creator and referring to natural law will not do any better combating the revolutionary ideas now embedded in our society than they did in holding them back over the last 200 years. Taking from the words of Kuiper, I believe speaking only in terms of a generic creator and natural law will prove again to be a cowardly ravellant for Christians to hide behind. Jesus Christ is really the assailed breastwork. And God has given this ministry of reconciliation to every Christian. This ministry must take place in our courtrooms and legislative chambers, but I believe it must start in the church with its elected ministers and those in the pews talking with their neighbors. It's a donning task and many may not want to take it up. But there is good news for those who do. And this is what I believe. However long this work takes, and however far this sovereign Christ has been purposed by the triumphant God to carry it. Its success rests on the fact that God the Father, quote, has put all things in subjection under his feet. And though we do not yet see all things put under him, Hebrews 2.8, we see Jesus, Hebrews 2.9. May God grant that my generation, and I'm 68 years of age, would begin to walk by faith in Christ toward this end and bring the next two generations along with us. Thank you for listening.

structure.rationale: The episode follows a discernible throughline from Franklin's quote through Van Prinsterer's framework to contemporary application, with a clear closing call to action, though pacing is dense and lecture-like throughout.

relevance.rationale: The episode engages political concepts like sovereignty, republic vs. democracy, and the 'no-king' protests against Trump, but the primary frame is theological and historical-philosophical rather than timely news coverage.

clipability.rationale: Most moments require the surrounding theological and historical scaffolding to land, though the passage connecting 'no-king rallies' to a misunderstanding of republic vs. monarchy offers a marginally standalone hook.

card.tagline: Did America lose its republic long before Trump ever showed up?
card.description: A theologically-grounded argument that the 'no-king' protests reveal America has already traded its republic for a democracy of individual sovereignty, and only Christ-centered revival can reverse it.
card.reason: Because this episode reframes the Trump-as-king debate through a historical and theological lens

From the opening bell to the final round, our boxing shows are tailored to the fight fan of today and even, yesterday.

Host T.J. Rives and insider Dan Rafael, who is the premier boxing media personality in the U.S., come your way with a Friday preview and a Monday recap of everything in the boxing ring, every weekend.

Plus, you'll hear from the biggest named fighters present and past in one on one interviews. And, the guys LOVE to talk nostalgia of the great fighters like Muhammad Ail, Sugar Ray Leonard, Mike Tyson, Oscar De La Hoya, Manny Pacquaio and more.

If you are even a casual boxing fan, you will want to be with us on the "Big Fight Weekend Boxing Podcast Feed."

scored.feedTitle: Chicks on The Right - Conservative Politics & Culture Commentary
scored.author: Chicks On The Right - Conservative Commentary
scored.description:
Chicks on the Right is your daily dose of unapologetic common sense, humor, and sanity. Each weekday, Mock and Daisy break down politics, culture, and everyday absurdities with quick wit and relatable honesty. Whether they’re calling out hypocrisy or laughing through the madness, the Chicks offer conservative women (and plenty of men) a place to unwind, connect, and stay informed.

For more information, visit:
https://www.radioamerica.com/chicks-on-the-right/
https://heliosmedia.us/chicks-on-the-right/

structure.rationale: The conversation has a loose interview throughline — book pitch, then conspiracy theory question, then brainwashing mechanics — but pacing is uneven with frequent tangents and banter that interrupt the analytical thread.

relevance.rationale: The episode engages substantively with political psychology, propaganda mechanics, and contemporary ideological movements like BLM and progressive identity politics, giving it clear News/Politics relevance, though it's partly a book promotion interview rather than hard news coverage.

clipability.rationale: The Chinese Communist thought-reform/forced confession passage and the land acknowledgement parallel are genuinely self-contained, quotable moments that would work as standalone previews without much setup.

card.tagline: Ex-CIA analyst Buck Sexton explains how brainwashing actually works on Americans
card.description: Buck Sexton draws direct lines from Soviet thought-reform and Maoist confession rituals to contemporary progressive identity politics and post-COVID conspiracy thinking.
card.reason: Because this episode breaks down the mechanics behind today's most divisive political movements

scored.author: Amanda Sturgill
scored.description: Unspun is critical thinking about the news. Hear real examples, past and present, of newsmakers attempting to mislead you and understand how they manipulate the truth. Learn how to avoid being swayed by fake news and misinformation. Get Unspun, because you deserve the truth.

latestEpisode.title: Should We Trust AI Fact Checkers? | UnSpun Journal Club

structure.rationale: The episode follows a clear journal-club format with an identifiable intro, framing of the research paper, and layered discussion of concepts like 'human in the loop' and 'knowledge decay,' though pacing is somewhat lecture-like and meandering in places.

relevance.rationale: The episode directly engages with a timely and substantive issue — AI's role in fact-checking and information integrity — with clear relevance to how news and political information is verified and consumed today.

clipability.rationale: Several standalone moments work well — particularly the 'someone can throw off lies a mile a minute and it takes hours to check those' line and the Ford Motor AI recall anecdote — but most insights require the surrounding setup to land fully.

card.tagline: Can AI fact-checkers actually be trusted — or are they making things worse?
card.description: Unspun breaks down new academic research on whether communication managers trust AI fact-checking tools, and why 'human in the loop' may be failing in practice.
card.reason: Because AI's role in shaping what you believe is the story right now

scored.author: K-BUZZ
scored.description: Stay up to date with the latest headlines from South Korea’s vibrant entertainment world. From K-pop comebacks and idol updates to Korean drama highlights and industry news, K-BUZZ delivers concise daily updates designed for on-the-go listening. But 14 00:00:54.159 --> 00:00:56.920 there are some under the radar players who could become 15 00:00:56.960 --> 00:01:01.520 contributors in twenty twenty six. Shaw Local counting down the 16 00:01:01.560 --> 00:01:04.840 top five under the radar Bears this week. These could 17 00:01:04.879 --> 00:01:07.040 be Bears who are waiting for an opportunity to take 18 00:01:07.079 --> 00:01:10.359 on a bigger role, rookies looking to make an impact, 19 00:01:10.519 --> 00:01:13.439 or simply a player Bears fans aren't talking enough about. 20 00:01:14.280 --> 00:01:16.560 Here's a look at number one on the list. Number 21 00:01:16.599 --> 00:01:21.519 one Jonah Jackson position right guard experience seventh season looking back. 22 00:01:21.840 --> 00:01:25.359 Bears general manager Ryan Poles took somewhat of a gamble 23 00:01:25.400 --> 00:01:28.200 when he traded for Jonah Jackson last offseason as part 24 00:01:28.239 --> 00:01:32.439 of his offensive line revamp. Poles, with some insight from Johnson, 25 00:01:32.840 --> 00:01:35.400 bet that Jackson could look like his former Pro Bowl 26 00:01:35.439 --> 00:01:38.319 self instead of the player that struggled to stay healthy 27 00:01:38.319 --> 00:01:41.480 in twenty twenty four. Poles bet paid off in a 28 00:01:41.480 --> 00:01:46.519 big way last season. Jackson helped the Bears offensive line 29 00:01:46.560 --> 00:01:49.319 transform into one of the best in the NFL. The 30 00:01:49.400 --> 00:01:51.640 unit paved the way for Chicago to have the third 31 00:01:51.719 --> 00:01:54.319 best running game in the league last year. He also 32 00:01:54.319 --> 00:01:57.439 did a good job of protecting Williams, allowing only three 33 00:01:57.480 --> 00:02:01.719 sacks last year while committing five penalties. Jackson played a 34 00:02:01.719 --> 00:02:05.359 major factor in the way Johnson called the offense last season. 35 00:02:06.000 --> 00:02:09.159 Johnson prioritized running toward the right side because of how 36 00:02:09.199 --> 00:02:12.759 well he and right tackled Darnell Wright were blocking. Jackson 37 00:02:12.800 --> 00:02:15.759 also played a role in Wright's development, who earned his 38 00:02:15.800 --> 00:02:19.719 first second team All Pro honor Despite his sex success 39 00:02:19.800 --> 00:02:23.439 Jackson didn't earn any recognition for a season nationally. Left 40 00:02:23.439 --> 00:02:26.879 guard Joe Thuni and center Drew Dolman earned Pro Bowl honors, 41 00:02:27.240 --> 00:02:31.360 while Thuny and Wright garnered All Pro honors. Looking ahead, 42 00:02:31.919 --> 00:02:34.800 Jackson might fall under the radar once again during training 43 00:02:34.800 --> 00:02:38.400 camp and even next season. Most of the attention starting 44 00:02:38.439 --> 00:02:41.680 next month on the line will be focused elsewhere. The 45 00:02:41.680 --> 00:02:44.039 Bears will have a competition at left tackle for a 46 00:02:44.080 --> 00:02:47.360 second straight training camp, and the team might have a 47 00:02:47.360 --> 00:02:51.639 battle for the starting center spot as well. Other fans 48 00:02:51.639 --> 00:02:54.479 will also be focused on whether the Bears can agree 49 00:02:54.520 --> 00:02:57.240 to a contract extension with Wright before the start of 50 00:02:57.240 --> 00:03:01.159 the season. But Jackson will play an important role on 51 00:03:01.199 --> 00:03:04.080 the line once again, even if he's not the focal point. 52 00:03:04.280 --> 00:03:06.680 Jackson will have to work with a new starting center 53 00:03:06.759 --> 00:03:09.639 and might have to be more of a mentor if 54 00:03:09.719 --> 00:03:13.800 rookie center Logan Jones wins the job. He'll also work 55 00:03:13.879 --> 00:03:15.840 with right in helping the right side of the line 56 00:03:15.840 --> 00:03:19.439 be the team's strength. Once again. Jackson will want to 57 00:03:19.479 --> 00:03:22.560 prove that he can stay healthy. Although he's not flashy 58 00:03:22.599 --> 00:03:25.680 at times, Jackson plays a critical role that fell under 59 00:03:25.680 --> 00:03:29.159 the radar last season. He 65 00:03:44.439 --> 00:03:47.240 played at a Pro Bowl level last year as well. 66 00:03:47.599 --> 00:03:51.479 Certainly didn't get those accolades, but hopefully another year, fully healthy, 67 00:03:51.520 --> 00:03:52.639 he'll get that recognition.
